Turnkey storage-management package used for the NGK battery installation; SCE later reported the separate battery control system inoperable in 2024 because its manufacturer no longer made required parts.

Generating-station automation installed in 2017 to supervise diesel units, microturbines, storage, emissions controls, auxiliaries, and switchgear; SCE later added licensed remote-workstation capability.
A 2012 technical presentation identifies 23 Capstone propane microturbines initially described at 60 kW each; later SCE/NREL records use 65 kW per unit. No newer replacement model was identified.

technicalDetails.detail: SCE described charging the battery overnight so diesel units could remain at the roughly 80% minimum load needed by their emissions-control systems, then discharging during higher daytime demand.

In May 2024 SCE said the battery control system was inoperable because its manufacturer no longer produced replacement parts. SCE distinguished that controls problem from battery end-of-life and had postponed a battery diagnostic until controls could be restored.

SCE issued an all-source solicitation in 2022 after contacting more than 2,000 potential participants. It received only a few bids and reported that none met project requirements; the current page therefore presents propane and solar/storage as alternatives still under evaluation.

NREL modeled a 60% renewable solar-storage-diesel scenario and other repower choices, including an undersea cable. These were feasibility cases and must not be represented as installed Catalina capacity.

SCE is replacing three of six older diesel generators with Tier 4-compliant units as South Coast AQMD Rule 1135 tightens allowable NOx emissions to 45 tons per year in 2027 and 30 tons in 2028. The replacement-unit manufacturer and ratings were not publicly disclosed on the reviewed page.

The most recent sources establish an operating isolated grid, a 2024 fuel transition, diesel replacement, and a storage-control problem. They do not provide a current annual electricity mix, battery availability after May 2024, or a selected long-term repower design.

caterpillar-tucson-proving-ground-microg.summary: Caterpillar's off-grid Tucson Proving Ground main-site microgrid, installed in 2016 with Empire Power Systems, combines 528 kWp DC/500 kW AC of First Solar PV, a 500 kW short-duration lithium-ion/ultracapacitor storage system, and three 410 ekW Cat C15 prime diesel gensets under a Cat Microgrid Master Controller. A separate 2025 gatehouse project added a small 20 kW PV and 24 kW/56.8 kWh Cat XES60 storage microgrid. The two systems are separate subsystems at the same proving ground and their capacities should not be added as if one commissioning phase.
caterpillar-tucson-proving-ground-microg.status: The main-site system has operated since 2016 and remains presented by Caterpillar as an off-grid reference installation. Public main-site sources state expected fuel/runtime reductions but do not disclose verified lifetime savings or a current annual solar percentage. Caterpillar separately documented a February 2025 gatehouse microgrid that was operating with nearly all gatehouse energy supplied by solar and storage and diesel use reduced by about 95%.

Twenty 25 kW AC string inverters, 500 kW AC total; the reviewed source does not name their manufacturer or model.

Coordinates PV, storage, gensets, and integrated switchgear; Cat Connect provides web-based monitoring.
Separate 2025 gatehouse unit rated 24 kW / 56.8 kWh with an onboard microgrid controller.

technicalDetails.detail: Described as one piece of a very big puzzle, the Caterpillar Tucson Proving Ground (TPG) is now enjoying the benefits of solar energy.
The new commercial offering pairs solar energy, with the reliability of Cat gensets and industry-leading energy storage technology.
Now, just a few steps from the roaring generator room that powers TPG, five acres of rolling desert hills are covered in solar panels.
The solar panels, manufactured by First Solar and installed by Empire Cat, are perfectly positioned to capture the abundant Arizona sun, reducing the genset requirements.
To account for these frequent, sudden and unpredictable drops in energy, the microgrid solution uses bidirectional inverters and energy storage in order to balance out the load.
This hybrid solution will reduce the facility’s diesel fuel use and emissions by 33 percent each year.

The controller can shut all three diesels down when daytime PV and storage can carry the site, then restart generation as needed. Caterpillar's publication attributes materially greater savings to its integrated controller than an earlier third-party control approach, but this is a vendor-reported comparison.
At installation Caterpillar forecast about 33% lower diesel use and 25% fewer genset operating hours. The reviewed sources do not present a measured multi-year result against those forecasts.

Caterpillar reported the gatehouse's diesel use falling from more than 500 gallons per month to under 25, a 95% fuel reduction and 97% runtime reduction, with about $6,000 in monthly savings. These figures apply only to the 2025 gatehouse subsystem.

The 20 kW/XES60 gatehouse installation and Caterpillar's 2024 automated mining-truck energy-transfer demonstration are distinct from the 2016 main-site microgrid. Only the gatehouse is included as a later electrical expansion; the mining-truck trial is not counted as microgrid capacity.

chavanga-kola-pennisula.summary: Chavanga's village power station on the remote Tersky Coast is a wind-solar-diesel system commissioned in 2015. A Kola Science Centre technical monograph gives the Chavanga-specific configuration as 50 kW of wind, 32.4 kW of PV, and two 88 kW FG Wilson P110 diesels. The often-repeated 120 kW wind and 75 kW solar figures are totals across the Chavanga, Chapoma, and Tetrino program, not Chavanga alone. Public records show wind-equipment repairs in 2017-2018, diesel replacement work and local line repair through 2025, but no recent measured renewable share.
chavanga-kola-pennisula.status: The Murmansk regional encyclopedia says the wind-solar-diesel station has operated since August 2015. Wind units were sent for repair in October 2017 and returned in November 2018. The Tersky District's 2025 program records diesel-power-plant replacement work in Chavanga as 100% complete by 31 December 2025, after a 2024 low-voltage line repair; it does not disclose replacement genset models or present operating performance.

technicalDetails.detail: Regional sources place operation from August 2015, while a later engineering presentation gives September 2015 commissioning. The difference likely reflects phased handover, so both dates remain labeled rather than silently collapsed.
The five wind units were sent for repair in October 2017 and returned to Chavanga on 1 November 2018. Local reporting also described restoration work on the wind-solar-diesel station in 2018.

The district program records a 4.275 million-ruble 0.4 kV line repair completed in 2024 and 6.501 million rubles of Chavanga diesel-station replacement work completed in 2025. Continued fuel deliveries confirm the system remains diesel-dependent.

The 2015 regional commissioning account said the Tersky Coast hybrid projects provided round-the-clock electricity to the previously constrained settlements. No recent reliability indices, annual generation, diesel savings, or renewable fraction were located for Chavanga specifically.

Published program totals of 75 kW solar and 120 kW wind combine Chavanga, Chapoma, and Tetrino. The village-level Kola Science Centre table is the source for Chavanga's actual 32.4 kW solar and 50 kW wind configuration.

695-2.summary: Bihar's chief minister residence/office complex at 1 Aney Marg in Patna received a building-scale solar and efficiency project initiated in 2012. Bihar Renewable Energy Development Agency's official achievement list records a 100 kWp installation, while contemporary reporting says Larsen & Toubro won the national tender and that the project also replaced lighting and equipment with efficient alternatives and added digital monitoring. Later industry/academic compilations instead list 125 kW and Optimal Power Solutions; those later attributions conflict with the official/contemporary record and do not explain whether 125 kW refers to solar, storage power, or a subsequent scope.
695-2.status: The project was announced and awarded in 2012 and was reported as an operating 100 kW installation by 2014. BREDA still lists the CM House 100 kWp project as an achievement, but no post-2014 generation, maintenance, battery, or outage-performance record was found. The official residence was renamed Lok Sevak Awas and changed occupants in 2026; that administrative change does not establish the current condition of the energy system.

Contemporary reporting describes LED lighting, five-star air conditioners, efficient fan regulators, and more efficient water pumps as part of the residence overhaul; individual equipment makes and models were not disclosed.
The planned system used digital technology for generation/delivery optimization and continuous monitoring, but no controller vendor or product name is publicly identified.

technicalDetails.detail: The 2012 plan covered the chief minister's residence, office, and Janata Durbar/public-meeting facilities at 1 Aney Marg, with the aim of reducing dependence on Patna's unreliable grid.

A 2014 report on BREDA's larger block-office program describes a 100 kW solar plant already installed at the chief minister's official residence, corroborating implementation beyond the 2012 announcement.

Contemporary sources name L&T and 100 kWp. Later tables name Optimal Power Solutions and 125 kW without documenting a retrofit or clearly distinguishing PV from storage power. Both dated records are retained; 125 kW is not substituted for BREDA's official value.

No source located through July 2026 reports current generation, solar fraction, islanding behavior, storage condition, controller status, or subsequent equipment replacement. The site's 2026 renaming and occupancy change are not evidence of the power system's condition.

chuja-island.summary: Chuja's record contains two different projects. A POSCO-led consortium was selected in 2015 to plan a large energy-independent-island conversion using multi-megawatt wind and solar plus storage, but contemporary POSCO and later research sources continued to describe it as site selection, design, or a proposal; no commissioning evidence for that large build was found. A separate 30 kW oscillating-water-column wave-energy microgrid with battery storage was completed at Muk-ri harbor in 2021 and remained under long-term operation and performance verification in the IEA-OES 2025 report.
chuja-island.status: The large POSCO consortium conversion should be treated as selected/planned, not verified built: POSCO ICT was still selecting a generation site in 2016, engineering papers described future conversion, and a 2019 paper still proposed an optimal Chuja microgrid. Separately, KRISO held the Muk-ri wave-energy completion ceremony in November 2021 and began trial operation; IEA-OES reported the 30 kW wave/ESS microgrid in long-term operation and performance verification during 2025.

The detailed design divided 1.6 MW into sixteen 100 kW PV blocks. Manufacturer and inverter model were not disclosed, and the array was not verified as built.
A 12 MWh BESS appears in the large-project engineering model; chemistry, power rating, manufacturer, and commissioning were not established.
30 kW slope-type oscillating-water-column converters incorporated into the existing Muk-ri breakwater; KRISO does not disclose the component model in the reviewed release.

technicalDetails.detail: The Korean government selected a POSCO consortium for Chuja in 2015. POSCO ICT's 2016 update said it was selecting the generation site and actively pursuing development, not that the system was operating.

A 2016 paper expected installation by 2019, a 2017 paper said the island 'will be converted,' and a 2019 peer-reviewed paper still proposed an optimal microgrid. In the absence of a commissioning or operator record, the multi-megawatt POSCO project is not counted as built.

KRISO held a completion ceremony on 11 November 2021 and began joint trial operation with Hydrowave Power and G-Philos after a research program dating to 2016 and technology transfer in September 2021.

The IEA-OES 2025 annual report describes the 30 kW wave-energy system as integrated with a microgrid and ESS and undergoing long-term operation and performance verification. It does not publish annual MWh, availability, or storage capacity.

A 2020 control study designed the Muk-ri wave/ESS subsystem to preserve service to Muk-ri if the rest of Chuja's diesel grid failed. Reported algorithm performance was simulated and should not be presented as observed outage performance.

cobija.summary: Cobija's isolated utility system in Bolivia's Pando department combines a 5.2 MWp-class solar plant, 15.2 MVA of diesel generation, and a 2.2 MW/approximately 1.16 MWh Saft lithium-ion stabilization system under SMA controls. Isotron/Isastur delivered the EPC project using Yingli modules, SMA PV and battery inverters, and an SMA Fuel Save Controller. ENDE Corporación owns the plant and ENDE Guaracachi currently operates it. Current owner and regulator pages confirm continued operation, but no current Cobija-specific annual renewable percentage was published in the sources reviewed.
cobija.status: Operating. SMA records commissioning in December 2014; ENDE inaugurated the second phase in July 2015. ENDE Guaracachi's current plant page identifies ENDE Corporación as owner and itself as operator, while Bolivia's 2024 regulator account still lists Guaracachi operating the Cobija solar plant within the isolated system.

equipment.detail: 17,040 polycrystalline modules rated 300 Wp, wired in 18-module series strings; arithmetic nameplate is 5.112 MWp while SMA describes the plant as 5.2 MWp.
Six central inverters in SMA's current reference, with 4.8 MW aggregate AC output.
Two lithium-ion containers, each 580 kWh and 1.1 MW peak, installed adjacent to the diesel station for fast stabilization.
Four large-scale battery inverters in SMA's system reference; commissioning-era secondary descriptions vary in how they group the inverter units.
Calculates permissible PV injection from real-time grid energy flows and coordinates batteries to smooth PV/load fluctuations while maintaining diesel stability.

equipment.category: SCADA and stabilization balance of plant
equipment.detail: Turnkey scope included SCADA, three transformer units, and a stabilization system reported at 2.3 MVA.

technicalDetails.detail: Li Battery – Saft intensium

The PV field is a few kilometers from the diesel station, while the two battery arrays sit beside the diesels. SMA control limits solar injection and uses fast battery response to manage ramps, load variation, and loss of the single solar interconnection line.

SMA clarified in 2020 that Dynamic Genset Shutdown was not implemented because it was unavailable when the plant was built; full diesel-off operation would require larger PV/storage and grid-forming battery inverters.
SMA dates initial operation to December 2014 after a nine-month delivery effort. ENDE's July 2015 ceremony inaugurated the second phase, explaining why public sources cite both 2014 and 2015.

ENDE Guaracachi's current page identifies two battery-bank arrays and regulation that optimizes PV penetration and diesel savings. Bolivia's 2024 regulator account separately lists Guaracachi as Cobija solar operator, corroborating continuing service.

The 2024 regulator publishes an aggregate mix for all Bolivian isolated systems, not a Cobija-only annual mix. Early design yield, fuel savings, and 2015 PV penetration therefore remain dated rather than being presented as 2024 performance.

coconut-island-dc-microgrid.summary: The built Coconut Island (Moku o Loʻe) DC microgrid is a University of Hawaiʻi Natural Energy Institute research testbed serving selected critical loads in two Hawaiʻi Institute of Marine Biology buildings. The installed system is 6.2 kW of rooftop PV with an 8 kW/8 kWh lithium-ion BESS, DC loads, a custom OPTO22/Node-RED controller, and an AC-grid interface to the island's aging undersea service. It also supported an electric car and a 24-foot solar-electric boat. Earlier 500 kW-class planning references were proposals; HNEI's current reports establish the much smaller configuration that was actually installed and commissioned.
coconut-island-dc-microgrid.status: Installed, commissioned, and performance-tested by 2024-2025. HNEI's December 2025 annual report says the testbed demonstrated islanded critical-load operation, 3.3% better air-conditioner efficiency and 5% lower lighting power in DC mode, but also says the research project was terminated after loss of APRISES funding in May 2025. The hardware was not reported decommissioned, yet no post-November-2025 operating program is documented.

equipment.detail: Custom HNEI control system using Node-RED and JavaScript/Node.js for scheduling and transitions among AC, DC, and hybrid operating modes, with Groov View dashboard monitoring.
Bidirectional inverter used primarily to convert AC utility supply to the 48 V DC bus, paired with two MPPT charge controllers; HNEI's reviewed report does not state its nameplate rating.
Three research converters described at 3 kW each, raising the 48 V PV/BESS bus to 200-350 V for lighting, cooling, refrigeration, and other loads.
AC/DC-capable air conditioner, AC/DC LED lighting, refrigeration, critical plug-load inverter, multipoint metering, contactors, breakers, and data-collection equipment installed across two buildings.
The original 11.2 kWh swappable packs were replaced by four fixed 48 V packs totaling 18.2 kWh, each with its own BMS, plus modern charge controllers and fault-tolerant switching.

technicalDetails.detail: Advanced Communications and Microgrid Control
Small-scale wind turbines
Distributed Solar PV
2 Diesel Generators to backup critical loads

Coconut Island remains connected to Oʻahu through an aging undersea electrical service, while the DC testbed can isolate selected critical loads. The stationary PV/BESS supplies a 48 V bus, DCON converters feed higher-voltage DC loads, and the Schneider inverter links to AC service.
Periodic AC/DC switching found an average 3.3% air-conditioner efficiency improvement and approximately 5% lower LED-lighting power in DC mode. Plug loads were excluded because they remained AC-only.

One-second Modbus polling overloaded the controller and caused freezes. HNEI reduced active connections, changed polling to five-second intervals, and shrank an oversized dashboard icon, eliminating observed freezes and stabilizing monitoring.

The original swappable-battery station suffered BMS/charger conflicts, poor cell balancing, and rising end-of-life failures. It was decommissioned during APRISES19 and the electric car and boat received fixed batteries; this does not mean the building microgrid was decommissioned.

The upgraded 24-foot boat demonstrated initial bidirectional energy flow in laboratory trials. Engineering for a permanent shore connection and full vehicle-to-grid integration began, but the May 2025 funding stop halted that next phase.

HNEI validated islanded critical-load operation and DC efficiency/control benchmarks, then reported project termination following loss of APRISES funding. The latest report does not say the installed testbed was removed, nor does it establish continued routine operation after November 2025.

Historical 500 kW-class planning listings and secondary 200 kW solar/440 kW diesel summaries do not match HNEI's as-built record. The verified installed building testbed is 6.2 kW PV with 8 kW/8 kWh stationary storage; island-wide diesel assets are not part of that testbed rating.

coober-pedy.summary: EDL's Coober Pedy Hybrid Renewable Power Station is a 9.25 MW isolated wind-solar-battery-diesel system supplying the remote South Australian town. The as-built renewable plant uses two 2.05 MW Senvion MM92 wind turbines, 1 MW of First Solar PV, a Toshiba SCiB short-duration battery, two diesel rotary UPS units, and a 3 MW dynamic resistor integrated with the existing eight-engine diesel station under EDL/Hydro Tasmania controls. EDL's 2025 update reports 74% average renewable penetration over operating life, more than 25,000 hours at 100% renewables, and a 116-hour diesel-free record.
coober-pedy.status: Operating since 1 July 2017. EDL's current CY2025 project page lists 9.25 MW total capacity and about 11 GWh annual production. A January 2025 owner update reports nearly nine years of reliable operation, 74% average renewable penetration, 116 continuous diesel-free hours, and 64% lower diesel use and generation-related greenhouse-gas emissions. The separate ARENA grant/knowledge-sharing project closed in December 2024; the power station continues operating.

equipment.detail: EDL reports proprietary integration technology; the reviewed government pages do not identify a controller model.
Two turbines rated 2.05 MW each, 4.1 MW total.
Fixed-tilt 1 MW AC solar plant; ARENA's public account describes approximately 12,000 modules.
1 MW-class central inverter housed in a Sunny Central medium-voltage power station.
High-power lithium-ion BESS reported at 1.5 MW/0.49 MWh in the detailed as-built inventory, used for smoothing and fast system response.
Two 850 kVA units provide rotating inertia, fault response, and dispatchable support during diesel-off operation.
Eight pre-existing reciprocating units at 518 kWe each.
3,000 kW dynamic resistor/heat-bank system absorbs excess renewable power and contributes to fast frequency/energy balancing.
Advanced Hybrid Off-grid Control System coordinating wind, solar, BESS, DUPS, resistor, and diesel generation. EDL ultimately owned the overall detailed control architecture.

technicalDetails.detail: PV, wind, storage
EDL began renewable-integration studies in 2013, signed the ARENA funding agreement in July 2014, began construction in September 2016, and reached commercial operation on 1 July 2017.

EDL reported in January 2025 that the station had exceeded 25,000 cumulative hours at 100% renewables, set a 116-hour continuous diesel-free record, averaged 74% renewable penetration, and reduced diesel use and associated generation emissions by 64%.

In the first half-year of operation the plant saved about one million liters of diesel and operated without diesel generation for more than 1,300 hours, providing a dated baseline for later lifetime results.

The balance-of-plant contractor initially held overall controls responsibility, but EDL took it in-house because hybrid communication protocols, interface points, media, hardware, and physical layouts crossed multiple supplier packages. EDL says single-party visibility reduced schedule and integration risk.

EDL used four major EPC packages and found that including subcontractors in design reviews was essential, particularly around BESS and PV-inverter interfaces. Roughly 17% of site labor hours were local after work packages were divided to match local capability.

The District Council distributes and retails electricity to nearly 1,250 customers over about 115 km of overhead and 13 km of underground lines. Smart meters replaced most customer meters in 2021; EDL owns the generation station, not the council distribution network.

A 2021-22 Technical Regulator audit found no evidence that EDL and the council generally failed to comply with their safety, reliability, maintenance, switching, and contractor-management processes, while still issuing improvement advice.

east-rockaway-bay-park-microgrid.summary: Nassau County's 2016 NY Prize feasibility study evaluated a community microgrid centered on the Bay Park Sewage Treatment Plant and nine other critical facilities in East Rockaway, New York. It compared an advanced-digestion/biogas CHP core with wind, solar, fuel-cell, and legacy tri-fuel generation options; the report did not document a selected construction design. No later NY Prize construction award or commissioned community microgrid was found, and a later Bay Park electrical-upgrade project says the plant's four old 3.6 MW engine-generators were decommissioned after new utility feeders were installed.
east-rockaway-bay-park-microgrid.status: Feasibility study completed in 2016; no construction or commissioning verified through July 2026. East Rockaway does not appear on NYSERDA's published Stage 2 award list, and later Bay Park work replaced the legacy on-site generation role with upgraded utility connections, so the public evidence supports a stopped-after-feasibility outcome rather than an operating microgrid.

equipment.detail: The study evaluated the plant's aging tri-fuel engine-generators as an interim islanding resource. A later H2M project page describes four 3,600 kW units at the site and says they were decommissioned; manufacturer and model were not disclosed in the reviewed sources.
A 2.5 MW reciprocating engine was preferred over microturbines for the digester-gas CHP concept, but no supplier or model was selected in the public report.

technicalDetails.detail: The proposed microgrid would combine natural gas generation with a newly-installed biogas-to-power engine, powered by gas created through waste anaerobic digestion, and combined heat and power (CHP)

The concept placed generation inside the wastewater-plant fence and proposed a new 5 kV community distribution system outside it. Islanded operation was designed not to depend on utility communications, and the preferred generation path was to be black-start capable.
The report's capacity table combines existing resources and mutually exclusive or staged options. Its figures should not be added together as though every resource was contracted for one final plant.

The proposal served public and institutional critical facilities rather than residential customers. Nassau County would own the central source, the Village would be the main customer, and the U.S. Postal Service site would have required a separate participation agreement.
The project received Stage 1 feasibility funding but is absent from the complete published Stage 2 award list. That absence is evidence of no NY Prize Stage 2 award, not a formal cancellation notice.

H2M's later Bay Park Phase E4 work reports a present peak load of about 6 MW, new utility-feeder connections, and decommissioning of four old 3.6 MW engine-generators. The reviewed page does not describe the 2016 community microgrid as having been built.
No commissioning record, as-built one-line, equipment award, operating data, or explicit project-cancellation notice was found through July 2026.

edmondson-village-milligrid.summary: Baltimore Gas and Electric proposed the Edmonson Village public-purpose microgrid in its 2015 Maryland pilot filing. The design was a roughly 3 MW natural-gas system serving a cluster of critical commercial and public loads in Baltimore City. Maryland's Public Service Commission denied BGE's two-site proposal without prejudice in July 2016, so this was never a commissioned microgrid. Later literature's label 'milligrid' describes the proposed multi-node topology, not a built demonstration.
edmondson-village-milligrid.status: Proposal denied by the Maryland Public Service Commission on July 19, 2016; no construction or operation. The denial was without prejudice, but no revived Edmonson/Edmondson Village project was found through July 2026.

technicalDetails.detail: BGE proposed a utility-owned system that would separate from the distribution grid during a significant or extended outage and energize a cluster of critical community services.

The Commission denied the filing as submitted because BGE had not sufficiently established public benefit or site selection, the design relied on a single fuel and lacked diverse distributed resources, costs would be socialized while benefits were geographically concentrated, third-party competition was limited, and projected PJM value was uncertain.

The official order uses 'Edmonson Village,' while the Baltimore neighborhood is commonly written 'Edmondson Village.' A later Asian Development Bank handbook calls the proposal a 'milligrid' because the planned resources and customers sat at multiple points on BGE's grid rather than behind a single point of common coupling.

The reviewed filing describes a community cluster around Edmonson Village, not an operating project at an 'Edmonson High School.' The authoritative regulator record also supersedes later shorthand that may imply the design was tested in 2016: it was denied before construction.
Because the project stopped at the regulatory proposal stage, there are no as-built equipment models, commissioning tests, outage results, or operating energy data.

el-hierro-microgrid.summary: Gorona del Viento is El Hierro's operating wind-pumped-hydro power plant, coupling five Enercon wind turbines with pumping, two reservoirs, four Pelton hydro units, and the island's Llanos Blancos thermal station. It can run the isolated island grid entirely from renewable power for sustained periods, but it has not eliminated diesel annually: Gorona's 2025 report attributes 56% of island supply to the hydro-wind system and 44% to thermal generation.
el-hierro-microgrid.status: Operational. The official operator publishes production through 2025. A proposed 5 MW solar-plus-5 MW battery addition was announced in 2023, but no reviewed source confirmed it as constructed or commissioned by July 2026.

ABB presentation material identifies an ABB power-management system coordinating the islanded hybrid plant; the reviewed public document does not provide a product model.

technicalDetails.detail: When the wind dies down and can’t meet the electricity needs of the community, the water is released and hydropowered turbines supply the needed power.
The stored water acts as a battery.
When demand rises and there is not enough wind power, the water will be released to four hydroelectric turbines with a total capacity of 11 MW.

Wind power can supply the grid directly or drive pumps that move water to the upper reservoir. Stored water is released through Pelton turbines when wind is insufficient; speed governors and flywheels support frequency control while the thermal station remains available for balancing and adequacy.
Red Eléctrica reported 18 consecutive days of 100% renewable supply from January 25 to February 12, 2018. In 2017, renewables supplied 20,234 MWh, or 46.5% of annual demand.

IDAE describes a €79 million project and Endesa dates startup to 2015. Public sources sometimes use 2014 for inauguration and 2015 for regular startup, so 2015 is used here for operating service rather than claiming one universal commissioning date.

Claims that El Hierro is a '100% renewable island' describe the design objective and periods of full renewable operation, not the annual mix. The operator's 2025 accounting still shows 44% thermal supply, and pumping consumption must not be counted as net generation.

No reviewed source confirmed construction, battery vendor, storage energy duration, or commissioning for the 2023 photovoltaic-and-battery proposal by July 2026. Current unit-level details for Llanos Blancos diesel generation were also not established in this review.

experimental-power-grid-centre.summary: Singapore's Experimental Power Grid Centre opened on Jurong Island in 2011 as a configurable 1 MW research microgrid where generation, storage, loads, and grid disturbances could be reproduced without risking the public network. The original A*STAR/ICES facility included diesel generators, solar, multiple storage chemistries, emulators, and Meidensha's MicroGrid Central Controller. It now operates under Nanyang Technological University's Energy Research Institute as the Electrification and Power Grids Centre, with updated storage, power-electronics, motor, simulation, and hardware-in-the-loop platforms.
experimental-power-grid-centre.status: Operational under Nanyang Technological University as the Electrification and Power Grids Centre. The site remains active in 2026 and is hosting a new FutureGrid Accelerator testbed; public sources do not establish which original 2011 assets remain in service unchanged.

Lead-acid, lithium-ion, and two short-duration ultracapacitor systems allowed comparison of energy- and power-oriented storage. Component manufacturers beyond the turnkey integrator were not disclosed in the reviewed paper.

NTU lists energy-storage, large-motor, PV-inverter, controller-hardware-in-the-loop, and real-time digital simulation facilities; its page does not equate these current platforms one-for-one with the original 2011 equipment.

technicalDetails.detail: Storage • Lead-acid batteries • Lithium-ion batteries • Ultra-capacitors unit 1 • Ultra-capacitors unit 2 110 kWh 40 kWh 90 kW 60 kW
PV, Diesel, Flow Batteries, Microturbines, Fuel Cell, EV Charging

Three buses could be operated independently or rearranged in series, parallel, and loop configurations. The emulators let researchers impose voltage, frequency, renewable-output, and load conditions without disturbing the public power system.

The facility officially opened on November 1, 2011. A*STAR described continuing industrial microgrid work in 2018, and the International Energy Agency still listed the 1 MW center as an in-force measure in its June 2025 update.

The acronym EPGC is retained, but the current name is Electrification and Power Grids Centre rather than Experimental Power Grid Centre. It is now presented as an NTU Energy Research Institute capability rather than an A*STAR/ICES facility.

NTU announced the FutureGrid Accelerator at EPGC in 2026 to study high-voltage direct-current architectures, AI/data-center power demand, and next-generation infrastructure with ST Telemedia Global Data Centres.
A*STAR's 2018 article also describes a much larger Sabang Island project. Its 1.4 MWp solar, 1.2 MW diesel, and 2.3 MWh battery ratings belong to Sabang, not to the Jurong Island EPGC test grid.
Current public pages do not give a retirement/retention inventory for the original diesel sets, PV array, batteries, ultracapacitors, or MGCC, so their 2026 service status is explicitly unknown.

f-grid-project-toyota-plant-tohoku-area-.summary: F-Grid Miyagi-Ohira is a factory-centered smart-community energy system in the Second North Sendai Central Industrial Area at Ohira Village, Miyagi Prefecture. Since 2013 it has combined utility purchases, a 7.8 MW city-gas cogeneration plant, solar, reused vehicle batteries, private distribution, heat delivery, and a community energy-management system across participating factories, with an emergency pathway to the Ohira Village disaster-management office.
f-grid-project-toyota-plant-tohoku-area-.status: Operating program. Toyota still publishes a current F-Grid LLP business sheet and profiled continuing F-Grid leadership activity in December 2024. No recent annual generation, fuel-use, emissions, outage, or availability statistics were found through July 2026.

Two charging/storage systems using reused Prius nickel-metal-hydride batteries, rated 30 kW and 20 kW.

CEMS predicts factory energy demand, captures electricity/steam demand and tariff data, and calculates optimal cogeneration output versus utility purchases. The public case does not name a specific Yokogawa product model.

Reused nickel-metal-hydride batteries from Prius vehicles; current aggregate power is 90 kW, but chemistry detail, energy capacity, pack count, and current converter models were not disclosed in the reviewed current sheet.

A 7.8 MW city-gas engine cogeneration plant supplies electricity and recovered steam/hot water. Manufacturer, engine model, thermal rating, and heat-to-power ratio were not found in the reviewed public sources.

technicalDetails.detail: The CEMS coordinates utility electricity, gas cogeneration, solar generation, shared private/self-operated lines, and factory energy-management systems. It also dispatches recovered steam to the vehicle plant and hot water to the greenhouse.

Yokogawa's system forecasts each factory's demand, develops cogeneration and purchase schedules, and created a framework for member factories to participate voluntarily in energy sharing and future demand-response coordination.

Yokogawa reports that the April 2013-March 2015 demonstration reduced costs by 20% compared with the prior baseline. This is a supplier case-study result; no independent measurement protocol was found.
Toyota's community account says recovered heat supports year-round paprika production and reduced the greenhouse's fuel spending to roughly half that of the operator's comparable farms.
The LLP was established February 28, 2013; energy operations began in April 2013; the Yokogawa CEMS completed a two-year field demonstration and was declared fully operational in July 2015.
The project is in Ohira Village, Kurokawa District, Miyagi Prefecture, in the Second North Sendai Central Industrial Area—not Niiza, Saitama.

Toyota's current business sheet and December 2024 personnel profile support continued organizational activity, but no recent annual production, renewable fraction, CHP efficiency, emissions reduction, outage performance, or battery state-of-health data were located.

faial.summary: Faial's isolated Azores grid is a wind-diesel-hydro-solar system operated by EDA. Its best-documented microgrid intervention is ABB Powercorp's RMC 600 control system, commissioned in 2013 to coordinate the island's fuel-oil generation and the 4.25 MW Salão wind farm. Current EDA statistics show that the island remains predominantly thermal: 2023 grid emission totaled 48,384 MWh, including 42,810 MWh from fuel-oil and diesel generation, 5,369 MWh wind, 133 MWh hydro, and 72 MWh photovoltaic sources.
faial.status: The Faial island grid and its wind, thermal, hydro, and photovoltaic resources remain operational. The reviewed current EDA data do not explicitly confirm whether the original ABB RMC 600 controller is still in service in 2026, so the controller's current lifecycle status is unknown.

Five 850 kW turbines at the Salão wind farm
EDA identifies heavy fuel oil as Faial's principal thermal fuel. Public sources reviewed here do not provide a current generator manufacturer/model inventory.

technicalDetails.detail: The RMC 600 calculated economical operating combinations, balanced generation and demand, maximized wind use subject to system limits, and coordinated loading, spinning reserve, and operating constraints for the conventional plant.
ABB announced the RMC 600 as delivered and commissioned on December 4, 2013, coinciding with integration of the expanded Salão wind resource.

ABB's infographic claimed fuel savings above 3.5 million liters per year. The reviewed source does not disclose the measurement baseline or independent verification, so this is retained as an ABB-reported benefit rather than a verified operating result.
EDA's 2023 figures show wind as the largest renewable source but thermal fuel oil still dominating annual production. Faial is therefore an operating hybrid island grid, not a renewable-only system.

The EU Clean Energy for EU Islands page identifies ongoing study of additional renewables and ferry electrification, but does not document a commissioned large battery or replacement controller for Faial.

Current RMC 600 service status, controller software version, present thermal-generator makes/models, installed battery capacity, and independently measured fuel savings were not found in public sources through July 2026.

fairfield-ct-microgrid.summary: Fairfield's public-safety microgrid links the town's police headquarters/emergency communications center, fire headquarters, Operation Hope shelter, and a cell site. Installed in 2015, it combines a 300 kW natural-gas standby generator, a 60 kW natural-gas CHP unit, and 47 kW of solar with Schneider Electric controls so the facilities can separate from the United Illuminating grid during an outage.
fairfield-ct-microgrid.status: Operational municipal microgrid, installed in 2015 and still cited as an operating regional resilience case in 2025. No public post-commissioning outage log or independently measured availability record was found through July 2026.

technicalDetails.detail: New electrical links connected the shelter, police/emergency communications facility, and fire headquarters. Controls permit grid-connected operation and intentional islanding while CHP heat is recovered for useful building service.
The islanded system supports police, fire, emergency communications, an emergency shelter, and a cellular communications site.
Connecticut awarded pilot funding in the first state microgrid round, and regional planning records identify the public-safety system as installed in 2015.

A 2025 Southwest Connecticut climate plan continues to use the project as an installed microgrid case, and Sustainable Fairfield separately lists completed microgrids at the Police Station and wastewater plant.

This record is the police/fire/shelter public-safety microgrid. Fairfield also developed a distinct wastewater-treatment-plant microgrid; capacities and equipment from that project should not be merged into this one.

No reviewed source publishes controller model, generator makes/models, CHP thermal output, measured fuel use, emissions, islanding-event history, reliability, or battery capacity; no battery was identified.

falkland-islands-microgrid.summary: The catalog record refers most accurately to Stanley's isolated electricity system, not one unified Falkland Islands grid. Stanley combines the 1.98 MW Sand Bay wind farm and three flywheel systems with legacy W.H. Allen diesels and three 2 MW Cummins interim sets. The existing hybrid system remains operational while the Falkland Islands Government develops Sand Bay Phase 3, a separate new Stanley power station, storage, and grid upgrades.
falkland-islands-microgrid.status: Existing Stanley wind-diesel-flywheel system operational. Sand Bay Phase 3 is procured/under development but not commissioned: three Enercon E70 turbines and an ABB grid-forming battery package were approved in 2024, and June 2026 reporting says the construction phase was deferred to January 2027 for safer weather conditions. The new Stanley power station is separately targeted for 2028.

Power-management integration intended to replace an aging Windows XP-based control system and coordinate Enercon converters with the ABB battery system.

technicalDetails.detail: Stanley uses wind and flywheels to reduce diesel dispatch on an isolated network. The flywheels damp short lags between load changes and generator response; they are fast-response stabilizers, not documented long-duration energy storage.

The current station generates at 3.3 kV and uses four step-up transformers for 11 kV distribution. The network reaches Stanley Airport, Mount Kent, Pony's Pass Quarry, and the abattoir and includes 96 substations; the new station is planned to generate directly at 11 kV.

The ABB battery's grid-forming inverter is intended to establish voltage and frequency, black-start the island system, and emulate diesel-generator behavior while Enercon turbines operate as grid-following converters. Modeling projected renewable utilization rising from 38% to as much as 81% and captured wind penetration from 21% to as much as 86%; these are forecasts, not operating results.

Executive Council approved the full joint renewable project and sole-source Enercon, ABB, and Innoven elements in March 2024. A February 2025 delivery update said works had commenced with estimated completion in Q2 FY2026/27. June 2026 reporting states that construction at Sand Bay and Mare Harbour was deferred to January 2027 for safer seasonal conditions.

Government planning documents expect Sand Bay Phase 1 turbines to reach planned end of life in 2027 and Phase 2 in 2030. Phase 3 is intended both to raise renewable supply and avoid a return to complete diesel reliance as those units retire.

Stanley/Sand Bay is separate from the Mare Harbour wind farm serving the British military's Mount Pleasant Complex and from small Camp settlement systems. Capacity procured for Mare Harbour must not be added to Stanley's microgrid.

The reviewed public record does not disclose the three existing flywheels' maker, model, power, or energy capacity; final as-delivered Phase 3 battery rating; new power-station engine manufacturer/models; or a recent audited annual wind/diesel generation table.

faroe-islands.summary: The recovered Faroe Islands record corresponds to Nólsoyar Orkufelag's 220 kW stand-alone wind-to-heat microgrid on Nólsoy, not the multi-island SEV electric system. A reused Wind World induction-generator turbine supplies resistive heating loads and was designed to buffer variable wind as hot water. The most detailed operating update found, from June 2020, says the volunteer-run project was still manually controlled, turned only intermittently, and served eight houses rather than the 23-house first phase or roughly 35-house technical potential. No reviewed source supports the recovered Schneider Electric attribution.
faroe-islands.status: Installed in 2012 but only intermittently operating as of the latest detailed local update in June 2020, when eight houses were connected and control remained manual. The Faroese regulator still publishes the project's 2011 permit, and a commercial wind database labeled the turbine operational in May 2026, but neither establishes recent output or confirms that the planned district-heating tank and full automation were completed. A separate 2025 Nólsoy Energy virtual-power-plant pilot uses household solar, heat pumps, and EV batteries; it is not evidence that the older wind-heating system was upgraded.

specifications.value: One Wind World W2500 rated 220 kW, with a 25 m rotor and 30 m tower; three-phase 690 V, 50 Hz squirrel-cage induction generator

specifications.label: Heating reach

specifications.value: Approximately 100 kW gas-oil boiler, with the permit requiring any installation to remain below 120 kW unless reapproved

equipment.detail: A reused early-1990s, stall-regulated Danish turbine with a 220 kW squirrel-cage induction generator, repurposed after roughly two decades of operation in Denmark.

The research configuration used an Arduino-class microcontroller to switch resistive heater banks for frequency control and capacitor banks for reactive power and voltage control. The paper reports improving the adjustment interval from about 260 ms to 62 ms and implementing PID loops; a commercial product model is not named.

Electric immersion heaters convert otherwise variable stand-alone wind output to hot-water heat. The permit contemplated a central tank, district-heating pipes, smaller household tanks, and an oil-fired backup boiler; completion of the full configuration is not verified.

technicalDetails.detail: Thermal Storage
Wind, diesel, storage

The project deliberately operated the turbine as a self-excited, stand-alone electrical microgrid because connecting it to the local utility grid proved difficult. It is an electricity-to-heat system, not Nólsoy's normal electric supply, which remains a separate utility network.

Frequency is regulated by continually matching switched heater load to wind generation; voltage is regulated by switched capacitors supplying the induction generator's magnetizing reactive power. High-wind self-excitation during startup was a documented control challenge.

The permitted design anticipated a 23-home first phase and later public buildings and additional homes. In 2020, the operator said only eight homes were connected, operation was manual and intermittent, technical work remained, and volunteers were completing it in their spare time.

The 85% wind-heat share, 15% curtailment, 15% backup requirement, and 870 MWh/year are model results. No public measured annual production, fuel savings, availability, or post-2020 connection count was located. The May 2026 commercial database status should therefore not be treated as proof of reliable operation.

Nólsoy Energy introduced a community virtual-power-plant pilot in 2025 using household solar, heat pumps, bidirectional EV charging, and grid transactions. The announcement does not connect that pilot to the W2500 wind-heating microgrid, so its assets are not added to this project's capacity.

fire-station-24-charlotte-nc-microgrid.summary: Duke Energy's McAlpine Creek single-customer microgrid islands Charlotte Fire Station 24 using a 50 kW photovoltaic array, a BYD lithium-iron-phosphate battery, utility-owned switching and controls, and the station's pre-existing diesel generator. McAlpine has served as a Duke test site since 2006; storage was installed in late 2012, integration began in 2013, and the microgrid entered service in July 2015. Primary sources disagree on whether the battery power rating is 200 kW/200 kVA or 240 kW, but consistently report 500 kWh of energy. A later 30 kW Eos battery was connected to the same solar array by 2020; its energy capacity and present role are not published.
fire-station-24-charlotte-nc-microgrid.status: Operational pilot in Duke and SEL's latest project-specific descriptions, with the core system in service since July 2015. It completed automatic storm transfers in April and September 2016 and later secondary reporting says it rode through additional outages. Duke's 2020 integrated resource plan is the latest dated primary source found that describes the site's configuration, so current 2026 availability, measured reliability, battery state of health, and whether the added Eos unit remains active are unverified.

specifications.value: BYD lithium-iron-phosphate, consistently reported as 500 kWh; Duke, EPRI, and the 2020 IRP use 200 kW or 200 kVA, while SEL's engineering paper and current case page use 240 kW

equipment.detail: Modular lithium-iron-phosphate system assembled from roughly 1,700 small-format batteries, rated 500 kWh and variously described as 200 kW/200 kVA or 240 kW. The discrepancy likely reflects different nominal and inverter/test ratings, but the sources do not explicitly reconcile it.
The existing inverter firmware was modified so the solar system could operate as a voltage source during an island rather than only follow the utility grid.
Coordinates island detection, source dispatch, synchronization, and transition using DNP3 over Ethernet and hard/fiber signaling including MIRRORED BITS communications.
Controls the isolation recloser and supports both manual and automatic grid-to-island and island-to-grid transitions.
The fire station already had a diesel generator before microgrid integration. Its manufacturer, model, fuel storage, and kW rating were not published in the reviewed primary sources.
A separate 30 kW unit was added to study another storage technology. Public project documentation reviewed does not give its kWh rating or establish that it is part of the automatic island sequence.

technicalDetails.detail: Grid-connected services include frequency regulation, reactive-power and voltage support, peak shaving, demand response, and solar smoothing. During an upstream outage the point of common coupling opens and the battery, solar inverter, and available diesel generation support the station as a local island.

Duke reports the first automatic storm-driven transfer in April 2016, lasting 1 minute 15 seconds before grid return, followed by another successful automatic operation in September 2016. SEL says the system supports both manual and automatic operation; no comprehensive outage log or annual availability was published.

Duke placed the battery, transformers, distributed-energy switch, isolation recloser, controller, and communications on the utility side of the customer meter. This ownership model let the utility maintain the system without placing new operating responsibilities on the fire department.

The recovered legacy figure of 50 kW is the photovoltaic rating, not storage power, and a secondary claim of a 500 kW battery is inconsistent with the technical record. The supported battery values are 500 kWh and 200/240 kW. The 36-hour duration is an estimate; measured energy delivered, fuel savings, renewable share, and present battery health were not found.

flacq.summary: The Flacq record is the grid-connected, behind-the-meter hybrid-energy system at the Flacq Coeur de Ville shopping center and Super U supermarket in Mauritius. DHYBRID's current project page describes 1.2 MWp of PV, 2,000 kVA of diesel generation, electrical storage of undisclosed size, chilled thermal storage, and its Universal Power Platform. Solar is self-consumed rather than exported in the current operating description, while central cooling and refrigeration create ice or cold storage from surplus daytime solar for use at night.
flacq.status: Operating commercial installation. The shopping center opened in June 2015, the official 1.146 MW PV application received a conditional permit in August 2015, and later accounts place formal plant inauguration in 2017. A 2023 environmental-award report still identified Super U Flacq's 1.146 MW PV system in use. DHYBRID continues to publish the installation as a reference project, but no audited post-commissioning production, diesel savings, battery availability, or storage capacity was located.

equipment.detail: Coordinates PV, grid, diesel backup, electrical storage, and the site's chilled thermal storage.
Coordinates PV, utility-grid supply, diesel backup, electrical storage, and chilled thermal storage. Earlier project-development reporting used DHYBRID's older HyGRID controller name.
Excess daytime PV is stored as ice or cold for the central air-conditioning and food-refrigeration systems and used at night; tank size, thermal capacity, and equipment vendor are not disclosed.
DHYBRID lists an energy-storage system and says storage plus diesel can support full-load autonomy, but it does not disclose chemistry, manufacturer, power rating, or energy capacity.
The reviewed public sources disclose counts and aggregate ratings but not PV-module, inverter, or genset manufacturers and models.

technicalDetails.detail: This is a behind-the-meter hybrid at a grid-connected shopping center, not an off-grid village microgrid. The CEB told Parliament that PV would cover only about 30% of site requirements and that the utility would supply the balance and backup.

The July 2014 application sought self-consumption and supply to the grid under the medium-scale distributed-generation/net-metering scheme. DHYBRID's current operating description says all self-generated energy is consumed on site with no export. The page preserves this as a dated application-versus-current-configuration distinction.

The UPP uses the refrigeration and central-air-conditioning plant as a controllable energy sink, shifting solar energy into nighttime cooling via ice/cold storage rather than relying only on electrical batteries.

The 1.146 MW government/commissioning value is the more precise permitted rating; 1.2 MW is a rounded commercial description. Likewise, module and inverter counts differ across planning, opening, inauguration, and current vendor sources and should not be combined as separate equipment additions.

A 2023 report confirms the PV installation remained part of Super U Flacq's operating energy-management program. Public sources do not provide current annual energy mix, measured availability, verified lifetime diesel savings, export data, or electrical/thermal storage ratings.

flores-island-microgrid.summary: Flores is an isolated island power system in the Portuguese Azores, not a single fixed-generation package. In 2024 it combined the 1.5 MW Ribeira de Além Fazenda hydro plant, the end-of-life 600 kW Boca da Vereda wind farm, diesel generation at the Lajes thermal station, and a historically installed ABB PowerStore flywheel. EDA Renováveis reports that hydro supplied 44.2% and wind 1.5% of island electricity in 2024, for a 45.7% renewable share; diesel supplied the remaining approximately 54.3%. The two old 300 kW wind turbines were dismantled for replacement by one 900 kW machine, but the latest March 2026 evidence documents equipment logistics rather than completed commissioning.
flores-island-microgrid.status: Operating isolated utility system, with hydro and diesel in service and wind repowering still unresolved. EDA Renováveis began dismantling the two old wind turbines in late 2024 and originally planned full operation of the 900 kW replacement by year-end 2025. RTP/Lusa reported in March 2026 that transport of the replacement towers, nacelles, and blades had been completed for the three-island repowering program, but did not say the Flores turbine was commissioned. The current availability of the 2005-era ABB flywheel is not disclosed in EDA's 2024 report.

equipment.detail: The remodeled Além Fazenda plant documents two 528 kW horizontal-axis Francis turbines, each coupled to a 640 kVA three-phase synchronous generator. EDA's current portfolio gives 1.5 MW total plant nameplate; the public plant page does not reconcile the additional rating with the two new units.
Two 300 kW direct-drive turbines installed in 2002 and dismantled beginning in late 2024 after reaching end of useful life.
One 900 kW turbine planned for Boca da Vereda. Components were included in the three-island logistics campaign completed by March 2026, but a commissioning notice for Flores was not located.

Fast-response flywheel/inverter system that absorbs or injects real power and supplies voltage/frequency support, allowing wind operation with less need to keep diesel generation online. Current availability was not established.

The isolated grid uses gas-oil-fired generating sets at the Lajes thermal station. Current aggregate nameplate, unit count, manufacturers, and models were not disclosed in the reviewed current sources; the recovered 600 kW diesel figure should not be treated as the present plant total.

technicalDetails.detail: Flywheel
Power Store Distributed Control of generators with inverters in combination with flywheel
The hydro intake, headpond, auxiliaries, and generating groups were fully automated in the 2015 remodel. The plant can operate unattended and is remotely controlled from the Lajes thermal station.

ABB described wind-induced voltage and frequency fluctuations, blackouts, and curtailment before PowerStore. Its fast injection/absorption let the utility smooth wind and at times operate without an online diesel set; this is a historical operating account, not a statement of current availability.

EDA's 2024 report planned new foundations, platforms, underground medium-voltage connections, and switchgear, with full service targeted for the end of 2025. A March 2026 logistics update confirms delivery work, implying schedule slippage or at least incomplete public closeout; it does not establish commercial operation.

EDA documents future fast-response BESS deployment on Flores and a planned Flores PV project, but the reviewed sources do not establish commissioning, capacity, vendor, or model. These are excluded from installed capacity.

The separate Ribeira Grande hydro project on Flores, estimated around 1.2 MW in EDA's 2024 accounts, remained under geological and routing reevaluation. It is not part of current installed hydro capacity.

The 2024 mix is measured and current for that year. Public evidence reviewed does not provide 2025 or 2026 island generation mix, current thermal nameplate, a current PowerStore status, or proof that the 900 kW turbine, BESS, or PV project entered service.

fort-belvoir-dc-microgrid.summary: The legacy Fort Belvoir Direct Coupling DC Microgrid was a small Nextek-led 24 V DC building demonstration, not the later multi-megawatt Area 300 resilience microgrid. A 2022 national-laboratory survey describes the legacy work as a partial fluorescent-lighting retrofit with about 1 kW of DC load, direct-coupled photovoltaic generation, and no reported battery-backed islanding. Nextek's now-unavailable project page reportedly described 16 kW of solar, but that figure could not be independently confirmed from an accessible primary record. Fort Belvoir later completed a separate AC microgrid at Area 300 using 905 kW of fixed natural-gas generation and four 400 kW mobile diesel generators; those assets are documented here as a later project, not silently merged into the DC demonstration.
fort-belvoir-dc-microgrid.status: Historical demonstration with current operation unverified. The accessible national-laboratory survey characterizes the DC installation as a partial demonstration rather than a campus microgrid, and the original vendor page is no longer available. Separately, the Area 300 cybersecure AC microgrid completed a 72-hour demonstration on January 13–15, 2020, but the mobile generators are not permanently stationed there and the final report says full mobilization after an unplanned outage could take roughly 24–36 hours.

equipment.detail: Direct-coupled 24 V DC photovoltaic and lighting demonstration. Accessible records do not disclose converter, panel, luminaire, or protection-device models.
Distributed intelligent power controllers and a human-machine interface coordinated generation, feeder switching, black start, and resynchronization over a cybersecure network.
Generator controls interfaced the three existing natural-gas units with the GridMaster system.
Four 400 kW Army mobile diesel generators operated at 4.16 kV and connected to the 34.5 kV system through a 2 MVA transformer.
Managed the control network integrated into the installation's closed restricted network.
The project added reclosers, disconnects, grounding transformers, bypass contactors, and associated medium-voltage equipment to sectionalize and island the existing distribution system.

technicalDetails.detail: The recovered record's title points to Nextek's small direct-coupled DC project. The 2016–2021 ESTCP effort at Area 300 is a distinct 34.5 kV AC microgrid with different scale, assets, contractors, and purpose. Its capacities are therefore labeled separately rather than used to overwrite the legacy DC project's identity.

A 72-hour demonstration in January 2020 exercised islanding, black start, weekday mission loads, N+1 generation redundancy, and reconnection. Black start was completed in about 90 seconds, beating a less-than-five-minute objective.

The four mobile generators are shared Army assets rather than permanently installed capacity. The final report estimated typical utility coordination at 12–24 hours and complete asset mobilization at 24–36 hours after an unplanned event, so the demonstrated island was not continuously ready in its full test configuration.

The demonstrated generation mix was natural gas plus diesel. The project did not incorporate renewable generation or energy storage, and no fuel-consumption, annual island-hours, or emissions data were published.

The final report groups approximate costs as $500,000 for high-voltage work, $400,000 for low-voltage work, $50,000 for generator use and fuel, and $350,000 for controls, studies, and training. These are demonstration categories, not a verified lifecycle cost.

The legacy Nextek page is preserved only as an archive reference and its 16 kW claim lacks accessible independent verification. No current operating data, renewable fraction, energy savings, or proof of continued use was found for the DC installation. CERDEC tactical research located at Fort Belvoir is not attributed to this project merely because it shares the base.

fort-carson-microgrid.summary: Fort Carson's legacy microgrid record corresponds to Phase 2 of the Smart Power Infrastructure Demonstration for Energy Reliability and Security (SPIDERS), a three-feeder, seven-building cybersecure demonstration completed in 2013–2014. It integrated three existing diesel generators, part of a 2 MW photovoltaic plant, and five bidirectional electric-vehicle chargers. The 72-hour test succeeded, but a 2025 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory handbook, based on a December 3, 2024 installation interview, reports that proprietary controls were not sustained, had become obsolete and unsupported by about 2020, and left the system effectively inoperable or 'bricked.' Fort Carson subsequently installed a separate 4.25 MW/8.5 MWh lithium-ion battery in 2018–2019 and completed a separate 1 MW/10 MWh flow battery in November 2024; neither should be presented as proof that SPIDERS remains operational.
fort-carson-microgrid.status: Legacy SPIDERS microgrid is abandoned and effectively inoperable. It was handed to the installation without a durable operations-and-maintenance or firmware-support path; limited training and proprietary controls made revival too expensive relative to mission priority. Separate later assets remain: the lithium-ion demand-management BESS was implemented in January 2019, and the GridStar Flow long-duration demonstration finished construction in November 2024 and entered an 18-month test program. No public 2025–2026 operating results were located for the flow pilot.

equipment.detail: Distributed intelligent power controllers, master control, HMI/GUI, and supervisory cybersecurity functions; the Fort Carson report does not give a product model.
Five high-capacity bidirectional chargers used the EV fleet as controllable generation and storage resources.

equipment.manufacturer: Smith Electric Vehicles / Boulder Electric Vehicle
equipment.detail: Three Smith and two Boulder vehicles participated in the demonstration; individual battery-pack models are not reported.
Motor-operated sectionalizing switches, generator bypass breakers, automatic synchronizers, and a neutral-deriving transformer enabled island formation; manufacturers are not consistently disclosed.

Distributed intelligent controllers, master controller, and human-machine interface coordinated generators, feeder switching, photovoltaic production, and vehicle-to-grid assets. Proprietary firmware and discontinued vendor support later became the principal reason the system could not be economically restored.
Five bidirectional chargers interfaced three Smith Electric trucks and two Boulder Electric Vehicles vehicles; the report does not publish per-charger kW or usable vehicle-battery energy.

High-voltage switchgear, automatic-transfer-switch bypass breakers, synchronizers, motorized switches, and network-distribution-transfer equipment enabled the three-feeder island and grid reconnection. Final device manufacturers and models are not comprehensively identified.

A containerized 4.25 MW/8.5 MWh installation, described by USACE as more than twelve battery containers, used for peak-demand reduction and energy-cost management rather than as a continuation of SPIDERS.
Artificial-intelligence and predictive-dispatch software optimizes the battery around installation load and tariff peaks.
A 1 MW/10 MWh aqueous redox-flow battery installed as the Department of Defense's first megawatt-scale long-duration field demonstration of this technology.

technicalDetails.detail: The microgrid formed in roughly ten minutes; PV inverters required an additional five to ten minutes before reconnecting to the islanded bus.
IPERC's distributed controllers coordinated local assets under a supervisory master controller instead of relying on one central field I/O point.
The design connected 1 MW of the 2 MW PV field, but the final demonstration had only 500 kW available; the page keeps design and test conditions separate.

The island was formed in roughly ten minutes; photovoltaic generation was brought in about five to ten minutes later, and a 72-hour exercise successfully demonstrated cybersecure islanding, multiple generation sources, N+1 operation, and reconnection.

The 2025 PNNL handbook says the demonstration was transferred without adequate sustainment funding, firmware updates, documentation, or operator training. By about 2020 the proprietary controls were obsolete and no longer vendor-supported; the installation described the system as effectively bricked and chose not to revive it.

DOE's 2014 SPIDERS report treats the 2 MW photovoltaic plant as an existing resource during the 2013–2014 demonstration, while PNNL's 2025 retrospective lists the 2 MW system as commissioned in 2016. The sources are not reconciled, so the discrepancy is retained rather than assigning a false single commissioning date.

The roughly $8 million system was delivered through an energy-savings performance contract with no up-front installation cost to the base. Project sources forecast about a 9% peak-demand reduction and roughly $500,000–$525,000 per year of savings under a 20-year guarantee; these are projections, not independently audited realized savings.

The GridStar Flow system is intended to charge from Fort Carson solar, reduce peaks, and provide limited-facility backup for as long as ten hours. ERDC began an 18-month operational test after November 2024 construction completion; no test results, availability, round-trip efficiency, or delivered backup events were found.

An April 2023 base-wide black-start exercise covering 93 facilities was a separate readiness event and is not evidence that the SPIDERS controller was restored. Likewise, Fort Carson's reported 12% renewable-electricity share in fiscal 2023 is installation-wide, not the energy mix of the abandoned microgrid.

SPIDERS sources do not publish measured diesel-fuel savings or renewable percentage, so generic expectations such as 30% fuel reduction are not treated as project performance. Later storage capacity is labeled separately and must not be added to the legacy SPIDERS configuration.

fort-collins-microgrid.summary: The Fort Collins entry is the municipal FortZED Renewable and Distributed Systems Integration demonstration, not a military installation and not one co-located generator plant. From 2008 through 2013, Fort Collins Utilities aggregated PV, methane and natural-gas CHP, conventional generators, microturbines, fuel cells, thermal storage, controllable loads, and vehicle-to-grid assets at roughly five participant locations on two distribution feeders. A second phase intentionally islanded a smaller microgrid linking Colorado State University's Engines and Energy Conversion Laboratory with the Northside Aztlan Community Center.
fort-collins-microgrid.status: Demonstration completed and no longer operating as an integrated project. City material says Phase I achieved a 20–30% peak-demand reduction on the two feeders and Phase II demonstrated the EECL–Northside Aztlan microgrid with cybersecurity safeguards. A 2020 NREL retrospective says the project was operational in 2012 but was disbanded in 2017 because of regulatory factors, historic distribution practices, and the natural end of the roughly ten-year collaboration. Individual participant assets may have continued independently, but the central FortZED dispatch system should not be represented as current.

These laboratory devices supported integration testing and should not be described as renewable generation deployed across the city.
Two city-fleet PHEVs were listed at 10 kW combined in the early design. Public closeout sources reviewed do not give battery size or verified delivered V2G energy.

Eaton supplied or developed switchgear/power components and Woodward contributed power management and mixed-fuel controls across project sites; individual final device models are not consistently disclosed.

technicalDetails.detail: thermal storage
solar PV,
Solar CHP,

Phase I coordinated assets at municipal facilities, New Belgium, CSU, Larimer County, and the InteGrid laboratory over two utility feeders. The aggregate resource figure therefore combines generation, storage-like thermal loads, and demand response owned by several parties rather than describing one fenced microgrid plant.

The later phase demonstrated an intentionally islanded microgrid comprising CSU's Engines and Energy Conversion Laboratory and the Northside Aztlan Center, including advanced cybersecurity safeguards for control communications.

DOE lists PV, microturbines, dual-fuel CHP using process methane, reciprocating engines, backup generators, a wind simulator, V2G plug-in hybrids, fuel cells, HVAC/process load shedding, thermal storage, and efficiency upgrades. The mix was designed to test dispatch and verification, not maximize renewable-energy percentage.

The project met its peak-reduction demonstration objective and advanced multi-building control, but did not achieve the original vision of a net-zero-energy district before the collaboration ended. NREL identifies stakeholder cooperation, regulatory structure, and existing utility practices as important lessons.

The detailed equipment list is a 2008 design snapshot, while later DOE, city, and NREL sources validate aggregate scale and outcomes. No current integrated FortZED controller operation or comprehensive inventory of surviving participant equipment was located; legacy solar, fuel-cell, gas/diesel, and thermal figures should not be presented as a current energy mix.

fort-devens.summary: The legacy Fort Devens microgrid record is best supported as a 2009–2010 net-zero and secure-energy planning effort, not a commissioned permanent microgrid. The Army Reserve asked FEMP and Pacific Northwest National Laboratory to assess net-zero electric and thermal operation, while Sandia developed a strategy for critical-load safe havens lasting at least two weeks. Because the three priority load clusters were nonadjacent, the study concluded that as many as three microgrids might be required. It screened photovoltaic, geothermal, hydro, natural-gas generation, batteries, and propane backup, but did not select final capacities or vendors. A separate 2012–2013 Smart and Green Energy (SAGE) tactical base-camp demonstration at Fort Devens did operate three 100 kW Caterpillar generators, 12.9 kW of PV, and roughly 250 kWh of AGM battery storage; that portable equipment moved to Fort Leonard Wood after testing and was not the permanent net-zero installation envisioned in 2010.
fort-devens.status: Planning/concept project with realization unverified. The 2010 PNNL report documents assessment, site barriers, and candidate technologies rather than construction or commissioning, and current Fort Devens public-works and sustainability materials reviewed do not establish that the proposed installation-scale microgrid was built. The separate SAGE base-camp demonstration ran from January 2012 through March 2013, completed stable field trials and fuel-reduction testing, and then transferred its equipment to the Contingency Base Integration Technology Evaluation Center at Fort Leonard Wood.
