specifications.label: Legacy resilience objective
specifications.value: Critical-base-operation safe havens capable of operating for at least two weeks during a utility outage, alongside a longer-term net-zero electric and thermal goal

specifications.label: Legacy prospective topology
specifications.value: Three nonadjacent priority clusters—Maloney, Ear Muffs, and Barnum—making one contiguous island impractical and potentially requiring three microgrids

specifications.label: Legacy screened resources
specifications.value: Rooftop, parking-canopy, ground, and landfill photovoltaic generation; geothermal heat pumps; waste heat; nearby hydro; natural-gas fuel cells or microturbines; flow or conventional batteries; and propane backup—all planning candidates, not a final installed mix

specifications.label: Adjacent hydro opportunity
specifications.value: A privatized nearby hydro plant was estimated to provide about 250 kW of firm capacity with a possible 400 kW expansion; off-site procurement and wheeling barriers meant it was not established as a Fort Devens microgrid asset

specifications.label: Separate SAGE prime generation
specifications.value: Three Caterpillar XQ100 100 kW diesel/JP-8 generator sets, with an optional 60 kW MEP-806A tactical quiet generator

specifications.label: Separate SAGE photovoltaic capacity
specifications.value: Two Sharp photovoltaic arrays totaling 12.9 kW with Enphase microinverters

specifications.label: Separate SAGE storage
specifications.value: Approximately 250 kWh modeled usable capacity through a Caterpillar BDI-250 250 kW bidirectional inverter; designed for 100 kW for two hours or 250 kW for 20 minutes and laboratory-tested for 2 hours 14 minutes at 100 kW and 26 minutes at 250 kW

equipment.detail: The 2010 assessment considered photovoltaic arrays, geothermal wells and heat pumps, waste-heat recovery, hydro purchases, natural-gas fuel cells or microturbines, conventional or flow batteries, and propane backup. No final manufacturers, models, ratings, or procurement decisions were documented, so these are not treated as installed equipment.
Three 100 kW mobile diesel/JP-8 units were coordinated as a right-sized microgrid instead of the baseline configuration of six independently operated 60 kW tactical generators.
Mobile 208/120 V, three-phase distribution connected the generators, storage, photovoltaic generation, and base-camp loads.
A 250 kW bidirectional inverter charged and discharged the battery banks and supported silent or reduced-generator operation.

Four banks of 384 valve-regulated lead-acid AGM cells provided the demonstration's electrochemical storage. The report models approximately 250 kWh of capacity but does not present a simple manufacturer-rated nameplate kWh total.
Two Sharp module arrays with Enphase microinverters supplied 12.9 kW total.

Supervisory software dispatched generators and storage in response to load and resource conditions. The detailed report does not identify a separate commercial manufacturer for the DSOM implementation.

technicalDetails.detail: The 2010 work identified resource options, critical-load clusters, and regulatory and ownership barriers. It did not produce an as-built design. Some rooftop photovoltaic projects and geothermal wells already had appropriations, but their installed capacity and integration into a microgrid were not established in the reviewed report.

After the 1996 base realignment, the Army retained roughly 4,800 acres in noncontiguous areas while MassDevelopment operated the surrounding municipal utility and Wellesley operated distribution. State-law, interconnection, and power-wheeling constraints complicated use of off-site renewable resources across the installation.

The Base Camp Integration Laboratory opened in June 2011 with twin 150-soldier camps. SAGE testing ran from January 2012 through March 2013, comparing the optimized microgrid, photovoltaics, storage, and efficiency measures against six 60 kW tactical generators.

The field report found stable operation and fuel reductions up to 34% for the microgrid controls alone, 20.6% in one reported phase, 30.1% for a modeled/scaled combination of microgrid plus 52 kW of PV and storage, and as much as 54% when all tested technologies were combined. Photovoltaics supplied as much as 12% of daily energy. These figures refer to different test configurations and should not be collapsed into one annual result.

After the Fort Devens demonstration, the portable system was moved to Fort Leonard Wood's Contingency Base Integration Technology Evaluation Center for continued testing. It therefore should not be counted as permanent Fort Devens generation or storage.

No commissioning record, as-built capacity, vendor inventory, measured energy mix, or current operating data was found for the 2009–2010 installation-scale concept. A separate CERDEC claim of 37% fuel savings and the SAGE results may reflect different base-camp configurations and are not assigned to the permanent concept.

fort-hunter-liggett.summary: Fort Hunter Liggett's installation microgrid is a multi-phase U.S. Army Reserve resilience project that integrates legacy solar, new photovoltaic generation, battery storage, distribution automation, and supervisory controls. The final Ameresco scope added 3.75 MW of PV and a 2.5 MW/5 MWh battery to earlier arrays, rather than the 5 MW PV and 3 MWh storage described in preliminary plans. The Army activated the renewables-only, fully islandable system in September 2024 and says it can sustain prioritized loads for up to 14 days during a grid outage.
fort-hunter-liggett.status: Operational. Construction broke ground in May 2021 and the Army held the activation ribbon cutting on September 19, 2024. The Army Reserve's 2024 annual report calls the $21.8 million system completed and says it can meet about 90% of installation energy needs; the Army separately describes annual net-zero capability and up to 14 days of outage support. These metrics describe different boundaries and should not be read as proof that every load is continuously off-grid. A December 2024 Army cyber-resilience exercise assessed the commissioned system, while the Army said further battery expansion and upgrades to older solar arrays remain possible future work.
fort-hunter-liggett.historicalLocation: Fort Hunter Liggett, Monterey County, CA, United States

specifications.value: 8000 kWh

specifications.label: New photovoltaic capacity in final Ameresco scope
specifications.value: 3.75 MW, integrated with smaller preexisting arrays; an April 2019 award announcement described a preliminary 5 MW addition, so 5 MW should not be treated as the final as-built increment

specifications.label: Battery energy-storage system in final scope
specifications.value: 2.5 MW / 5 MWh. Earlier Army planning described 3 MWh, and a 2015 controller demonstration used an existing 1 MWh system; those are different phases rather than interchangeable as-built figures.

specifications.label: Installation solar portfolio context
specifications.value: The Army's 2019 award history described a multi-phase 8 MW solar program: an initial phase had installed 3 MW of PV plus 1 MWh of storage, followed by the later microgrid phase. Public sources reviewed do not provide one reconciled post-commissioning nameplate total for every surviving array.

specifications.label: Resilience duration
specifications.value: Army-reported ability to support the installation for up to 14 days during a utility outage by islanding and prioritizing loads; this is not a claim of 14 days at unrestricted full-installation load

specifications.label: Renewable-energy share before final commissioning
specifications.value: 21.97% of installation electricity in FY2020 and 23.2% in FY2021, before the final microgrid was activated

specifications.label: Project value
specifications.value: $21.6 million contract award in 2019; the Army Reserve's completed-project account rounds total value to $21.8 million

equipment.detail: Ameresco installed 3.75 MW of new PV and integrated it with legacy arrays. Module and inverter manufacturers and models were not disclosed in the reviewed public sources.
The completed expansion uses a 2.5 MW/5 MWh battery system. Public Army and contractor sources reviewed do not identify cell chemistry, battery manufacturer, or power-conversion model.

The project added a microgrid control system, automated medium-voltage switches, and distribution-system upgrades so prioritized loads and generation can be isolated and coordinated. Specific control-platform and switchgear models were not published.

The earlier demonstration coupled Siemens SCADA with Berkeley Lab's Operations DER-CAM optimizer for forecasting, day-ahead battery scheduling, 15-minute reoptimization, and one-minute balancing. It should not be assumed to be the same controller used in the 2024 Ameresco build.

technicalDetails.detail: The 2024 system is described by the Army as fully islandable and powered only by renewable generation and storage when isolated. Its controller prioritizes loads and manages power distribution during an outage.

The 2021 Army account explained that annual net-zero energy does not mean continuous physical independence: seasonal solar surpluses and deficits still require imports and exports, battery capacity is insufficient to store every surplus kilowatt-hour, and PG&E export constraints affect dispatch. The later 90% independent-needs and 14-day resilience statements use still different operating boundaries.

Public descriptions changed as the design matured: FY2016 planning called for 5 MW PV plus 3 MWh storage; the April 2019 award still said 5 MW; the 2021 contractor and 2022 federal award descriptions give the more specific final addition of 3.75 MW PV and 2.5 MW/5 MWh storage.

In July 2015, Berkeley Lab and Tri-Technic deployed a controller against a then-existing 2 MW PV/1 MWh storage system. It forecast load and PV, optimized a day-ahead battery schedule, reoptimized every 15 minutes, and balanced at one-minute intervals. The final report called for further performance collection, so it is evidence of deployment, not a long-term savings result.

In December 2024, an Army Reserve 'Hack the Army Microgrid' exercise brought 11 vetted researchers to assess battery storage, solar integration controllers, radio-frequency exposure, and network monitoring. The Army presented the event as a model for cyber commissioning and resilience testing, not as evidence of a known compromise.

No independently audited post-activation energy production, outage event, battery round-trip efficiency, state-of-health, renewable fraction, or realized annual savings through July 2026 was located. The Army also identified future battery expansion and older-array upgrades as possible next phases.

fort-severn.summary: Fort Severn First Nation's community-owned solar hybrid in Ontario's far north was completed in 2021. Current Canadian and Ontario sources consistently describe approximately 300 kW of solar integrated with the Hydro One Remote Communities diesel grid, while a federal project description identifies the planned resource breakdown as 240 kW ground-mount PV, 10 kW rooftop PV, 10 kW wind, battery storage, and a microgrid-control computer. The often-repeated 400,000 litres per year figure is an expected diesel reduction, not a published measured result.
fort-severn.status: Completed and operational since 2021 according to the Canada Energy Regulator and Ontario's current remote-community project inventory. Fort Severn First Nation owns the project and Hydro One Remote Communities operates the local diesel grid. A federal contribution record was administratively extended through December 31, 2028, but that grant end date does not overturn the cited 2021 completion date. No post-commissioning measured generation, battery performance, availability, or realized fuel reduction through July 2026 was located.

equipment.detail: Current project evidence identifies ground-mounted and rooftop PV totaling approximately 250 kW in the funded component breakdown, while program-level completed-capacity summaries round the hybrid's solar capacity to 290-300 kW. Module and inverter vendors and models are not publicly identified in the sources reviewed.
A 10 kW wind turbine is included in current NRCan project descriptions; manufacturer and model are not disclosed.

NRCan confirms that battery storage is part of the hybrid, but no reviewed completion source provides an as-built kW or kWh rating, chemistry, supplier, or model. The recovered 300 kW storage figure is therefore unsupported; 410 kWh belongs only to the earlier 2016 design description.

The funded project includes a control computer that coordinates renewable generation, storage, and the remote diesel system. Trimark was contracted for SCADA communications in an earlier phase, but the reviewed sources do not prove that Trimark hardware is in the final installation.

technicalDetails.detail: The system is a community-owned renewable hybrid connected to Hydro One Remote Communities' isolated diesel grid, not a grid-tied export project connected to Ontario's bulk power system.

The CER and Ontario say the project was completed in 2021. Canada's public grant record shows the funding agreement, originally beginning in July 2019, amended through December 2028. That extension may cover administration, monitoring, or follow-on obligations and is not evidence that construction remains incomplete.

The 2016 Canadian Solar/Trimark description and the later NRCan project schedule differ materially in PV and battery ratings. They are retained as dated design phases; the 410 kWh battery and 337 kW combined old-plus-new PV should not be silently assigned to the completed 2021 system.
Federal and engineering accounts say the project included local skills development and training so community members could participate in construction and ongoing operation.

The widely cited 20% and 400,000-litre figures are forecasts. No public measured annual renewable energy, actual diesel or emissions reduction, battery dispatch data, outage performance, or post-2021 availability was located through July 2026.

fort-sill.summary: Fort Sill has two separate microgrid histories that should not be combined. Eaton's 2013 full-scale research microgrid demonstrated seamless islanding with 480 kW of natural-gas generation, a 400 kW/56 kWh lithium-ion battery, 20 kW PV, and a 2.5 kW wind turbine. A much larger installation-resilience project now in federal planning would add 10.3-18 MW of natural-gas reciprocating generation, optional battery storage, controls, and islanding infrastructure, with a separate planned 10-12 MW-class solar plant. The later capacities are not upgrades proven to have been built.
fort-sill.status: The Eaton research demonstration successfully operated in 2013, but its current physical status and continued use were not verified. The newer Energy Resilience and Conservation Investment Program project remained pre-solicitation as of a March 16, 2026 virtual industry day: official 2025 market research described a 10.3 MW base natural-gas plant with an option to 18 MW and optional 2 MW/2 MWh battery increments. A March 2025 draft environmental assessment separately evaluated up to 12 MW of PV and 4 MWh of batteries. No reviewed source establishes a construction award, notice to proceed, or commissioning for the new installation-wide system.

specifications.label: Storage
specifications.value: 400 kW, 56 kWh lithium ion (Li-ion)

specifications.label: 2013 demonstration natural-gas generation
specifications.value: Two 240 kW generator sets, 480 kW total

specifications.label: 2013 demonstration battery system
specifications.value: 400 kW / 56 kWh lithium-ion battery connected through a 500 kVA inverter

specifications.label: 2013 demonstration renewable generation
specifications.value: 20 kW solar PV and one 2.5 kW wind turbine

specifications.label: New ERCIP natural-gas generation under 2025-2026 planning
specifications.value: 10.3 MW base plant, with an option to increase total generation to 18 MW using two nominal 9 MW reciprocating internal-combustion-engine generators

specifications.label: New ERCIP battery options under 2025 planning
specifications.value: Two separately priced options of 2 MW / 2 MWh each, for up to 4 MW / 4 MWh if both were exercised; the draft environmental assessment described 4-8 containers of 0.5-1 MWh each. These are proposed options, not installed equipment.

specifications.label: Planned solar facility
specifications.value: Army announcement: 10.9 MW on 66 acres. PSO's draft RFP allowed 10-14 MW and originally targeted December 31, 2024. The March 2025 environmental review instead evaluates up to 12 MW with approximately 40,005 fixed-tilt panels and up to 28,000 MWh/year. None of these planning figures proves the plant was built.

specifications.label: New ERCIP resilience objective
specifications.value: Fourteen days of mission-critical energy resilience using on-site natural-gas generation, isolation, and load shedding; estimated procurement range $25 million-$100 million and planned construction duration 720 days in the 2025 sources-sought notice

equipment.detail: Two 240 kW generators, each with a commercial isochronous controller. The detailed paper does not identify genset manufacturer or model.

A 500 kVA inverter coupled to a 400 kW/56 kWh lithium-ion battery provides fast transition support, load following, and import/export control. Cell and pack supplier and model are not disclosed in the reviewed sources.
A 1,200 A, 480 V static switch isolates the test microgrid from the utility and supports both planned and unplanned islanding.

The full-scale test bed included a 360 hp wye-delta-start induction motor, a 200 hp rectifier-fed variable-frequency drive, smaller motors, and lighting loads, deliberately representing highly dynamic and nonlinear conditions.

equipment.category: Proposed ERCIP generation and balance of plant
equipment.detail: The 2025 concept uses natural-gas reciprocating generators with compressed-air starting, paralleling switchgear, transformers, protective relays, a load bank, medium-voltage breakers, SCADA, reclosers, microgrid controls, and a new six-inch gas connection. Vendors and models have not been selected publicly.

technicalDetails.detail: The microgrid is composed of two natural gas generators, a battery energy storage inverter, renewables, and a static switch.
The integration of the energy storage inverter with a commercial isochronous generator control system is described.
A comparison of droop control vs. isochronous control is provided, and the benefits of using an existing isochronous generator control system for microgrid applications are described.

During an unplanned outage, the inverter immediately carried the islanded load while the natural-gas generators started and synchronized. The controller then shared power among the inverter and gensets; the static switch enabled later resynchronization and reconnection to the utility.

Isochronous controllers were attached to each generator, the inverter, and static switch over dual-redundant Ethernet, with a battery-management-system interface. Demonstrated functions included peak shaving, import/export control, voltage and frequency regulation, and intentional and unintentional islanding.

Starting the 360 hp induction motor produced more than 2,700 A of inrush. Successful islanded starting required both natural-gas generators and inverter support, while the variable-frequency-drive load was throttled to preserve headroom.

The ESTCP project reported that a power-optimized storage configuration met IEEE 1547 performance limits and occupied 13% of the volume and cost 33% as much as the energy-optimized alternative evaluated. This is a demonstration design comparison, not a current battery-price benchmark.

The future system is intended to black-start, separate from the PSO grid, shed noncritical loads, coordinate proposed solar and storage, and sustain prioritized facilities. Its primary firm-energy resource is natural gas, making it architecturally and operationally distinct from Eaton's small 2013 research microgrid.

The planned 10.9-12 MW solar array, 10.3-18 MW ERCIP generators, and optional 4 MWh storage must not be added to the 2013 demonstration's 480 kW/56 kWh capacities. Through the March 2026 industry day, the newer project remained in acquisition planning rather than documented construction.

gapa-island.summary: Gapa Island is Gapado (가파도), Jeju's early Carbon Free Island demonstration. The original 2011-2016 system combined 500 kW of wind, roughly 141-174 kW of rooftop PV, three 150 kW diesel generators, and staged battery/PCS installations reported between 2 and 3.864 MWh. It demonstrated multi-day renewable-only operation, but Korean government and National Assembly material now describes the original project as unsuccessful because of maintenance, parts-supply, and owner/operator coordination failures. A new Gapado RE100 project is being designed to replace it with grid-forming controls, much larger storage, and new renewables.
gapa-island.status: Original system no longer functioning as intended. In 2025-2026, Korean government sources acknowledged that the first Carbon Free Island effort had failed or stopped operating properly and that Gapado was again supplied primarily by 1.2 MW of diesel generation plus household solar. A replacement project was still in design in March 2026, with renewable supplier selection anticipated as early as November 2026 and completion targeted for 2027. The latest report gives a revised 2026 budget of KRW 8 billion and total cost of about KRW 14 billion, which differs from the National Assembly's earlier KRW 22 billion one-year budget-plan description.

organizations.role: Supplier/integrator associated with the original project's ESS, power-conversion, and microgrid systems. Its published 1 MW/2.3 MWh and 3.25 MW/3 MWh references describe vendor scope or different stages, not one reconciled total asset rating.

specifications.label: Original wind generation
specifications.value: Two 250 kW Siva induction-generator turbines, 500 kW total, commissioned in September 2012

specifications.label: Original rooftop solar capacity
specifications.value: Sources report approximately 141-142 kW for 47-48 three-kW systems, 144 kW in another table in the same field paper, and 174 kW across 48-50 households in public project accounts. These are preserved as scope/date differences rather than forced into one value.

specifications.label: Original diesel generation
specifications.value: Three 150 kW generator sets, 450 kW total, with research accounts commonly describing two operating and one reserve

specifications.label: Original battery and PCS configuration
specifications.value: Final-stage field account: 3 MWh plus 864 kWh of batteries, served by three 1 MW PCS units plus one 250 kW PCS. Other studies simplify the system to 1 MVA/2 MWh or 3 MWh, while a 2020 review identifies 3.0 MWh lithium-ion plus about 0.85 MWh lead-acid. These appear to reflect stages and study boundaries.

specifications.label: Original demand
specifications.value: Research sources report average demand around 119-120 kW and maximum demand around 230-300 kW

specifications.label: Replacement wind and storage plan
specifications.value: National Assembly budget review: 2.3-4.2 MW wind and 20 MWh ESS, plus 249 kW household solar. March 2026 reporting confirms a grid-forming, storage-heavy design but does not yet establish awarded or as-built ratings.

specifications.label: Current island demand and diesel supply
specifications.value: March 2026 report: average demand 227 kW, 2025 electricity use 1.99 GWh, and 1.2 MW of diesel generation providing the primary supply alongside household solar

equipment.detail: Two 250 kW induction-generator turbines commissioned in September 2012; the official wind inventory does not provide a model number.

equipment.category: Original energy storage and power conversion
equipment.detail: Hyosung identifies Gapado as an ESS/microgrid reference and describes its role in microgrid development. Field literature reports multiple PCS and battery blocks added through 2016; public sources reviewed do not fully reconcile vendor, chemistry, or capacity by block.
Three 150 kW sets provided backup and restoration when battery state of charge was low. Manufacturer and model were not disclosed.

The replacement is explicitly planned around grid-forming technology, a large ESS, wind, household PV, and heat pumps. Procurement was not complete by March 2026, so no manufacturer, model, or final rating is assigned.

technicalDetails.detail: Battery PCS operated in constant-voltage/constant-frequency mode to form the island grid. Diesel generation reconnected at low state of charge, while wind output could be curtailed at high state of charge; the staged PCS blocks supported both grid formation and renewable smoothing.

A 2020 technical review reported periods of up to seven consecutive days supplied only by renewables and storage by 2018. This was a best operating interval, not proof of year-round diesel-free service.

Research found that the two concentrated squirrel-cage induction wind turbines could create high inrush and voltage flicker when connected, placing unusual demands on ESS voltage support. A proposed medium-voltage DC link to Marado was a simulation/research concept, not a deployed asset established by the paper.

Korean government and National Assembly accounts attribute the original project's poor outcome to inadequate maintenance funding, difficulty sourcing replacement parts, and divided ownership and operational responsibility. The National Assembly cites a 2011-2016 project period and says the system ultimately stopped operating properly.

The government says the new project is meant to address the earlier failure through adequate ESS and backup capacity, an explicit operating-and-maintenance budget, clearer responsibility, household heat pumps, and modern grid-forming controls.

The National Assembly's late-2025 review described a one-year 2026 KRW 22 billion plan, including KRW 9 billion for wind and KRW 11 billion for ESS through KEPCO. March 2026 reporting instead said design and supplier selection would extend into 2026-2027 and gave KRW 8 billion for 2026 and about KRW 14 billion total, indicating a revised scope or budget.

gasa-island-microgrid-in-southern-korea.summary: This recovered slug and `gasado-island` refer to the same KEPCO microgrid on Gasa-do/Gasado (가사도), Jindo County; their capacities must not be counted twice. The as-built academic record gives 314 kW of PV, four 100 kW wind turbines, three 100 kW diesel generators, and a 3 MWh lithium-ion BESS with 1.25 MVA of conversion capacity. Vendor literature sometimes rounds PV to 320 kW and battery energy to 3.2 MWh. Commercial operation began in October 2014, and measured 2015-2016 fuel records show roughly 73-77% less diesel than the 2013 baseline.
gasa-island-microgrid-in-southern-korea.status: Commissioned for commercial operation in October 2014 and documented as operating in field measurements through 2016. A 2022 peer-reviewed study still used Gasa-do as the existing pilot microgrid, and current Hyosung reference material continues to list it as completed, but the study's AI/demand-response results are simulations rather than evidence of a deployed upgrade. No public post-2016 fuel series or independently verified 2023-2026 availability, battery health, renewable fraction, or major refurbishment was located.

organizations.name: Hyosung Heavy Industries

organizations.name: Kokam

organizations.name: Korea Electric Power Corporation

organizations.name: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy, Republic of Korea
organizations.role: Central-government sponsor within the KRW 9.2 billion public-private demonstration program.

organizations.name: NPS
organizations.role: Manufacturer of the four 100 kW wind turbines in the official Korean generation inventory.

specifications.label: Storage
specifications.value: 3.2 MWh

specifications.label: Solar photovoltaic capacity
specifications.value: 314 kW as built in field literature: 266 kW across land and rooftop arrays plus 48 kW floating PV. Recovered/vendor summaries commonly round this to 320 kW.

specifications.label: Wind generation
specifications.value: Four 100 kW NPS turbines, 400 kW total

specifications.label: Diesel generation
specifications.value: Three 100 kW generators, 300 kW total. A recovered 100 kW figure is the per-unit rating, not the plant total.

specifications.label: Battery storage and power conversion
specifications.value: Operational and Hyosung accounts report 1.25 MW/3 MWh; Kokam's supplier presentation and some recovered summaries give a 3.2 MWh nominal battery rating. The 3.0 versus 3.2 MWh values are retained as operational versus vendor-nameplate reporting.

specifications.label: Pre-project island demand and diesel use
specifications.value: Average load 96 kW, maximum 173 kW, minimum 61 kW, and approximately 285,000 litres/year of diesel in the design baseline; the measured 2013 comparison year used 260,847 litres

specifications.label: Measured diesel use
specifications.value: 260,847 L in 2013, 214,921 L in 2014, 60,194 L in 2015, and 72,416 L in 2016—equivalent to 77.3% and 72.7% reductions in 2015 and 2016 against the 2013 comparison year

specifications.label: Project funding
specifications.value: KRW 9.2 billion public-private project: 60% central government, 35% KEPCO, and 5% provincial contribution; the IDB retrospective reports about US$7.67 million investment

equipment.detail: Kokam identifies Gasado as a KEPCO 1.25 MW/3.2 MWh battery reference, while Hyosung identifies a completed 1.25 MW/3 MWh microgrid/ESS scope. Field literature specifies nickel-manganese-cobalt lithium-ion chemistry rated for one-C operation and approximately 4,000 cycles; sources do not fully divide pack, PCS, and integration responsibilities.
Four 100 kW permanent-magnet-synchronous-generator turbines with full power converters. The official inventory and technical paper do not provide a reliable public model number.
Eight PV groupings totaling 314 kW include rooftop, land-based, and 48 kW floating installations. Public sources reviewed do not identify module or inverter models.
Three existing 100 kW units are retained as backup. The battery inverter normally forms the grid, so diesel sets need not remain continuously online.

KEPCO combined EMS and PMS functions in one system with SCADA, generation and load forecasting, battery-SOC management, automatic power-flow control, and direct-load control through a supervisory application.

technicalDetails.detail: Battery system: 3.2MWh
Wind Power: 400kW
Solar: 320kW
Application: Renewable Enhancement, Reduce Diesel Generator
Diesel Power Capacity : 300kW
EMS: Automatic control
Previous(Diesel power plant) : 57% – Today (MicroGrid) : 100%

The BESS inverter regulates island voltage and frequency in constant-voltage/constant-frequency mode, while PV and wind operate as sources behind converters and the diesel generators provide backup. This reverses the conventional diesel-forming architecture and allows extended diesel-off operation.

The EMS directs surplus renewable energy to useful loads including water pumping and battery-room cooling instead of curtailing all excess. Forecasting, SOC thresholds, and direct load control coordinate dispatch automatically.

From October 2014 through March 2015, field researchers reported an average renewable fraction of about 82% and roughly 80% lower fuel use. A seven-month utility account similarly reported more than 81% diesel reduction and 29,387 litres consumed. These short-window figures differ from the full-year 2015-2016 records but are directionally consistent.

The IDB retrospective calculated a simple payback longer than 25 years using about US$270,000 in annual fuel savings on US$7.67 million investment. That estimate excludes or simplifies broader resilience, emissions, demonstration, and learning benefits.

A 2022 Scientific Reports article uses the physical Gasa microgrid as its case and models distributed optimization and reinforcement-learning demand response, including a modeled 20% peak reduction. It does not establish that those algorithms or results were deployed in the community system.

`gasa-island-microgrid-in-southern-korea` and `gasado-island` are spelling variants for the same Gasa-do installation. Aggregate maps, portfolios, and capacity calculations should deduplicate them rather than add 314-320 kW PV, 400 kW wind, and 3-3.2 MWh storage twice.

gasado-island.summary: Gasado Island is Gasa-do (가사도), the same KEPCO installation represented by `gasa-island-microgrid-in-southern-korea`; it is not a second project. The October 2014 as-built system combines 314 kW of PV, four 100 kW wind turbines, three 100 kW backup diesels, and a 1.25 MW/3 MWh lithium-ion BESS. Supplier literature gives 3.2 MWh as a nominal battery figure and some summaries round PV to 320 kW. Field data show substantial diesel savings after commissioning.
gasado-island.status: Commercial operation began in October 2014. Measured records show 60,194 litres of diesel used in 2015 and 72,416 litres in 2016, respectively 77.3% and 72.7% below the 2013 comparison year. The physical installation remained an active research case in 2022 and appears in Hyosung's current completed-project references, but no later verified fuel series, availability report, battery state-of-health, or refurbishment record through July 2026 was found.

equipment.detail: NMC lithium-ion storage with approximately one-C capability and 4,000-cycle design life. Kokam publishes the nominal 3.2 MWh battery reference and Hyosung the 3 MWh integrated ESS reference; public evidence does not fully separate their exact subsystem responsibilities.
Four 100 kW permanent-magnet-synchronous machines with full converters; no reliable model number was found.
Integrated EMS/PMS with SCADA, weather and load forecasts, battery-SOC dispatch, direct-load control, and automatic power-flow management.
Eight PV groupings, including 48 kW floating PV, operate with four wind turbines and three retained diesel generators. PV module, inverter, and diesel-genset models are not disclosed.

technicalDetails.detail: Battery – Lithium Ion
The BESS PCS establishes island voltage and frequency in constant-voltage/constant-frequency mode; diesel generation is backup rather than continuously required for grid formation.
KEPCO's EMS uses forecasts and SOC limits to schedule energy, and can route surplus renewable output to water pumping and battery-room cooling.

Field data for October 2014-March 2015 gave about 82% renewable share and about 80% lower fuel use; the longer annual series shows 77.3% and 72.7% reductions for 2015 and 2016 versus 2013. The design-stage 94% renewable target was therefore an expectation, not the demonstrated annual result.
An IDB retrospective estimated more than 25 years simple payback using roughly US$270,000 annual fuel savings, while noting the project was also a national demonstration and learning investment.

A 2022 paper modeled distributed optimization and reinforcement-learning demand response against the Gasa system and reported a modeled 20% peak reduction. It does not prove that these controls were deployed or that field demand fell by that amount.
This slug is a duplicate spelling of `gasa-island-microgrid-in-southern-korea`. Do not sum their generation, storage, costs, or fuel savings as if they were two islands.

geocha-island.summary: The recovered Geocha Island record spans two neighboring but technically distinct Korean projects in the Geochado pair off Jindo: an AC hybrid on Donggeochado (East Geocha) and KEPCO's later low-voltage-DC demonstration on Seogeochado (West Geocha). Hyosung reports a 2016 Donggeochado microgrid with a 250 kW/500 kWh ESS. The West Geocha demonstration added 200 kW PV, 100 kW wind, 1.5 MWh storage, DC distribution, a variable-speed diesel, building-level controls, and electric vehicles, and was completed in September 2018. These phases should not be collapsed into one capacity total without an island boundary.
geocha-island.status: Both phases advanced beyond the recovered 'planning' status. Hyosung dates its East Geocha energy-island microgrid to 2016. KEPCO and technical sources say the West Geocha DC demonstration began in 2016 and was completed in September 2018; a 2021 CIRED paper reports design, construction, and operating experience. However, completion refers to a demonstration system linked to the preexisting AC supply, not proof that every community load was permanently converted to DC. No public post-2021 generation, diesel, availability, or maintenance update was located through July 2026.
geocha-island.historicalLocation: 30-27 Donggeochado-ri, Jodo-myeon, Jindo, Jeollanam-do, South Korea

specifications.value: Hyosung identifies the 2016 Geochado energy-island microgrid as 250 kW/500 kWh; an academic comparison describes 100 kW PV, a wind turbine, three 150 kW diesel generators, and a 250 kW/500 kWh BESS. Public sources reviewed do not provide a reliable wind rating or complete vendor list.

specifications.label: West Geocha renewable generation
specifications.value: 200 kW solar PV and 100 kW wind in the low-voltage-DC demonstration

specifications.label: West Geocha battery storage
specifications.value: 1.5 MWh; public project sources reviewed do not disclose power rating, chemistry, manufacturer, or model

specifications.label: West Geocha demand and legacy supply
specifications.value: Approximately 100 residents in 70 households, 124 kW average load and 305 kW peak load, supplied before the DC project by three 150 kW diesel generators

specifications.label: West Geocha new variable-speed diesel
specifications.value: The KEPCO presentation describes an adjustable variable-speed diesel, but its slides appear to conflict between 100 kW and 200 kW depictions. The rating is therefore left unresolved rather than treated as a firm as-built value.

specifications.label: West Geocha DC distribution
specifications.value: Approximately 4 km of low-voltage DC lines with 1,500 Vdc backbone (±750 V) and lower-voltage ±190 V service/conversion levels

specifications.label: West Geocha project targets
specifications.value: More than 70% renewable-energy contribution, approximately 490 tonnes/year CO2 reduction, and 10% efficiency improvement were project objectives; no reviewed source provides audited annual results confirming them

equipment.detail: Hyosung reports a 250 kW/500 kWh energy-island microgrid reference from 2016. Its public page does not identify battery chemistry or model, and the rating should not be added to West Geocha's 1.5 MWh without preserving the separate island phase.

equipment.category: West Geocha DC generation and storage
equipment.detail: DC-coupled 200 kW PV, 100 kW wind, 1.5 MWh ESS, and a new adjustable-speed diesel work alongside the existing AC diesel plant. Manufacturers and specific component models are not consistently disclosed in the reviewed sources.

The demonstration uses bipolar ±750 V distribution, lower-voltage service conversion, DC smart meters, building converters, and interfaces that allow the DC system to exchange power with the existing AC island system.

The project design included five electric vehicles and three vehicle-to-grid-capable units/chargers as controllable DC resources. Public sources do not report fleet utilization or energy throughput after completion.
Demonstration scope included DC household equipment, LED lighting, a solar wall, thermal storage, public buildings, home energy management, advanced demand response, and smart metering.

technicalDetails.detail: Seogeochado and Donggeochado are adjoining communities connected within the local supply context, but KEPCO literature distinguishes the West Geocha DC demonstration from an already existing East Geocha microgrid. A singular 'Geocha Island' record can therefore mask two installations and two commissioning dates.

The West Geocha system is a DC microgrid connected to the preexisting AC island network for backup and power exchange. It demonstrates reduced conversion stages for native DC generation, storage, EVs, and loads rather than eliminating the AC diesel system outright.

A central EMS coordinates the power-management system, home energy-management systems, advanced demand response, smart meters, generation, storage, diesel, and EV resources. Household-level controls were part of the end-to-end demonstration architecture.

Industry and research accounts say the DC system was completed in September 2018 after work beginning in June 2016. A 2019 account described demonstration loads during the trial and contemplated wider future interconnection, so 'completed' should be read as completion of the demonstration—not verified universal or permanent conversion of island service.

The 70% renewable share, 490-tonne CO2 reduction, and 10% efficiency gain are targets. The 2021 operating paper confirms field experience but the reviewed sources do not disclose a post-commissioning annual renewable fraction, fuel series, outage history, ESS health, or 2022-2026 operating status.

geomun-island.summary: The recovered Geomun Island record should resolve to Geomundo (거문도, Port Hamilton) in Samsan-myeon, Yeosu, South Jeolla—not Geomunoreum on Jeju, where the recovered coordinates appear to point. Geomundo was selected in Korea's 2015 private 'energy-independent island' program for an LG CNS-led proposal combining 1.2 MW solar and 1.5 MW wind with the island's existing 4.5 MW diesel plant. Program reviews show the private-island initiative stalled on weak economics, and no credible construction or commissioning evidence for Geomundo was located through July 2026.
geomun-island.status: Stalled/unbuilt proposal based on the evidence reviewed. LG CNS described Geomundo among selected stand-alone microgrid opportunities in 2016-2017, but a 2016 policy table still showed no project operator for the island and a 2018 program retrospective said the selected private energy-island projects had stalled because low oil prices and poor economics undermined feasibility. No construction award, financial close, installed solar/wind/storage, commissioning, or operational update was found through 2026. A separate 440-500 MW offshore-wind development carrying the Geomundo name is unrelated and is excluded.

equipment.detail: LG CNS promoted a proprietary microgrid EMS and end-to-end engineering, procurement, financing, and operating model in connection with its island projects. Public evidence does not establish delivery of that system on Geomundo.

The public planning record gives aggregate 1.2 MW solar and 1.5 MW wind targets but no panel, inverter, turbine, switchgear, or storage manufacturers and models. Assigning equipment to the island would be speculative.
The pre-project island supply was reported as 4.5 MW. The reviewed sources do not provide generator count, individual ratings, manufacturer, fuel-consumption history, or later replacement status.

technicalDetails.detail: Geomundo was one of several islands selected around 2015 for private-sector energy-independent projects intended to combine renewables, storage, diesel integration, and energy management. LG CNS was associated with Jodo and Geomundo, but project selection was not equivalent to construction authorization or financial close.

A 2016 table already listed no business operator for Geomundo despite the announced consortium association. By December 2018, reporting on the overall private-island program said projects had stalled and their future was uncertain because avoided-diesel economics deteriorated as oil prices fell.

The relevant project site is Geomundo/Port Hamilton in Samsan-myeon, Yeosu, South Jeolla. The recovered record's coordinates appear to correspond instead to Geomunoreum, a volcanic site on Jeju; future data cleanup should correct the project coordinates rather than interpreting this as a Jeju microgrid.

A separate Geomundo-named offshore-wind project appears in current Korean power-market planning at approximately 440-500 MW with a 2031 target. It is geographically associated with the area but is not the 2015 island microgrid proposal and its capacity is excluded from this record.

No source reviewed through July 2026 documents site construction, commissioned renewable or storage capacity, energy production, fuel savings, customers served, outage performance, or an explicit cancellation order. 'Stalled/unbuilt proposal' is more supportable than either 'operational' or a definitive legal cancellation.

goldwind-demonstration-microgrid.summary: This record is Goldwind's grid-connected smart microgrid at its global-headquarters/industrial park in Beijing Yizhuang. The most detailed 2016 participant presentation documents the demonstration as a 2.5 MW Goldwind wind turbine, 500 kWp of PV, several battery and supercapacitor technologies, 730 kW of gas microturbines, and 500 kW of diesel backup. Goldwind subsequently expanded the park: its January 2021 certification announcement reports 4.8 MW of wind and 1.3 MW of solar in 2020. The recovered 3.73 MW figure therefore describes neither the full 2016 resource portfolio nor the expanded 2020 renewable capacity.
goldwind-demonstration-microgrid.status: Operating and evolved into Goldwind's zero-carbon smart-park reference site. Goldwind reported that clean energy supplied 50% of park electricity in 2020 and that the headquarters received China's first carbon-neutral smart-park certification in January 2021. Goldwind continues to market the Yizhuang park as a reference project, but no later public operating dataset was located for annual generation, storage availability, islanding events, or the present installed capacity of each storage technology.

equipment.detail: 2.5 MW permanent-magnet direct-drive turbine used by the original Beijing demonstration. Public evidence reviewed does not identify the additional turbines behind the 4.8 MW wind total reported for 2020.

The 2016 configuration deliberately combined vanadium-flow, lithium, lead-carbon, and supercapacitor devices across long-, medium-, and very-short-duration response. Manufacturers and commercial models are not disclosed in the reviewed material.

Two 65 kW and one 600 kW gas microturbines were paired with 300 kW and 200 kW diesel generators. The presentation labels the gas equipment as microturbines but does not identify its manufacturer or model.

Goldwind described wind/PV/load forecasting, economic dispatch, integrated monitoring, protection, dynamic/transient stability controls, and both grid-connected and isolated operation. No public commercial controller model is identified.

technicalDetails.detail: Adaptable control interface able to control different generation, load and storage technologies
The power storage unit is composed of a 200KW Vanadium redox flow cell, a 200 lithuim cell and a 200KW super capacitor.
The first two storage units can last four hours each and the last for 40 seconds.

The headquarters installation combines a utility interconnection, local wind and PV, dispatchable gas generation, diesel reserve, four complementary storage types, controllable loads, and park-level monitoring/dispatch. It is an industrial-campus demonstration and product-development environment, not a stand-alone village system.

The flow battery provides four-hour energy shifting; the lithium battery provides two-hour response; the lead-carbon battery provides 1.5-hour response; and the supercapacitor addresses roughly ten-second transients. The 2015 engineering study evaluates coordinated hybrid-storage control rather than treating the devices as one interchangeable battery.

The detailed 2016 asset list and Goldwind's later 2020 totals must be kept as separate phases. Adding 2.5 MW wind, 500 kW PV, and 730 kW gas yields the recovered 3.73 MW value, but that arithmetic excludes diesel and storage and was superseded by the 4.8 MW wind/1.3 MW solar expansion.

Goldwind says clean generation covered 50% of park electricity in 2020. The broader carbon-neutral certification also relied on purchasing China Certified Emission Reductions to offset reported greenhouse-gas emissions; it should not be interpreted as 100% on-site renewable electricity.

No reviewed source provides audited post-2020 annual wind/PV production, fuel use, storage cycling/availability, outage performance, or a current one-line equipment inventory. Goldwind's current park page confirms the site remains a reference but is not an operating report.

graciosa-azores-microgrid.summary: Graciólica is the operating island power system on Graciosa in Portugal's Azores, integrating a 4.5 MW wind plant, 1 MWp solar plant, Leclanché lithium-titanate storage, and the island's diesel station under Wärtsilä GEMS controls. It began supplying the island on August 21, 2019. Public sources disagree on the storage power/usable-energy label—7.425 MW/3.2 MWh from Leclanché, 7.5 MVA/2.6 MWh from the Azores project page, and 6 MW/3.2 MWh from Wärtsilä—so those figures are preserved by source rather than silently merged.
graciosa-azores-microgrid.status: Operating. Wärtsilä reports 61% renewable penetration and 139 days of 100% renewable operation during 2024. A regional-government page reported that 8.9 million liters of diesel and €8.2 million had been saved through August 31, 2023. A July 2026 Azores government update shows that the island is now studying flexible water-heater and other loads to reduce remaining renewable curtailment and thermal generation, evidence of continued operation and optimization rather than project abandonment.

equipment.detail: LTO battery system with grid-forming capability, rapid charge/discharge, voltage and frequency control, turnkey delivery, and a 20-year performance warranty. The public rating varies by reporting convention/source.
Coordinates wind, solar, battery, and thermal generation using load patterns, weather forecasts, and asset data to increase renewable dispatch while maintaining reliability.
Aggregate ratings and wind-turbine count are public, but the wind turbine, PV module, and PV inverter manufacturers/models were not identified in the reviewed authoritative sources.
Six existing diesel units remain under the intelligent controller and are dispatched when renewables and stored energy are insufficient or redundancy is required.

technicalDetails.detail: The world’s first megawatt-scale renewable energy plus storage system, currently being built on the island of Graciosa in the Azores (Portugal), will set new standards for islands worldwide.
The centerpiece of the system is a fully automated, intelligently managed 2.8 megawatt battery park integrated into the intelligent energy management system developed by Younicos.
In addition, the system will incorporate wind (4.5 MW) and solar (1 MW) power resources, and intelligent inverters.

The battery forms and stabilizes the isolated island grid, compensates for fast renewable variability, and regulates voltage and frequency; GEMS schedules the full hybrid portfolio from forecasts of generation and demand.

The strongest dated current evidence is Wärtsilä's 61% and 139 fully renewable days in 2024. The Azores project page separately reports a cumulative 62% and 273 fully renewable days without giving its measurement cutoff, so it is not substituted for the dated 2024 figure.

Leclanché says diesel consumption fell by more than 65% and estimates €1.9 million in annual fuel savings. The regional government later reported cumulative physical and financial savings through August 2023. These are publisher-reported results; no independent audited dispatch dataset was located.

Leclanché announced installation completion in December 2018 and Wärtsilä lists delivery in 2018, while the Azores government identifies August 21, 2019 as the start of electricity production and island supply. These dates describe installation/delivery versus commercial supply rather than a contradiction.

In April 2026, the LIFE IP CLIMAZ program convened Graciosa stakeholders to characterize daily demand flexibility, including controllable water heaters, so additional renewable energy can be absorbed instead of curtailed and thermal generation can be reduced.

The public sources do not reconcile the three storage power/energy labels, publish unit-level wind/PV output, or provide battery state-of-health and availability. A proposed future hydrogen system in later studies is not part of the as-built plant and is excluded from installed capacity.

graziosa.summary: The recovered Graziosa record is conflated. Its coordinates and Spain/Canary-Islands location identify La Graciosa beside Lanzarote, but its legacy 4.5 MW wind, 1 MW solar, and multi-megawatt battery specifications belong to the unrelated Graciólica plant on Graciosa in the Portuguese Azores. Spanish La Graciosa instead remains connected to Lanzarote by subsea electricity and water links. It has a distributed 60.1 kWp residential solar-and-storage demonstration from the EU REACT project and is planning broader community microgrids and solar desalination through 2028; it does not have the Portuguese utility-scale hybrid plant.
graziosa.status: Identity corrected to La Graciosa, Canary Islands, Spain. The island remains grid-connected and largely dependent on fossil-derived energy delivered from Lanzarote. REACT installed a distributed residential pilot across 22 dwellings, while the El Sol de La Graciosa energy community and the 2025-2028 ICONIC program are developing additional PV, BESS, internal-microgrid, water, and mobility pilots. No source reviewed supports treating the legacy Azores equipment as installed in Spain.

equipment.detail: REACT deployed rooftop PV, multiple storage technologies, and smart energy controls at 22 preselected dwellings. The project-level results contain one internally inconsistent LFP total and an unexplained 'PLH-C' label, and do not provide a complete household-by-household manufacturer/model inventory.

The island's main electricity supply remains the subsea cable from Lanzarote; the local energy community explicitly plans to retain that connection for security and emergency backup while increasing local self-consumption.
The current 2025-2028 work proposes PV, BESS, software/AI-managed internal microgrids, and a tailored solar-desalination pilot. These are under development and are not added to installed capacity.

technicalDetails.detail: Wind, storage

The app's latitude/longitude and Spain label point to La Graciosa in the Canary Islands. The legacy organizations and ratings point instead to Graciosa in the Azores, roughly 2,000 km away. This enrichment keeps the Spanish identity and removes the Portuguese plant from its installed equipment.

The REACT pilot is a set of coordinated behind-the-meter household resources connected to the local grid, not a single island-forming utility plant. Its role is self-consumption, demand/storage coordination, user participation, and validation of community-energy controls.

The EU island profile says some homes have self-consumption PV but that other buildings and infrastructure depend on Lanzarote; about 90% of supplied energy is associated with fossil combustion. No current island-wide hourly generation or renewable-share dataset was located.

The community's stated path is decentralized rooftop and public-infrastructure PV, storage, renewable-powered desalination and wastewater treatment, and continued subsea-cable backup. These are targets for greater independence before 2030, not an as-built 100% renewable system.

No reviewed source supports wind turbines, a 1 MW central solar farm, or a 2.6-3.2 MWh utility battery on Spanish La Graciosa. The capacity of any post-REACT PV/BESS installed under ICONIC had not been published by the research date.

greenport-microgrid-feasibility-study.summary: This record is the 2016 NY Prize Stage 1 feasibility concept for the Village of Greenport municipal utility on Long Island, not an operating 8.4 MW microgrid. The concept combined a proposed 7.4 MW Wärtsilä dual-fuel reciprocating generator, 375 kW hospital CCHP, 500 kW total PV, a 125 kW/500 kWh battery, demand response, existing backup generation, and distribution controls. Its $14.54 million financial case depended heavily on NY Prize grant support and unresolved fuel/offtake arrangements.
greenport-microgrid-feasibility-study.status: Feasibility/design concept, not verified as built. Greenport appeared among GE's Stage 1 studies but not among the four projects GE supported in Stage 2, and the statewide Stage 2 winner list also omitted Greenport. A separate 2018 Village RFP sought design services for distribution hardening plus an as-yet-unselected renewable resource and storage with anticipated construction funding of $1-5 million; no reviewed public source confirms that the 2016 7.4 MW/CCHP/BESS design or the later RFP concept reached construction or operation.

specifications.value: 70,000 gallons, modeled as seven days of fuel at average GMU load if pipeline gas was unavailable

specifications.label: Conceptual project cost
specifications.value: $14,535,810, including $11.36 million generation, $1.5 million CCHP, $666,750 solar, $300,000 battery, and $709,060 distribution/controls; excluded some design, financing, legal, and development costs

equipment.detail: A prepackaged 7.4 MW dual-fuel unit selected conceptually for output, heat rate, installed-cost, and limited peaking dispatch. The study does not give a model, purchase order, or as-built serial equipment.
125 kW/500 kWh system; chemistry, manufacturer, inverter, and model were to be selected later and are not disclosed.

The conceptual controller would monitor multiple points of interconnection, dispatch resources, forecast/curtail PV, shed load rapidly, manage voltage/frequency, coordinate protection settings, and present a web interface. Vendor selection was explicitly undetermined.
The Village's three aging 6.8 MW oil units and the hospital's backup diesel equipment existed before the proposal. They should not be reported as newly built microgrid equipment.

technicalDetails.detail: liquefied natural gas-fueled generator and a combination of existing and proposed wind, solar, and energy storage resources

Boundary switches would open after loss of grid voltage/frequency; hospital emergency generation would start first, then the CCHP and main gas engine would start/synchronize, feeder ties would close to build the island, loads would be picked up sequentially, and PV would reconnect after stable voltage and frequency were established.

The concept targeted the entire GMU service area during outages, including the hospital, wastewater and water facilities, schools, fire services, Village Hall, ferry/downtown businesses, and other loads on the reused feeders. The 7.4 MW unit was sized near GMU's roughly 6.6 MW peak.

The CCHP was modeled as hospital baseload, PV and the battery as peak reduction, and the 7.4 MW plant as a market/contract resource serving customers outside GMU when prices justified dispatch. The design was therefore more than an emergency-only backup concept.

The study found the microgrid technically feasible, but modeled $7 million of NY Prize support. It concluded GMU could not service its modeled share of debt without that support and that MESCO equity would likely be difficult to attract because short-term offtake might not cover plant output.

GE's current program summary places Greenport only in Stage 1 and lists Albany University Heights, Binghamton, Freeport, and Syracuse as its Stage 2 communities. The statewide 11-winner announcement likewise omits Greenport, so the Stage 1 ratings remain proposals rather than an award-backed as-built configuration.

The Village later solicited engineering/design services for pole replacement, reconductoring, automated switching, and an unspecified renewable source plus storage under a $1-5 million CDBG-DR construction envelope. Because the RFP asked the engineer to select the renewable resource, it is not evidence that the earlier Wärtsilä/CCHP/BESS design was procured.

No construction notice, equipment award, commissioning record, operating data, or current microgrid asset list was located. Every new-generation and storage rating from the Stage 1 report is therefore labeled proposed.

groton-submarine-base.summary: The operating islandable microgrid at Naval Submarine Base New London in Groton, Connecticut now combines a 10.75 MW natural-gas combined-heat-and-power plant with first-call access to a 7.4 MW FuelCell Energy fuel-cell park, plus cybersecure controls, fast load shedding, upgraded distribution, steam, lighting, and base-wide energy management. Its directly documented firm resources total 18.15 MW, so the recovered 13 MW rating is obsolete.
groton-submarine-base.status: Operating. The FuelCell Energy project entered commercial operation on December 16, 2022; the Navy held its comprehensive microgrid ribbon cutting in October 2024; and DOE/NAVFAC recognized first-year performance in 2025. NORESCO says the ESPC has exceeded its annual savings guarantee in every year of performance and has 18 years of ongoing O&M/repair/replacement services. A 2026 FuelCell Energy SEC filing continued to list the Groton project in its operating portfolio.
groton-submarine-base.historicalLocation: Naval Submarine Base New London, Crystal Lake Road, Groton, CT, United States

specifications.label: ESPC economics
specifications.value: NORESCO's 2025 fact sheet describes a $90.1 million ESPC with more than $183.8 million guaranteed savings; its 2020 groundbreaking release described a broader 21-year, $169.3 million contract. The different published scopes are retained rather than reconciled without contract documents.

specifications.label: Resilience coverage
specifications.value: NORESCO states that the CHP and microgrid support 100% of base power requirements during grid outages; DOE says the system now powers the entire base, including moored submarines

equipment.detail: 7.4 MW natural-gas fuel-cell portfolio on leased base land. Public project sources reviewed do not give a definitive commercial platform/model designation, so none is inferred.
Two new natural-gas generators totaling 10.75 MW plus high-efficiency boilers and steam distribution. NORESCO and Navy sources do not identify generator/engine manufacturer or model.

Cybersecure base-wide controls provide automated data collection, precise peak-demand management, utility separation, fast load shedding, islanding, and dispatch of on-site resources. A product/model name is not disclosed.
The ESPC also upgraded electrical infrastructure, steam distribution, LED lighting, and base-wide building/energy controls. No as-built BESS is identified in the current project sources.

technicalDetails.detail: In normal operation the fuel-cell park sells to the public grid through CMEEC. During a base interruption, the Navy has first right to its 7.4 MW output, and the microgrid combines that resource with the 10.75 MW CHP after separating from the utility.

The islanded system supports submarine maintenance, training systems, base-wide communications, mission-critical waterfront facilities, shore power, and moored submarines, rather than only a small subset of buildings.

The Navy/NORESCO CHP expansion broke ground in June 2020 with late-2022 operation expected. FuelCell Energy reached COD in December 2022. The Navy celebrated completion of the comprehensive microgrid in October 2024, reflecting phased completion rather than a single commissioning date.

DOE and NAVFAC report $8 million first-year savings and quantified energy/water reductions. NORESCO's 2025 fact sheet adds that annual savings guarantees have been exceeded every performance year and supplies emissions reductions; these are federal/contractor reports, not raw interval-meter data.

FuelCell Energy's April 2026 Form 10-Q continued to describe the Groton Station project as part of its operating portfolio with CMEEC as purchaser and Navy resilience access, providing current regulatory evidence that the fuel-cell asset remained in service.

No current primary source identifies a battery-storage installation, individual CHP-engine models, fuel-cell platform model, island duration under a specified outage/load case, or measured annual renewable share. The system is predominantly natural-gas generation plus efficiency/control measures, not a renewable-storage microgrid.

hachinohe-microgrid.summary: The Hachinohe microgrid was a NEDO research demonstration in Hachinohe, Aomori, Japan, operated from October 2005 through March 2008. It linked city hall, schools, and other municipal loads over a dedicated 6 kV distribution/communications network to 510 kW of sewage/food-waste biogas generation, 130 kW of PV, a 20 kW wind turbine, a 100 kW lead-acid battery, and heat equipment. It was not a permanently operating post-2008 city utility microgrid.
hachinohe-microgrid.status: Demonstration concluded in March 2008 after the NEDO operating period. The project successfully completed a one-week islanded test in November 2007, but the Microgrid Symposium's project summary says the microgrid is no longer operating because public funding ended. No credible current source was located showing that the private feeder, gas engines, battery, or EMS continue as a unified microgrid.

equipment.detail: Project collaborator for coordinated generation and storage control; the reviewed public materials do not name a commercial controller model.
Three 170 kW reciprocating engines dispatched by the EMS. The reviewed sources do not identify manufacturer or model.
100 kW fast-response resource used for tie-line compensation, isolated-operation frequency response, and instant supply-demand imbalance. Energy capacity and product details are not disclosed.

Hub and local PLCs communicated over private optical-fiber Ethernet and MELSECNET/H, with RS-485, analog, and digital interfaces to generators, storage, meters, and breakers; SCADA and EMS used Ethernet/TCP-IP.
PV, wind, wood boiler, and heat-recovery equipment are documented only by aggregate size/function; public manufacturer and model information was not located.

technicalDetails.detail: 100kW lead-acid battery
510kW(170×3) biogas engines

Daily operating plans and three-minute economic dispatch formed the upper optimization layers; one-second tie-line control and roughly 10 ms/local frequency response formed the power-quality layers. Gas engines handled slower adjustment while the battery responded within about one second.

The project used private electrical and communication lines and a single point of common coupling to the utility. Tie-line power measured system imbalance in normal mode; after separation, generator droop and battery frequency control maintained the island.
From November 3-10, 2007, city hall and six other users maintained normal operation on the private network while isolated from the commercial system.

Electric loads included city hall, four schools, and other offices/facilities. The local generation mix was treated as renewable because the dispatchable engines used sewage/food-waste gas, supplemented by PV, wind, and wood-waste heat, while the utility remained available during grid-connected operation.

The 71.3% energy and 68.9% CO2 reductions are demonstration-period results reported by a controls participant, not post-project performance. No annual fuel, renewable share, battery cycling, or availability series was located.

IEA documents operation only through March 2008, and the Microgrid Symposium summary says the integrated microgrid did not continue once public funding ceased. Historical assets must not be labeled currently operating without newer evidence.

hakai-beach-institute-microgrid.summary: The Hakai Beach Institute microgrid is the off-grid solar-battery-diesel system at what is now Hakai Institute's Calvert Coastal Observatory in Pruth Bay on Calvert Island, British Columbia. The owner currently describes about 450 PV modules totaling roughly 150 kW, approximately 240 kWh of battery storage, one 72 kW normal-operation diesel generator, a second 72 kW backup, and a 12 kW core-load backup. This is a major expansion from the 30-50 kW PV configurations documented around its 2011 commissioning.
hakai-beach-institute-microgrid.status: Operating. Hakai Institute's current technology page says the solar array can power the entire site under favorable conditions and charges the battery with surplus energy. The latest quantified fuel outcome found is historical: a 2016 owner article said diesel use had fallen below 20% of the pre-project level despite added buildings. Current annual renewable share, diesel liters, battery state of health, and generator runtime are not publicly reported.

Documented in SMA's 30 kWp system guide; the separate SMA portal records a 50 kWp early plant. Current expansion equipment is undisclosed.
Module type shown in the SMA monitoring portal for the 2011 plant; no evidence reviewed confirms that all roughly 450 present modules use this model.
Current owner page gives 72 kW + 72 kW + 12 kW roles but not manufacturers or models. Generator heat recovery was incorporated in the early system to serve heating loads.

technicalDetails.detail: The Calvert Island research station has no utility interconnection. Solar directly serves site loads, excess charges the battery, and dispatchable diesel fills renewable/energy deficits; multiple generators provide operational and core-load redundancy.

The original retrofit included demand-side management and recovered generator heat for building heating. Site growth continued while fuel consumption fell, so simple capacity comparisons across the 2011 and current phases do not represent an unchanged load.

SMA documented an early 30 kWp configuration and portal metadata labels 50 kWp; Hakai Energy likewise describes 50 kW by 2011; the current owner page reports about 150 kW. These are expansion stages, not three simultaneous PV plants.

Hakai Energy reports pre-2010 fuel use of up to 500 L/day and under 100 L/day by 2011. Hakai Institute's 2016 retrospective reports less than 20% of former diesel use. Neither is a current audited energy mix, and no annual solar-generation series was found.

The electrical system is at the Calvert Coastal Observatory in Pruth Bay on Calvert Island. Hakai Institute also operates instruments across nearby Hecate Island, but that does not make Hecate Island the microgrid site.

The public SMA portal's annual-production metadata is implausibly large for a 50 kWp plant and is excluded. Current storage chemistry, inverter fleet, PV module models, generator makes, measured renewable fraction, and state-of-health remain undisclosed.

hangzhou.summary: This is the NEDO-Hangzhou Dianzi University high-PV microgrid demonstration at the university's Xiasha campus in Hangzhou, China. Completed in September/October 2008, it paired 120 kW of rooftop PV with a 120 kW diesel generator, battery storage, a 100 kW electric-double-layer capacitor, and multiple power-quality devices to supply two teaching buildings in grid-connected and islanded modes. Credible technical sources identify the disputed 50 kW component as lead-acid storage, not a fuel cell.
hangzhou.status: The formal China demonstration ran during Japanese fiscal years 2007-2009 and was evaluated as completed by NEDO in 2010. A 2022 profile of a university researcher still described the campus equipment, but no current operating report, generation data, maintenance status, or explicit decommissioning notice was found. The safe current classification is completed research demonstration with physical-system status unknown, not a verified continuously operating commercial microgrid.

equipment.detail: 120 kW installed on the roofs of teaching buildings. Public sources reviewed do not disclose module or inverter manufacturers/models.
120 kW dispatchable source used for grid-connected and island-mode balance/frequency support. Manufacturer and model are not identified.
100 kW electric-double-layer capacitor used for rapid load/renewable fluctuation compensation; product manufacturer, energy capacity, and model are not disclosed.
Lead-acid battery used for slower energy balancing. Published power/energy labels conflict, so neither a 50 kW nor 50 kWh figure is converted into an assumed definitive current rating.

Dynamic/power-quality regulator, voltage-dip compensator, disturbance-producing test device, high- and low-voltage switchboards, supply-demand controller, and monitoring equipment supported grid and island tests. Commercial model numbers are not public.

technicalDetails.detail: NEDO designed the China work to prove stable grid-connected and islanded operation with PV making up at least 50% of generation capacity, evaluate secondary-battery and EDLC load-following, assess voltage/power-quality compensation, inject controlled disturbances, and validate simulations.

PV, diesel, battery, EDLC, and compensators served two teaching buildings behind a controllable point of common coupling. Closing the linking breaker provided grid-connected mode; opening it created islanded operation under the supply-demand control system.

The diesel generator carried sustained dispatch and frequency/voltage control, the battery addressed lower-frequency energy imbalance, and the EDLC supplied rapid transient response. Power-quality and voltage-dip compensators allowed tests that went beyond ordinary energy scheduling.

NEDO's post-evaluation says the China project completed grid-connected and independent-operation testing at roughly 50% PV capacity share, including battery/EDLC tracking and power-quality equipment evaluation. It also records that simulation/experiment iteration was needed to tune an initially uncertain diesel response and correct unexpected voltage distortion and lightning-related communications damage.

A 2020 China microgrid history identifies the October 2008 Xiasha installation as a 240 kW early interconnected demonstration and, at the time, the only experimental microgrid with 50% PV generation-capacity share. This is historical context, not evidence of current uniqueness or operation.

A 2022 retrospective describes the teaching-building rooftop array, substation controls, diesel, battery, EDLC, and test devices, but publishes no operating measurements. No credible source was found for current output, battery condition, diesel hours/fuel, islanding availability, ownership changes, or retirement.

hartley-bay-microgrid.summary: Hartley Bay's documented smart-microgrid project was a diesel-efficiency and demand-response retrofit, not a commissioned hydro hybrid. Natural Resources Canada reports a 1.05 MW diesel plant, building controls, smart meters, and an energy-management system that shifted or shed electric heating and ventilation loads. The community was still diesel supplied in 2025; a separate 948–950 kW Gitga'at Power hydro project is in development and is currently projected for 2028–2030 construction and completion if leadership proceeds.
hartley-bay-microgrid.status: The 2008-era smart-metering, efficiency, and demand-response project was commissioned and demonstrated, although continued operation of every legacy controller is not independently confirmed. BC Hydro reports 0.6 million litres of diesel consumption in Hartley Bay in 2025. The proposed hydro plant has not been built: permitting, studies, commercial negotiations, and financing remain in progress, with 2030 the current target completion year.

Monitoring and load-management system added or removed controllable loads according to a system-efficiency algorithm.

technicalDetails.detail: It has been able to reduce the maximum demand (61.3 kW) by 15%.
The microgrid project has been estimated saving around 77,000 L of diesel per year .
The controller shifted thermostatically controlled building loads according to generator efficiency and available system margin rather than using battery storage.
The recovered project page reported a 61.3 kW peak reduction and about 77,000 litres of annual diesel savings; these remain archive-sourced figures.
The government paper documents 1.05 MW of installed diesel generation; the recovered directory's 3 MW figure is retained separately and should not be treated as the same verified configuration.
The reviewed smart-microgrid papers do not document an operating 900 kW hydro plant, so the recovered hydro value is not presented as installed equipment.
The project overlaid monitoring, building-efficiency measures, coordinated diesel dispatch, and demand response on an isolated diesel power system.
Baseboard heating, domestic hot water, HVAC, and ventilation loads at the school, clinic, community centre, and other buildings were candidates for short-duration control.
The technical report describes about 170 residents and 82 buildings, comprising 62 residential and 20 commercial or mixed-use buildings.
The demonstration-era cost of electricity was estimated at roughly C$0.67/kWh and more than C$500,000 per year; these are historical estimates, not current tariffs.
The latest primary planning evidence still treats Hartley Bay as a diesel community. The hydro contribution is zero until the separate Gitga'at Power project is commissioned.
A 5.7 km access road was completed in January 2024, commercial negotiations began in August 2025, and environmental, technical, and permitting work continues.

hefei-university-of-technology-microgrid.summary: Hefei University of Technology built a laboratory microgrid testbed with the University of New Brunswick to study multi-energy generation, power electronics, storage, and hierarchical control. The best primary descriptions identify 10 kW single-phase and 30 kVA three-phase PV sources, two 30 kW wind-generation simulators, a 5 kW fuel cell, battery and ultracapacitor storage, and two 15 kW conventional-generator simulators. This corrects legacy descriptions that treated all 60 kW of wind as physical turbines and attributed the project to the Chinese Academy of Sciences.
hefei-university-of-technology-microgrid.status: Historical academic research facility. The testbed was reported in 2008 and reviewed in 2013. HFUT researchers continued publishing related islanded-microgrid control work through at least 2018, but no primary source confirms that the original hardware configuration remains intact or operating in 2026.
