hempstead-port-washington-microgrid-feas.summary: The Town of North Hempstead's Port Washington project was a NY Prize Stage 1 feasibility study for five coordinated, site-level microgrids serving municipal, school, fire, water, library, cultural, and emergency facilities. The 2016 concept combined 616 kW of CHP, 310 kW of proposed PV, 150 kWh of storage, 40 kW of existing PV, and 1.712 MW of existing emergency generators. It remained a planning study: Port Washington does not appear on NYSERDA's official Stage 2 award list, and no commissioning evidence was found.
hempstead-port-washington-microgrid-feas.status: Stage 1 feasibility study completed in April 2016; not built. The project received a Stage 1 award but is absent from the official Stage 2 award roster. The conclusion that it did not advance is an inference from NYSERDA's award records and the lack of subsequent construction or operating documentation.

technicalDetails.detail: The proposed microgrid would use solar, energy storage, energy efficiency, and natural gas generation.
The proposed microgrid would use solar, energy storage, energy efficiency, and natural gas generation
Five site-specific nodes were intended to coordinate as one resilience entity, but the study notes that the nodes might not all be physically interconnected.
Each site microgrid was conceived to isolate locally and support prioritized critical loads with CHP, PV, storage, and existing standby generators.
Landmark on Main Street, Port Washington Public Library, and two fire stations formed the central civic/emergency cluster.
Additional nodes covered school-district buildings, water-district infrastructure, fire headquarters, and the Town animal shelter.

The CHP output in the model equals roughly three-quarters of the listed annual load by arithmetic, but this is a planning-model result rather than measured generation. Solar and grid energy would supply the balance under normal conditions.
NYSERDA lists the project among Stage 1 winners but not among the eleven Stage 2 awards, supporting classification as an unbuilt feasibility concept.

huatacondo-microgrid.summary: Energía Sustentable Cóndor (ESUSCON) is the isolated community microgrid inaugurated in Huatacondo, Chile, in 2010 by the University of Chile, Compañía Minera Doña Inés de Collahuasi, and the community. It expanded electricity service from roughly 8–10 hours per day to continuous service using diesel, tracking PV, a small wind turbine, lead-acid storage, advanced dispatch, and a community-facing 'Social SCADA.' Published capacity figures conflict by date and boundary: the strongest monitored configuration reports 23 kW PV, 108 kW diesel, and 27 kW/127 kWh storage, while earlier control papers report 22 kW PV, 5 kW wind, and 120 kW diesel.
huatacondo-microgrid.status: Commissioned in 2010 and directly described as operating in a 2019 University of Chile thesis. Monitoring papers analyze 2011–2013 operation. Later publications continue to use ESUSCON as a live case study, but no primary 2026 operating report or current equipment inventory was located, so present-day status beyond the last direct confirmation is unverified.

technicalDetails.detail: The energy management system provides online set-points for generation units while minimizing operating costs, taking into account renewable resource forecast, load, solar tracking, and water consumption.
HUATACONDO’S MICROGRID / STORAGE SYSTEM
• 1ph – 3ph inverter
HUATACONDO’S MICROGRID / PV POWER PLANT
• 84 PV panels
The robust EMS formulation used 15-minute scheduling intervals and a two-day prediction horizon for solar, wind, and load uncertainty.
Social SCADA translated system conditions into simple visual signals, allowing the neighborhood council and households to participate in load management.
Monitoring literature records failure of the PV tracking mechanism and a repair period of roughly two months, helping explain the low early PV capacity factor.

The 23/108/127 figures come from a monitored-system paper, while 22/5/120 and 22/3/150/170 appear in control and demonstration descriptions. They likely reflect different dates, rating conventions, or system boundaries and cannot be safely collapsed into one exact configuration.
A 2019 thesis reports that the original C# SCADA code was difficult to maintain and describes a modular redesign while stating that ESUSCON had operated since 2010 with Energy Center support.

Diesel remained the firm source; PV, wind, and storage reduced fuel consumption and enabled continuous service. The most defensible measured renewable share is the dated 21% early-operation value, not a current annual mix.

huntington-microgrid-feasibility-study.summary: The Town of Huntington NY Prize concept linked Town Hall-area civic loads, Huntington Hospital, and the wastewater treatment plant through a 13.8 kV community microgrid. Its Stage 1 design proposed a 2.8 MW fuel-cell CHP system, 400 kW reciprocating-engine CHP, a 1.3 MW peaking generator, 560 kW of PV, 2 MW/2 MWh battery storage, a 100 kW flywheel, and 900 kW of existing diesel generation. Huntington won US$1 million for Stage 2 detailed engineering in 2017, but NYSERDA later ended the Stage 3 funding path; no construction or commissioning evidence was found.
huntington-microgrid-feasibility-study.status: Feasibility and detailed-engineering work completed or substantially advanced, but the community microgrid was not built. In October 2020 the Town stated that NYSERDA was no longer pursuing NY Prize and that no Stage 3 funding would be available. A 2021 federal research proposal referenced the facilities, but it is not evidence that the NY Prize design entered construction.

technicalDetails.detail: The feasibility study will evaluate adding methane and natural gas fired generation with waste heat recovery, solar power, and energy storage technologies.
Methane and natural gas fired generation with waste heat recovery, solar power, and energy storage technologies

The proposed ring connected the wastewater plant and hospital underground, while an overhead link reached the Town Hall area. The Town Hall cluster was designed to retain an independent island if the overhead tie failed.
Storage would bridge transitions and regulate voltage and frequency while dispatchable generators started; the fuel cell was treated as grid-following rather than the primary grid-forming source.
The control concept shed load to about 80% of available online distributed-generation capacity and restored loads according to criticality and reserve.
The feasibility design targeted roughly 70–80% state of charge before a forecast islanding event to preserve transition reserve.
Huntington moved from a US$100,000 Stage 1 study to a US$1 million Stage 2 engineering award in 2017 and was preparing for Stage 3 by 2019.

The Town reported in October 2020 that NYSERDA would not offer Stage 3 construction funding and sought FEMA BRIC funding instead. No evidence located shows that alternative funding produced the designed microgrid.

A January 2021 Town resolution supported a DOE Connected Communities proposal with Ecogy, Brookhaven National Laboratory, Stony Brook University, and Pacific Northwest National Laboratory; this was a proposal, not commissioning documentation.

ilse-of-eigg-microgrid.summary: Eigg Electric is an operating, community-owned island microgrid commissioned in 2008. The current system combines about 170 kW of PV, 112 kW of hydro, 24 kW of wind, two 64 kW backup diesel generators, and a substantially upgraded Rolls/SMA battery system of roughly 714 kWh usable-to-50%-depth-of-discharge basis and 216 kW inverter capacity. Current project sources report approximately 93–95% renewable electricity, while a new 300 kW wind project remains in planning rather than operation.
ilse-of-eigg-microgrid.status: Operating. Eigg Electric's official site is current, contractor records document PV and storage upgrades through 2024–2025, and Companies House shows the operating company filing accounts through March 2025. Three proposed 100 kW wind turbines are in planning and fundraising and must not be counted in present capacity.

technicalDetails.detail: Storage: 212 kWh Rolls Surrette FLA
Hydro: 3 x Hydro generators, 110 kW (Major renewable energy source) providing sufficient power most of the year, except in summer months
Wind: 4 x Proven wind turbines connected to Windy Boy 6000A, 24 kW (Second largest renewable resource), providing power throug entire year, lowest yield in summer
PV: 165 Watt modules with 3 Sunny Boy 3000, a 10 kWp (Smallest renewable resource), provides additional power in summer months- upgraded to 32kW in 2010
 Diesel Generator: 2 x 64kW, primarily backup
SMA Sunny Island battery inverters establish island voltage and frequency and coordinate renewable generation, storage, and diesel backup.
The generators automatically start at approximately 50% battery state of charge and stop around 90%.
Frequency-based controls route excess energy to community heating loads and wind dump heaters and can throttle hydro output when batteries are full.

The original system had roughly 212 kWh usable at 50% depth of discharge and 60 kW of inverter capacity; an intermediate configuration reached about 530 kWh and 156 kW before the 2024 upgrade to roughly 714 kWh and 216 kW.

Diesel is standby firm capacity, not the dominant annual energy source. The official site says diesel generators run less than 17% of the time on average; this runtime percentage is not the same as energy share.
The 5 kW household cap limits simultaneous electric heating, transport, cooking, and conventional loads. Current planning includes a second 5 kW household circuit for electrification growth.
Eigg Electric Ltd remains active in the UK register with accounts filed through the year ending March 2025, corroborating ongoing institutional operation.

indian-head-nwc.summary: Naval Support Facility Indian Head progressed from a 2009–2010 Sandia energy-surety concept to a real 13.2 kV microgrid delivered under MILCON P-222 in 2015. The installed electrical resources are a 4.6 MW natural-gas combustion-turbine CHP plant and two 2.5 MW diesel standby generators, totaling 9.6 MW of on-site generation. A frequently cited '15 MW microgrid' label describes the served system/load class rather than generation nameplate. The Navy replaced the old coal-fired central steam plant with decentralized natural-gas steam plants and the CHP facility; in 2023 it privatized the base distribution system to SMECO while retaining the cogeneration plant.
indian-head-nwc.status: Operational. The first islanding test occurred in August 2015, and a 2018 commissioning review documented corrective work after more than two years of operation. A 2023 NAVFAC utility-privatization award requires the Navy-owned cogeneration plant to remain able to feed the distribution system, providing the latest direct evidence of continuing microgrid capability, though no recent annual islanding or energy-performance statistics were found.

equipment.detail: 4.6 MW natural-gas combustion turbine and heat-recovery steam generator; manufacturer and model are not identified in the cited sources
Two 2.5 MW diesel generator sets; manufacturer and model are not disclosed
SEL protective relays programmed for coordinated utility-connected and island protection; exact model numbers are not disclosed
Centralized SCADA and Utilities and Energy Management controls coordinate breakers, generators, load priorities, and islanding
Uninterruptible-power supplies were added or corrected so protective and fail-safe controls would remain functional through utility-loss events
Seven decentralized natural-gas boiler plants, approximately 200–750 hp each, plus the Strauss CHP steam output

technicalDetails.detail: The system can separate from the utility, prioritize and shed mission loads, synchronize local generators, and restore load as generation becomes available.

Sandia's 2009–2010 Energy Surety Microgrid work was a consequence-based conceptual design. The later P-222 construction documents the actual commissioned system and should be used for installed equipment.
The installed generator arithmetic is 4.6 MW plus 5 MW, or 9.6 MW. The presentation's 15 MW microgrid label is best interpreted as a system or served-load rating, not generation nameplate.

The 2018 review found false breaker-failure indications, generator-availability and fuel-control problems, and a combustion-turbine trip following utility loss; control logic, recovery sequencing, UPS coverage, and turbine isolation were corrected.
The base used overhead 13.2 kV delta distribution. Microgrid protection had to accommodate low fault current and different source configurations during island operation.

SMECO received a 50-year contract to own, operate, maintain, and modernize the electric distribution system. The Navy retained the cogeneration plant, which must continue to be capable of supplying the privatized network.
Normal electricity can come from the SMECO grid and the gas CHP plant; diesel provides standby/island support. The cited sources do not publish a current annual MWh or fuel-share breakdown.

intelligent-dc-microgrid-living-lab.summary: Aalborg University's Intelligent DC Microgrid Living Lab (iDClab) was a 2014–2017 research project comparing a multivoltage DC apartment microgrid with a conventional 230 V AC apartment under realistic residential use. The documented DC architecture included 380 V down to 12 V buses, four bidirectional TDK-Lambda EZA2500 converters, storage, programmable/real loads, meters, and proposed interfaces for PV, wind, electric vehicles, and hydrogen micro-CHP. A partnered North China Electric Power University facility used separate 750 V DC buses and must not be merged into the Aalborg equipment inventory.
intelligent-dc-microgrid-living-lab.status: The funded iDClab project is finished; Aalborg records its duration as January 2014 through December 2017. A 2020 IoT-MGLab publication and Aalborg's current Microgrids and Energy Internet Laboratory show related research continuing with newer infrastructure, but neither source proves that the original apartment testbed remains in its 2015 configuration.

technicalDetails.detail: Electrolyser produces hydrogen with excess of renewable energy to store until EMS requires heat or electricity
Solar PV
Whisper Wind Turbines
Micro-CHP based on fuel cells
Two fully functional residential sections—one multi-bus low-voltage DC and one conventional AC—were designed for direct efficiency comparison.
The EMS architecture spans electricity, gas, and district heating and can choose between battery storage and hydrogen production during renewable surplus.
The program studied design, modeling, control, coordination, communications, and management rather than documenting a commercial production microgrid.
The reviewed paper does not name PV modules, battery cells, DC/DC converters, fuel-cell stack, electrolyser, or central EMS software model.
The paired apartments enabled direct comparison of AC and DC distribution efficiency, conversion losses, control, power quality, and user behavior under similar load profiles.
The NCEPU 750 V buses, 1 MVA transformer, supercapacitors, and multiple battery chemistries belong to the separate Chinese industrial lab, not the Aalborg residential apparatus.
The project paper cites efficiency-savings percentages from other DC-distribution literature but does not report a completed, measured annual energy-savings result for iDClab itself.

A 2020 paper describes an IoT-MGLab at Aalborg with 230 V AC and 48/400 V DC buses, PV, wind, and battery resources using FIWARE/NGSI context-broker infrastructure. It is related follow-on work, not a verified unchanged continuation of iDClab.

Aalborg's current Microgrids and Energy Internet Laboratory supports microgrid, converter, intelligent-electronic-device, advanced-metering, and real-time-platform research, confirming institutional continuity rather than original-project operating status.

This was a flexible laboratory, not a utility-serving plant with a stable annual generation mix. PV, wind, storage, grid exchange, and hydrogen micro-CHP were research elements, and no annual MWh split is published.

islas-secas-island-microgrid.summary: Islas Secas is an off-grid resort microgrid in Panama's Gulf of Chiriquí built around a 355 kW PV array, advanced lead batteries, SMA inverters, and two 200 kW diesel backup generators. The battery system began with 18 East Penn stacks and was later augmented by nine GS Yuasa stacks under a Nuvation BMS/energy controller. The most detailed battery case study reports 2.1 MWh and 162 kW after augmentation. This is a solar-dominant hybrid system, not literally diesel-free: the resort now markets 100% of property electricity as solar-generated, while the engineering record retains diesel standby and the original design estimate was 90% solar/battery energy.
islas-secas-island-microgrid.status: Operating as the resort's island power system. Islas Secas' 2025 factsheet continues to state that 100% of property electricity is generated by renewable solar, and current Nuvation and Consortium for Battery Innovation pages continue to feature the installation. No audited recent generation, diesel-run-hour, fuel-use, battery-availability, or outage dataset was found, so the current 100% statement is treated as a resort claim about electricity generation rather than proof that backup generators never run.

equipment.detail: 1,400 modules installed beside the island airstrip.
Eighteen PV inverters and 36 bidirectional battery inverters form the AC-coupled system.
Advanced lead batteries of different manufacturers, capacities, ages, and states of health are operated as one system by configurable Nuvation controls.
Nuvation BMS units replaced the original BMS on the East Penn banks and were added to the GS Yuasa banks; a custom ESS controller coordinates all 27 banks and two diesel gensets.

technicalDetails.detail: Under normal operation, the batteries and solar array should be able to supply 90% of all the energy needed by Islas Secas.
Similar to a plug-in Prius, the PV array (and batteries) power all the island’s systems, 365 days of the year.
Islas Secas is powered a 355kW PV array located next to the island’s air strip.
For heavy loads or unexpected bad weather, the system can call on two 200kW diesel generators at any time.
PV serves island loads and charges storage during the day; at dusk, the resort normally transfers to silent battery operation. Diesel sets are available for heavy loads or prolonged cloudy periods.

The augmentation did not discard the original 18 stacks. Nuvation replaced their BMS, added nine new stacks, and programmed the EMS to prioritize cycling across banks with different charge/discharge profiles and states of health.

Use 355 kW as the repeatedly published plant rating while retaining 378 kWp as module arithmetic. For storage, preserve the 1.5 MWh original, approximately 0.5 MWh augmentation, and 2.1 MWh technical-summary values instead of forcing a false exact total.
No reviewed source discloses commissioning date, hourly operating data, recent diesel use, battery replacement history after the GS Yuasa augmentation, or measured outage/reliability performance.

jo-island.summary: This record is the proposed Jodo (조도) Energy-Independent Island project in Jodo-myeon, Jindo-gun, South Jeolla—not an operating project called simply 'Jo Island.' The LG CNS consortium's plan paired 294 kW of PV, two 750 kW wind turbines, an 8.12 MWh ESS with 4.25 MW PCS, and the existing 3.6 MW oil-fired internal-combustion plant. It targeted 45% renewable supply. Evidence from 2018 and a 2022 Korea Energy Economics Institute study shows that Jodo and the other five-island private-investment projects did not reach a PPA/SPC implementation stage; the planned renewable assets should not be counted as installed.
jo-island.status: Stalled/unbuilt proposal. LG CNS still described Jodo as a selected track record in its 2016-2018 sustainability materials, but the 2022 academic review found that none of the five follow-on islands had signed a PPA and a December 2018 Korean report said the same five had failed to establish project companies or show implementation progress after tariff-policy changes destroyed the expected economics. No later authoritative evidence of construction or commissioning at Jodo was located.

equipment.detail: Two 750 kW wind turbines and 294 kW of PV were specified only at aggregate level; no manufacturer or model is identified, consistent with a proposal that had not reached procurement.

The plan specified 8.12 MWh of ESS and 4.25 MW PCS and assigned the project to LG CNS, whose offering included an energy management/microgrid control system. No evidence shows this equipment was ordered or installed at Jodo.
Three oil-fired internal-combustion units totaling 3.6 MW remained the documented supply base; engine manufacturers and fuel subtype are not disclosed in the reviewed sources.

technicalDetails.detail: Private operators were expected to finance renewables, ESS, and EMS, then recover investment through a 20-year electricity sale to KEPCO. The project was therefore contingent on a bankable PPA rather than a normal government-funded demonstration procurement.

Falling oil prices and a 2016 change from a fixed island renewable price to compensation linked to diesel operating cost substantially reduced expected revenue. The five selected follow-on islands, including Jodo, consequently failed to establish SPCs or conclude PPAs.

Use Jodo, Jodo-myeon, Jindo-gun, Jeollanam-do as the project identity. There are multiple Korean islands named Jodo; this proposal is not the much smaller Jodo near Namhae and not the Jo Island spelling used in the recovered record.

All renewable and storage figures are planned capacity. Only the 3.6 MW conventional plant and 2015 generation baseline are documented as existing; representing 1.794 MW of renewables or 8.12 MWh as operational would materially overstate deployment.

kalkeri-sangeet-vidyalaya-dc-microgrid.summary: Kalkeri Sangeet Vidyalaya's campus energy project near Dharwad, Karnataka was built with its $100,000 2014 Zayed Future Energy Prize award and SELCO India/SELCO Foundation support. The installed package included a 14 kW AC solar mini-grid for classrooms, hostels, office, hall, and kitchen; a separate 5 kW solar water-pumping system; solar hot water; biogas; LEDs; and daylighting improvements. The recovered title calls it a DC microgrid, but the school's own annual report says AC mini-grid and the detailed configuration has a one-phase AC output. Its 120 × 2 V × 300 Ah battery inventory equals 72 kWh nominal, not the recovered 17 kWh.
kalkeri-sangeet-vidyalaya-dc-microgrid.status: Installed in 2014-2015 and historically described as operational. KSV remains an active school with 230 students in 2023-2024 and still lists SELCO Foundation as a partner; a 2024 partner update says local electricity rationing continues to affect the school. No recent technical report confirms present battery health, solar output, outage coverage, or whether the original lead-acid bank has been replaced, so current microgrid availability is not independently verified.

equipment.detail: Fifty 280 Wp modules mounted on a custom module-mounting structure above the battery room; manufacturer and model are not disclosed.
A 120-cell 2 V/300 Ah bank and a power-conditioning unit serve the mini-grid. Battery chemistry, PCU manufacturer/model, and usable depth of discharge are not public.
Building clusters connect through distribution boxes with MCB protection. Each distribution point has an energy meter and consumption data is logged to a central server for load auditing and control.

technicalDetails.detail: The Kalkeri Sangeet Vidyalaya DC Microgrid is a solar microgrid in India by SELCO foundation in Karnataka.
14 kWp Solar – (10 series x 5 parallel 280Wpk)

Despite the recovered record's 'DC microgrid' title, KSV's annual report explicitly calls the installation a 14 kW AC solar mini-grid, while the technical description specifies one-phase AC output. The DC battery/PV subsystem does not make the campus distribution network a DC microgrid.

The electrical mini-grid was one part of a student-led sustainability package that also reduced load through efficient lighting and daylighting and displaced other energy uses through solar hot water, solar pumping, and biogas.

Submetering was designed to expose excess consumption by building cluster and allow school staff to intervene. The reviewed sources do not provide logged generation/consumption data or measured savings from the planned before-and-after energy audits.

KSV and its partner network remain active, but a 2024 funding story still cites electricity rationing at Kalkeri. That does not prove failure of the mini-grid—loads may have grown or storage may be aging—but it prevents claiming full energy independence without a current engineering report.

kema.summary: The recovered 'KEMA' project is Leonics' abbreviated label for the RPS Kemar solar-diesel-battery mini-grid in Gerik, Perak, Malaysia—not Kemayan, Pahang. Supplier and TNB presentations document an 850 kWp PV array split between 600 kWp DC-coupled and 250 kWp AC-coupled sections, 850 kW of inverter capacity, 4.8 MWh of 480 VDC battery storage, and four diesel generators totaling 1.6 MW. The project's published 3.30 MW label is Leonics' sum of PV, inverter, and diesel ratings, not a conventional net generation-capacity figure.
kema.status: Commissioned in November 2012 for 24-hour rural electrification. A 2018 TNB Energy Services company profile still listed TNBES as EPC and O&M contractor for 342 consumers, but no later project-specific operating report was located. The plant should be described as commissioned/historically operating, with present battery condition, diesel consumption, renewable share, and service availability unverified.

equipment.detail: Three 200 kW units provide 600 kW of grid-forming/bidirectional conversion. The reviewed project diagram does not give the commercial model.
A 250 kW grid-connected inverter serves the AC-coupled PV section; nine 70 kW MPPT charge controllers serve the 600 kWp DC-coupled section.
The supplier architecture uses a System Monitoring Unit, Inverter System Controller, and Hybrid System Control and Command Unit for coordinated PV, battery, and diesel dispatch and remote monitoring.
Aggregate ratings and diesel-unit sizes are public, but battery manufacturer/chemistry and genset manufacturers/models are not identified in the reviewed authoritative material.

technicalDetails.detail: Daytime loads can be served by 250 kWp of AC-coupled PV while the 600 kWp DC-coupled array charges the 480 V battery through MPPT controllers. Battery inverters and diesel sets support the common isolated AC mini-grid and provide overnight/low-solar supply.

Leonics alternates between 'Kema' in overview graphics and 'Kemar Village, Perak' in the detailed diagram. TNB names the site RPS Kemar, Grik/Gerik, Perak. The recovered coordinates near Kemayan, Pahang are therefore a geocoding error and should not be used for this asset.
The system was intended to provide expandable 24-hour electricity with remote monitoring to settlements and public facilities that lacked normal grid service.

No project-specific public source reviewed after the 2018 O&M listing reports energy production, PV share, diesel savings, outages, battery replacements, or whether the original 4.8 MWh bank remains in service.

kiffa-hybrid-project.summary: Kiffa is an operating 6.3 MW solar-thermal plant about 13 km from Kiffa, Mauritania, supplying Kiffa and Guérou through a 33 kV network. It combines 1.3 MWp of PV with four HFO-capable engine-generators totaling 5 MW. Vergnet delivered the solar portion and ABC Contracting/ABC Engines the thermal portion under SOMELEC/AFD-EU sponsorship. Unlike some generic microgrid listings, it has no documented battery: renewable variability is balanced by dispatchable thermal generation on the isolated regional grid.
kiffa-hybrid-project.status: Operating but capacity-constrained and undergoing maintenance. Commissioning is reported as April 2018, with public inauguration in May 2019. An AFD evaluation found the installed capacity insufficient for demand growth by 2020 because a planned expansion had been delayed. The PV plant produced nearly 2.3 GWh in 2021 with strong measured performance, while SOMELEC's 2025 procurement records show an ABB motor rewinding service and planned replacement of failed generating set No. 1, evidence of continued use but also equipment issues.

equipment.detail: Two central inverters; the study reports 98.31% average inverter efficiency for the 2021 monitoring year.
Four 750 rpm HFO engine-generator sets arranged as two 1 MW and two 1.5 MW units. Commercial engine model numbers are not consistently documented in the reviewed plant-specific sources.
The performance paper identifies 4,320 polycrystalline-silicon modules but the reviewed accessible text does not reliably expose the module manufacturer/model.
A plant command/control system coordinates PV and thermal production on the isolated 33 kV network. No battery energy-storage system is listed by the EPC, AFD, government, or performance sources.

technicalDetails.detail: The project integrates the Engineering, Procurement and Construction (EPC) of a 1.3 MWp solar PV plant along with a 5 MW thermal (LFO/HFO) plant.
The new plant will replace the existing power plants in the two towns (diesel generators) and will be the only generating station on Kiffa’s isolated electrical grid.
To achieve this innovative project, Vergnet entered a partnership with ABC DIESEL.

PV reduces fuel use while the four synchronous thermal units provide dispatchability and grid support. The plant was conceived as the sole central station on Kiffa's isolated grid and later linked Guérou; it is a hybrid power station rather than a battery-centered microgrid.

Analysis of January-December 2021 supervisory data estimated that the PV portion displaced more than 550 tonnes of fossil fuel. Dust and high summer module temperatures produced seasonal capture losses, while annual inverter and system performance remained strong.

AFD judged the project successful but reported that accumulated implementation delays erased the demand-growth margin: an extension expected by 2017-2018 had not occurred, and installed capacity was already insufficient by 2020.

SOMELEC's 2025 procurement plan calls for replacing failed unit No. 1 with a new engine, while a September 2025 notice sought rewinding of an ABB electric motor at the plant. These records support operating relevance but also flag reduced thermal availability pending repair/replacement.

kihola-bay-residential-solar-microgrid-h.summary: The legacy 'Kihola Bay' entry is the Bakken Hale Estate microgrid at Kīholo Bay/North Kona, Hawaiʻi. Renewable Energy Services completed the off-grid system in June 2015 for Earl Bakken's 17,000-square-foot estate. Original sources describe approximately 172-176 kW of PV, 1 MWh of 48 V Aquion aqueous-hybrid-ion storage, SMA inverters, MidNite charge controllers, and propane/biodiesel backup. The legacy 1,000 MWh storage value is a three-order-of-magnitude error. RES' current case page now lists Blue Planet Energy Blue Ion HI batteries, indicating a storage retrofit from the original Aquion system, although its date and exact replacement capacity are not disclosed.
kihola-bay-residential-solar-microgrid-h.status: Completed June 2015 and presented by the installer as a successful operating case. The current RES page retains a 176 kW/1 MWh project rating and shows Blue Ion HI batteries plus biodiesel backup. No current operating dataset or retrofit announcement was found, so present battery capacity, annual solar fraction, backup-fuel consumption, and estate operation should be treated as unverified beyond the installer case page.
kihola-bay-residential-solar-microgrid-h.historicalLocation: Kiholo Bay, Kailua-Kona, HI, United States

equipment.detail: 1 MWh, 48 V AHI system using a water-based electrolyte, manganese-oxide cathode, and carbon-composite anode. It is no longer the battery brand listed by the installer.
RES' live case study identifies Blue Ion HI batteries and shows the replacement battery room; no public retrofit date or detailed configuration was located.
SunPower 345 W modules with Tigo module-level power optimizers and cloud monitoring/shutdown capability.

equipment.model: Sunny Island, Sunny Boy, and Classic
equipment.detail: SMA battery and PV inverters work with MidNite Classic charge controllers in the original 48 V off-grid architecture.

technicalDetails.detail: Battery Inverter: SMA Sunny Island Charge
Controller: Midnite Solar Classic

The original battery charged from PV during roughly eight daylight hours and discharged across an approximately 16-hour non-solar interval; standby generation covered multi-day low-solar periods and contingencies.

The coastal estate could not practically be served by utility lines and had relied on three aging propane generators with unusually expensive fuel delivery. Solar and storage were intended to remove noise and reduce fuel dependence.

Original 2015 sources uniformly identify Aquion AHI storage, whereas RES now names Blue Ion HI. The safest interpretation is a later battery retrofit, not two simultaneous 1 MWh banks; absent a dated retrofit source, exact timing and retained Aquion equipment are unknown.
Use Kīholo Bay/Bakken Hale rather than 'Kihola.' Preserve 172 kW as the original engineering label and 176 kW as the installer/module-nameplate value. Correct storage from 1,000 MWh to 1 MWh.

king-island.summary: Hydro Tasmania's King Island system is a continuously evolving off-grid utility network, not a single static project. The 2010-2016 KIREIP program integrated 2.45 MW of wind, 470 kW of solar, a 3 MW/approximately 1.5 MWh advanced lead-acid battery, two 1 MVA diesel-UPS flywheels, a 1.5 MW dynamic resistor, demand response, diesel/biodiesel generation, and autonomous controls. A new 1.5 MW/5,000-panel solar farm entered service in 2023, and Hydro Tasmania began a $11.5 million wind-farm repowering plus battery replacement in September 2024, due for completion in 2027.
king-island.status: Operating and under refurbishment. ARENA marks the original KIREIP funding project complete as of April 19, 2016, while the island power system continues in service. Hydro Tasmania reported that diesel use had fallen 50% over the decade to 2023, saving 2.1 million liters and 5,700 tCO2 annually; the new solar farm was forecast to save another 300,000 liters and 800 tCO2 per year. The five wind turbines and battery are now being refurbished/replaced through 2027.

specifications.label: KIREIP battery
specifications.value: 3 MW/1.5 MWh advanced lead-acid storage in Entura's project account; other sources cite 1.6 MWh usable, reflecting a reporting/usable-energy difference

specifications.label: Renewable performance
specifications.value: KIREIP was designed for 65% annual renewable energy and 100% instantaneous renewable operation. APEC's 2023 review says the system operates diesel-off about 20% of the year and reduced annual diesel use from 4.5 to 2.6 million liters.

equipment.manufacturer: Vestas and original 250 kW turbine supplier not consistently named in current primary sources
equipment.detail: All five turbines are being repowered using refurbished nacelles and restored foundations, towers, and blades; the work began in September 2024 and is due in 2027.

The KIREIP system used an advanced lead-acid battery rated about 3 MW/1.5-1.6 MWh. Hydro Tasmania's 2024 repowering announcement confirms a new battery is included but does not publish its chemistry, supplier, power, or energy rating.
Two large rotating flywheels provide inertia and bridge sudden renewable shortfalls while diesel engines start only when extended support is required.

Hydro Tasmania's hybrid control system coordinates generation, battery, flywheels, resistor, and demand-side devices fast enough to move autonomously between diesel-on and extended diesel-off operation.

technicalDetails.detail: Advanced lead acid battery, flywheel

Battery and rotating flywheel systems supply frequency/voltage support and fault response when conventional generators are off. The dynamic resistor absorbs sudden excess renewable power, and sub-second demand response helps rebalance the isolated grid.

The original diesel-only system consumed about 4.5 million liters/year. APEC reports roughly 2.6 million liters after KIREIP, while Hydro's 2024 annual report expresses the decade result as 2.1 million liters and 5,700 tCO2 saved annually. These are compatible baselines/rounding, not exact live telemetry.

The 2023 solar farm and 2024-2027 wind/battery work post-date the original KIREIP inventory. Current total solar cannot safely be calculated by simply adding 470 kW and 1.5 MW without confirmation that all earlier arrays remain in service.

Earlier dual-axis PV and concentrated-solar trials were abandoned, and a 2003 vanadium-redox-flow battery failed and was decommissioned before the advanced lead-acid KIREIP battery. Those superseded technologies should not appear in a current equipment total.

Hydro Tasmania is reusing the five wind-turbine towers, foundations, and blades where possible and installing restored nacelles refurbished in Denmark. The $11.5 million program is intended to extend wind-farm life by at least ten years and includes battery replacement.

kings-contrivance-village-center.summary: Kings Contrivance Village Center in Columbia, Maryland was one of two public-purpose microgrids proposed by Baltimore Gas and Electric in December 2015. The Kings Contrivance site would have used 2 MW of natural-gas generation, islanded during major distribution outages, to serve a cluster of grocery, pharmacy, fuel, restaurant, bank, and other critical commercial services. The Maryland Public Service Commission denied the two-site proposal without prejudice on July 19, 2016. It was never an operating 3 MW microgrid, and it included no solar or battery storage.
kings-contrivance-village-center.status: Denied and not built. Maryland PSC Order 87669 rejected the proposal and Rider 12 tariff while allowing BGE to submit a redesigned project later. The reviewed PSC record and subsequent NREL regulatory case summary show no approval for this site; no later BGE or PSC evidence of a Kings Contrivance resubmission, construction, or operation was located.

equipment.detail: Two megawatts of small-scale natural-gas generation were specified functionally, but generator count, manufacturer, model, heat rate, emissions controls, and black-start equipment were not identified in the PSC order.

The design would disconnect the commercial-service footprint from BGE's distribution system during an extended outage and energize selected critical businesses. Control/protection vendors and switching topology were not disclosed.

None proposed. The Commission specifically criticized the absence of renewable generation, combined heat and power, and storage as a lost opportunity for fuel diversity and forward-looking demonstration value.

technicalDetails.detail: The microgrid was intended to keep grocery, pharmacy, fuel, restaurant, bank, and similar commercial services open during significant or extended outages, potentially serving residents who could travel to the village center.

BGE had not quantified customer-interruption/resilience benefits, conducted a cost-benefit analysis, justified socializing all risk through a surcharge, pursued grants or beneficiary investment, defined a major event, or shown that customers could reach and resupply the site during disasters.

BGE selected the two sites without initial customer, county, or state-agency participation, relied on 2010-2012 SAIFI/SAIDI data, did not explain factor weighting, and did not establish a competitive third-party generation process.

The 2-3 MW language in the order describes the range across two proposed sites: Kings Contrivance was 2 MW and Edmonson Village was 3 MW. The recovered 3 MW value for Kings Contrivance is therefore incorrect.

Denial was without prejudice, meaning BGE could file a better-supported pilot later; it was not an authorization to construct. Absent a later approved filing, the correct project status remains denied/unbuilt rather than planned or operational.

kirtland-afb.summary: Kirtland Air Force Base hosts a 250 kW, ten-node, bipolar-DC research microgrid that links six temporary-lodging duplexes, a community building, and Sandia National Laboratories test facilities. The first phase entered sustained autonomous operation in December 2019 and combines rooftop PV, distributed and central battery storage, natural-gas backup generation, electric-vehicle charging, bidirectional utility exchange, and hierarchical controls. A separate Air Force Research Laboratory contract modification announced in May 2025 is intended to add generation, storage, and multi-microgrid control for Civil Engineering facilities; public evidence does not establish that expansion as commissioned.
kirtland-afb.status: The original Emera/BlockEnergy-Sandia pilot is operational: DOE reported autonomous operation from December 15, 2019, Sandia reported a second successful year in 2021, and a 2023 technical paper described the fielded system as operating since 2019. In May 2025, AFRL added $3.5 million to Concurrent Technologies Corporation's contract, bringing that separate expansion effort to $8.5 million. The announcement describes work to be performed, not a completed second phase. No public completion notice, 2024-2026 annual production, outage record, storage health, or realized cost savings was located.

specifications.value: 250 kW, ten nodes, on a single bipolar ±375 VDC common bus; the field network can be configured radially or as a loop and uses high-impedance grounding

specifications.label: Solar photovoltaic capacity
specifications.value: 100 kW of rooftop PV in the original project

specifications.label: Battery rating conflict
specifications.value: BlockEnergy publishes 220 kWh, while the project's consulting engineer publishes 200 kW/200 kWh. The reviewed sources do not reconcile whether these are nominal versus usable ratings or design revisions.

specifications.label: Utility-interface capability
specifications.value: Up to 100 kW bidirectional import/export at the central energy park in the research architecture

equipment.detail: Each residential power block combines local battery storage, power conversion, grid-forming capability, controls, protection, and up to a 10 kW bidirectional DC/DC interface. Each node can island locally.
Central storage and generation establish the DC-bus voltage and coordinate power across the distributed nodes and utility connection.

The original system includes rooftop solar, batteries, natural-gas generation, and EV chargers. Public sources reviewed do not disclose the gas-generator manufacturer/model/rating, PV module/inverter models, charger ratings, or battery cell vendor/chemistry.

technicalDetails.detail: DOE reported import and export to the bulk grid, black start, and long-duration self-sustained operation. During the first six months, the system exported excess renewable power rather than only serving an isolated test load.

Distributed nanogrids can form their own local AC service while the central energy park sets and coordinates the shared DC bus. BlockEnergy describes real-time, data-driven control; public material does not identify a commercial controller model or publish the optimization code.

The custom protection system detects high current slew rate, isolates a fault, and discharges the affected bus. Sandia's field tests report one fault case clearing current in under 1 ms and describe a design target to detect and disable within 2 ms.

CTC's later contract is described as building on the existing testbed to serve Civil Engineering facilities, add generation and storage, coordinate multiple DC microgrids, extend off-grid duration, and reduce utility purchases. No capacities, vendors, construction milestone, or commissioning date were published.

No independently audited annual energy mix, renewable output, fuel consumption, outage-support duration, battery degradation, availability, or realized savings through July 2026 was located. The 200 versus 220 kWh storage discrepancy also remains unresolved.

kissi-county-kenya-microgrid.summary: The database label misspells Kisii County as 'Kissi' and appears to compress Powerhive's multi-village western Kenya portfolio into one project. Powerhive's initial four-village pilot totaled 80 kW, while a later technical market study lists six named Kisii systems totaling 100.25 kW, including a 47.25 kW Barane site that likely explains—but does not conclusively prove—the legacy 47 kW figure. The company subsequently expanded across Kisii and neighboring Nyamira using solar PV, batteries, smart meters, cloud controls, and mobile-money prepayment.
kissi-county-kenya-microgrid.status: Portfolio with documented operating sites, but not one 47 kW plant. Powerhive operated a four-village pilot for more than two years before receiving a 25-year generation-and-distribution license in February 2015. A REPP case-study snapshot reports 24 operating mini-grids totaling 0.9 MW and serving 24,806 people, and 2023 field reporting described 23 stations serving roughly 7,000 homesteads in Kisii and Nyamira. Powerhive's current corporate site says it has deployed more than 30 off-grid sites since 2013, but does not provide a site-by-site 2026 availability list. The exact present status of each original village system is therefore not independently verified.

organizations.role: Lead investor in the announced 2015 scale-up vehicle, with Powerhive as the minority partner; the 100-village target was a plan rather than a completed-asset count.

organizations.name: Energy Regulatory Commission / Energy and Petroleum Regulatory Authority
organizations.role: Kenyan regulator that granted and now records Powerhive's generation-and-distribution license for Kisii and Nyamira.

organizations.role: Provided financial and technical backing and advanced thin-film PV modules for the initial pilot program.

organizations.name: Powerhive
organizations.role: Publicly named participant; role not specified in the recovered record

organizations.role: Developer, licensed generator/distributor, technology supplier, and portfolio operator; the related legal names appear in licensing and financing sources.

organizations.name: Renewable Energy Performance Platform
organizations.role: Approved a $3 million results-based financing facility in May 2018 for the broader Powerhive portfolio.

specifications.label: Initial pilot portfolio
specifications.value: Four villages, 80 kW total, serving more than 1,500 people after more than two years of pilot operation as of April 2015

specifications.label: Named Kisii systems in technical market study
specifications.value: Kirwa C 10 kW, Mtangamano 21 kW, Bogeka 10 kW, Mokomoni 1.5 kW, Nyamondo 10.5 kW, and Barane 47.25 kW; 100.25 kW combined in the study's table. This later six-site boundary should not be substituted for the earlier four-site 80 kW pilot.

specifications.label: Licensed ceiling and geography
specifications.value: 3 MW authorized solar-PV generation/distribution capacity across Kisii and Nyamira under license GD.01.15, granted February 16, 2015 for 25 years. This is a regulatory authorization, not evidence that 3 MW was installed.

specifications.label: Expansion target versus reported achievement
specifications.value: The 2015 investment plan targeted 100 villages, 1 MW, roughly 20,000 households, and 90,000 people. REPP later reported 24 operating grids totaling 0.9 MW and 24,806 people gaining first access at its reporting snapshot.

specifications.label: Pilot energy mix
specifications.value: Powerhive described the initial systems as 100% renewable; later portfolio descriptions identify solar PV with distributed battery storage but do not publish a measured annual renewable fraction for every site.

equipment.detail: First Solar supplied advanced PV modules to the pilot. The reviewed sources do not disclose module series, site-by-site module counts, inverter vendors, or final array sizes beyond the listed site capacities.
Customer meters support remote monitoring and prepaid electricity purchases, including mobile-money payment.

Honeycomb provides cloud-based monitoring and control; SWARM supports site assessment and deployment. Public sources do not publish controller hardware, communications redundancy, dispatch logic, or cybersecurity architecture.

Powerhive and REPP describe distributed batteries as part of the mini-grid design. Chemistry, manufacturer, power, energy, usable depth of discharge, and replacement history are not disclosed for the named Kisii sites.

technicalDetails.detail: The legacy 47 kW value aligns closely with Barane's 47.25 kW listing, but the record name refers to a county-wide portfolio. Because no source explicitly equates the database record with Barane, that correspondence remains a likely explanation rather than a confirmed alias.
The systems use smart meters, cloud supervision, and prepaid mobile payments so customers can buy small increments of electricity and the operator can monitor village assets remotely.

REPP reports 24,806 people receiving first access and 875 cumulative tonnes of CO2-equivalent avoided at its snapshot. The latter should not be read as a current annual savings value or independently audited performance for Kisii County alone.

A May 2023 Kenyan report described 23 Powerhive stations across Kisii and Nyamira serving about 7,000 homesteads, while a 2023 university field account described continued smart-metered service and customer experience at Miwani. Neither source provides a 2026 site-level availability table.

No reconciled commissioning list, present battery inventory, site-level annual generation, outage duration, losses, tariffs, collected revenue, or measured fuel displacement through July 2026 was located. The public record also does not reconcile the original four pilot villages, the six-site technical table, and the later 23/24-grid portfolio snapshots.

kodiak-alaska-microgrid.summary: Kodiak's 'microgrid' is the islanded Kodiak Electric Association utility grid, not a single 75 MW facility. Its core resources are the approximately 34 MW Terror Lake hydro plant, six 1.5 MW GE wind turbines, a 3 MW lithium-ion battery system upgraded in 2017, two 1 MW ABB flywheels, and multiple diesel plants retained for backup. KEA generated more than 99% renewable electricity in 2023; a regional current profile gives 99.8%, while KEA's member handbook describes the typical mix as approximately 81% hydro, 18% wind, and 1% diesel.
kodiak-alaska-microgrid.status: Operational. KEA's 2023 annual report says the cooperative generated more than 99% of its power from renewables and delivered another safe, reliable year. The cooperative raised rates 12.5% on April 1, 2024 after roughly 30 years without an increase, citing inflation, sharply higher equipment costs, lower fish-processing sales, and debt—not technical failure of the hybrid grid. Public sources reviewed do not provide a newer full-year generation mix, storage state of health, or unit-by-unit availability through July 2026.

specifications.label: Wind capacity
specifications.value: 9 MW total from six GE 1.5 MW turbines on Pillar Mountain

specifications.label: Battery storage
specifications.value: 3 MW. The original 2012 advanced lead-acid system was 750 kWh according to IRENA, not the 2 MWh repeated in some secondary accounts. Younicos replaced the lead-acid racks with lithium-ion batteries in 2017 but did not disclose the upgraded energy capacity in its commissioning release.

specifications.label: Flywheel storage
specifications.value: 2 MW total from two 1 MW ABB PowerStore units

specifications.label: Diesel backup inventory
specifications.value: 37.2 MW in Alaska Energy Data Gateway's 2021 inventory: Kodiak 18.3 MW, Nyman's 10 MW, Swampy Acres 8.4 MW, and Port Lions 0.5 MW. This dated inventory is more specific than the legacy 33 MW aggregate.

equipment.detail: Terror Lake has three approximately 11.25 MW turbine-generators and reservoir storage that can absorb excess wind by reducing hydro output. NOAA estimates about 135 GWh annual generation and says the project supplies roughly 80-85% of Kodiak's electricity.
Six 1.5 MW units, 9 MW total. Three were added in 2012 to the original three-turbine installation.

The current 3 MW system uses lithium-ion batteries installed in 2017 as a replacement for Xtreme Power's original advanced lead-acid racks. Public commissioning material does not publish current MWh, cell supplier, rack model, or current degradation.
Two 1 MW flywheels respond to rapid frequency and voltage excursions, integrate the port's large electric crane, and reduce high-frequency cycling stress on the battery system.
Networked distributed controllers coordinate resources peer-to-peer rather than through one single controller.

technicalDetails.detail: The Kodiak microgrid is an islanded grid, meaning there is no interconnection with any outside grid.
Kodiak’s 3 MW (2 MWh) gel lead-acid battery contributes to the stability of the system, and KEA recently added two 1 MW flywheel systems.
The flywheel will take over some of the workload from the battery, which currently charges and discharges around 1,000 times each day.
The pairing of multiple types of storage that operate on different time scales effectively matches system needs, especially when variable renewable penetration is high.

The flywheels handle microsecond-scale changes and the crane's sharp load swings, allowing the battery to respond over longer seconds-to-minutes intervals; hydro governors and reservoir storage provide slower balancing and bulk renewable energy.

The often-cited 3 MW/2 MWh advanced lead-acid description is not supported by IRENA's detailed case study, which gives 3 MW/750 kWh. More importantly, that chemistry was retired: Younicos commissioned a 3 MW lithium-ion replacement in November 2017 without publishing its MWh rating.

APEC's 2023 review reports no major reliability events caused by high renewable penetration, and KEA's 2023 annual report describes continued safe and reliable operation. Neither provides standardized outage indices or unit-availability data.

KEA paid down more than $8 million of debt in 2023 but implemented a 12.5% rate increase in April 2024 after roughly three decades without an increase. The utility attributed the change to inflation, equipment costs, debt service, and reduced fish-processing demand, not to diesel-price exposure returning as the dominant cost.

No public post-2023 full-year mix, diesel gallons, curtailment, BESS state of health, round-trip efficiency, flywheel availability, outage indices, or realized annual storage savings through July 2026 was located.

kotzebue.summary: Kotzebue Electric Association operates a diesel-wind-solar-battery district-energy system in Qikiktagruq (Kotzebue), Alaska. A federal FY2026 strategic plan provides an unusually complete as-operated inventory: 12.111 MW of diesel engines, 1.096 MW of solar PV, only 1.8 MW of presently functional wind capacity out of a larger aging fleet, and a 1.225 MW/950 kWh Saft lithium-ion battery, plus recovered heat, electric boilers, and residential thermal storage. In 2024 the system delivered 21.66 GWh, with 20% from non-diesel resources and 1.219 million gallons of diesel consumed.
kotzebue.status: Operational but still approximately 80% diesel by annual electricity in 2024. Two 900 kW EWT turbines were functional in the FY2026 inventory; the older Vestas, Northwind, and fifteen AOC turbines were listed as needing repair. The 2015 battery remains in service but was described to Alaska legislators in April 2025 as nearing end of life. Proposed additions—two 1 MW turbines, 700 kW solar, and a 4 MW/8-12 MWh grid-forming battery—were not yet built in the strategic plan, and a $20 million EPA grant for the Native Village's wind project was terminated in May 2025. Funding and final scope therefore remain uncertain.

equipment.detail: Two functional 900 kW turbines, installed in 2012. The separate older Vestas, Northwind, and AOC fleet is listed as needing repair.
A 950 kWh lithium-ion container with a cold-climate package including insulation and hydronic heating; the FY2026 plan rates the grid interface at 1.225 MW.
The 1.076 MW utility portfolio uses bifacial modules. Public sources reviewed do not identify module, inverter, racking, or tracker manufacturers and models.
Six diesel units supply firm capacity; recovered engine heat is circulated for useful thermal loads. Individual engine models and current hours/availability were not published in the strategic plan.

technicalDetails.detail: Battery – Saft Intensium Max+20M

Despite decades of renewable integration, 80% of 2024 electricity remained diesel-supplied. The plan reports that annual diesel electricity has fallen about 20% since 1995, which is a long-term system trend rather than a storage-only savings claim.

The BESS provides spinning reserve and frequency support so wind can displace diesel without keeping as much engine reserve online. Saft attributes broader fuel and cost savings to the integrated wind-storage system, but its case study does not isolate battery-only causality.

Electric boilers and residential thermal-storage heaters absorb otherwise excess wind energy. ACEP's 2024 work evaluated household heaters that charge when excess wind is available, coupling electric and thermal dispatch.

The strategic plan evaluates two new 1 MW wind turbines, 700 kW of solar, and a 4 MW/8-12 MWh lithium-ion battery capable of roughly two hours of community supply and diesel-off operation. These are planning values, not installed capacity.

The FY2026 plan flags uncertain funding, and public-radio reporting says EPA terminated a $20 million grant for the Native Village of Kotzebue's two-turbine wind project in May 2025. Other storage/solar funding streams should not be assumed canceled without separate evidence.

No 2025 full-year energy mix, turbine-by-turbine availability, BESS state of health, current usable energy, renewable curtailment, outage indices, or final financing/commissioning schedule for the expansion was located.

kpcl-mandya.summary: No authoritative evidence was found for the legacy description of a 100 kW KPCL microgrid in Mandya, Karnataka. The verifiable KPCL assets are grid-connected photovoltaic plants: a 5 MW Belakavadi project awarded to BHEL in 2011 and registered under the Clean Development Mechanism, plus a separate 10 MW 'KPCL-Mandya' project documented in BHEL engineering and commissioning records. Later public sources describe 15 MW of KPCL solar generation in the Shivanasamudra/Mandya area. None of the reviewed sources documents islanding, battery storage, diesel backup, or a microgrid controller.
kpcl-mandya.status: Legacy project identity unresolved; related KPCL grid-connected solar assets are operational. BHEL records the 5 MW and 10 MW projects as commissioned, April 2024 reporting says KPCL was generating 15 MW from the Shivanasamudra solar plants during a hydro shutdown, and an October 2025 university visit documented operating panels, inverters, control rooms, and grid integration. These sources do not prove that the database's 100 kW record was a real standalone asset, so it should not be presented as an operating microgrid.

Place-name transliterations and grouping differ across sources, so this should be treated as portfolio context rather than proof of one 15 MW plant.

equipment.detail: The 2014 design document specifies 280 Wp monocrystalline modules with approximately 43 V open-circuit voltage, 8.5 A short-circuit current, 35 V maximum-power voltage, and 8 A maximum-power current; 20 modules form a string.

Sixteen 630 kWp three-phase grid-connected PCUs feeding eight three-winding step-up transformers. The reviewed BHEL request describes required functionality but does not establish the selected PCU manufacturer/model.
None established. No battery, diesel generator, transfer switch, islanding relay, black-start system, or microgrid-controller vendor/model was found for this record.

technicalDetails.detail: BHEL and UNFCCC repeatedly describe the relevant projects as grid-connected or grid-interactive solar PV. That is evidence against silently retaining the 'microgrid' classification, but it does not reveal how the original 100 kW database entry was created.

The 5 MW Belakavadi project and the 10 MW KPCL-Mandya project are separately documented. A later 15 MW regional total is consistent with their sum, but inconsistent location spellings prevent a stronger one-to-one site mapping without original KPCL asset registers.

During low-water conditions in April 2024, reporting said hydro generation at Shivanasamudra had stopped while KPCL's local solar plants continued producing 15 MW. A supervised university visit in October 2025 observed PV arrays, inverters, control rooms, safety systems, and grid-integration practices.

No primary KPCL source was located that reconciles the site names, confirms the 100 kW record, publishes recent annual generation/capacity factor, or documents storage, curtailment, outage performance, or microgrid operation.

kwig-alaska-microgrid.summary: Kwigillingok's community system is a high-penetration wind-diesel-heat microgrid built around five 95 kW Windmatic 17-S turbines, electric thermal-storage heaters, a lithium-ion BESS added in 2014-2015, and Frontier Power Systems' e+micro supervisory control and SCADA. The BESS/control phase enabled diesel-off operation during sufficient wind. Supplier claims say diesel generators can remain off at least 33% of the year and fuel-cost savings reach 50% or better, but those figures are not accompanied by a dated independent measurement report.
kwig-alaska-microgrid.status: Core system operational in the latest explicit system accounts, but turbine condition and current availability are incompletely documented. A 2019 DOE assistance report found one of five turbines inoperable and only 20 of the original 27 thermal-storage units still present. Alaska Energy Authority described all five 1980s-era turbines as needing more efficient blades in 2022; its October 2024 portfolio report still listed the blade-upgrade grant in construction with status 'Development.' No completion notice or 2025-2026 turbine-by-turbine availability record was located.

equipment.detail: Five 95 kW units, 17 m rotor diameter, 24.4 m hub height.
System master, distributed asset controllers, SCADA, and fast load-regulator functions integrate wind, diesel, thermal storage, and battery resources.
Residential heat-storage units absorb surplus wind energy; manufacturer and heater model are not stated in the reviewed state project sheet.
Integrated in 2015 to enable diesel-off operation; the recovered record supplies power and energy values but reviewed public sources do not identify the battery or inverter manufacturer.
Five 95 kW machines. By 2019 one was inoperable; the 2022 upgrade narrative says the 1980s blade design was inefficient and not optimized for Kwigillingok's wind regime.

Automotive-derived lithium-ion batteries store surplus wind and support village-scale wind-only operation. The reviewed sources do not disclose a current battery maker, exact cell model, current usable energy, or replacement history.
Supervisory controls, integration hardware, software, and SCADA coordinate wind, diesel generators, the BESS, load regulation, and thermal-storage stoves.

Electric thermal-storage stoves absorb excess wind energy in homes. The system-level sources do not publish individual stove ratings, manufacturers, present connected count beyond the 2019 audit, or verified household savings for Kwigillingok alone.

technicalDetails.detail: Wind Diesel Thermal Storage System
Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) added in 2014 (260 kW x 15
At high wind output, the smart-grid controller diverts surplus electricity to metered residential thermal-storage heaters instead of curtailing all excess generation.
Frontier reports that the battery and control-system expansion allowed the community to shut down diesel generation during suitable wind and load conditions.
A 2019 assistance report found one of five turbines inoperable and arranged peer troubleshooting and Yup'ik-language resident training on thermal-storage use.
DOE's 30% power-plant fuel reduction is a multi-community Chaninik result and is not presented as a Kwigillingok-only measured value.

Frontier says the 2015 BESS integration allowed a village in this early Alaska program to shut all diesel generators off and its project list identifies Kwigillingok as the 2014-2015 BESS site. IES explicitly says Kwigillingok can run the whole village from wind and claims diesel-off operation for at least 33% of the year.

IES claims fuel-cost savings of 50% or better, but its page gives no measurement period, baseline, diesel gallons, or independent audit. DOE's approximately 30% fuel-reduction account covers the wider Chaninik multi-village program and must not be presented as a Kwigillingok-only measured result.

DOE technical assistance documented one unavailable turbine, fewer thermal-storage units than originally installed, and operator troubleshooting/training needs. This is evidence of partial degradation, not proof the entire microgrid had failed.

AEA proposed replacing older rotor blades with SERI Thin Airfoil designs to improve production. In October 2024 the grant remained in 'Development' within the construction portfolio, so improved production should not be counted as realized.

No verified 2025-2026 turbine availability, completed blade-upgrade notice, annual wind generation, diesel gallons, current storage state of health, usable kWh, outage indices, or independently measured savings was located.

kithnos.summary: The slug uses 'Kithnos,' but the island is normally transliterated Kythnos. Public descriptions also combine two different systems: the island-wide diesel-wind-PV grid and the much smaller permanently islanded Gaidouromantra (also Gaidouromandra) holiday-home microgrid. The latter began operating in 2001, historically used roughly 10-11 kWp PV with lead-acid batteries and diesel backup, and was modernized through Kythnos Smart Island and RE-EMPOWERED. A May 2025 deployment report lists its current resources as 20.8 kW PV, 3 kW wind, and 22 kW backup diesel, with new metering, communications, SCADA, forecasting, demand response, and multi-vector energy management.
kithnos.status: Both boundaries remain active in the latest located evidence. The EU Clean Energy for Islands secretariat reported Kythnos Smart Island completed on June 21, 2024, including renovation of the Gaidouromantra off-grid system. RE-EMPOWERED's May 2025 deliverable says all tools were deployed, identified issues resolved, and demonstration had been ongoing since October 2024. It also lists the separate whole-island system at 5.2 MW diesel and 908.65 kW renewable capacity. No later 2026 production or reliability report was located.

equipment.detail: Fully automatic 1998 control system coordinating diesel starts/stops, wind, PV, battery conversion, phase shifting, and fast load control.
Central grid master for the 2001 modular AC-coupled PV-battery and PV-battery-diesel systems.
500 kW turbine installed in 1998.
Five 12-cylinder turbo-diesel sets rated 400 kW each.
One 500 kW turbine in the late-1990s island hybrid. The current 908.65 kW renewable aggregate does not by itself confirm the present availability of this exact machine.

Five 400 kW diesel units in SMA's historical project description. The newer whole-island inventory gives 5.2 MW total diesel without unit-level models, so the old fleet should not be treated as the complete current inventory.

The documented 12-year configuration used three SI5048 units in the main three-phase system and one SI4500 in the secondary subsystem. The 2025 report does not confirm whether all original inverters remain installed.
A locally manufactured 3 kW turbine was installed and commissioned at Gaidouromantra and maintained in September 2023; a commercial model is not identified.
An Ethernet gateway, extended smart metering, environmental sensors, data logging, fiber/communications repairs, and zenon data-concentrator/SCADA support remote monitoring and control.

technicalDetails.detail: Assets: Solar, Storage
It is a small village scale autonomous microgrid, composed of a 3-phase low-voltage network, solar PV generation, battery storage, and a backup generator.
There are 10 kW of PV at two locations, a nominal 53 kWh battery bank, and a 5 kW diesel genset.
When power is demanded by customers than the PV systems can directly provide, one or of the 3.6 kW battery inverters is activated.
The battery inverters can operate in isochronous or droop mode.

The island system and Gaidouromantra system have different owners, loads, capacities, and histories. Capacities such as the island's 5.2 MW diesel fleet must not be combined with the remote site's 20.8 kW PV as though they were one physical bus.

The permanently islanded system began in 2001 and served vacation homes with PV, batteries, and automatic diesel backup. The 12-year paper records a 2009 main-battery replacement and maintenance problems such as frequent watering of the original lead-acid bank.

RE-EMPOWERED deployed ecoEMS, ecoMicrogrid, ecoPlanning, ecoDR, ecoPlatform, ecoMonitor, ecoCommunity, and ecoResilience tools. Functions include forecasts, outage/fault detection, demand response, SCADA/MQTT integration, remote disconnection of noncritical loads, and joint electric/HVAC optimization.

technicalDetails.category: Smart Island completion and continuing demonstration
technicalDetails.detail: The Kythnos Smart Island project was declared complete in June 2024, including renovation at Gaidouromantra. RE-EMPOWERED separately reported that its tool demonstration continued from October 2024, so 'completed' refers to the construction/program milestone rather than the end of all research operation.

The 2025 deliverable characterizes Gaidouromantra as supplied by PV and batteries since 2001 while also listing 22 kW of backup diesel. The defensible interpretation is a renewable-led microgrid with emergency/low-state-of-charge backup, not evidence that diesel can never run.

Current battery chemistry, manufacturer, installed and usable kWh, state of health, annual renewable fraction, diesel runtime, outage indices, PV/wind output, and post-October-2024 demonstration results were not published in the reviewed sources.

la-gomera.summary: The La Gomera record is best understood as Endesa/Unelco's STORE flywheel demonstration, not the island's entire roughly 22 MW electricity system. ABB/PowerCorp supplied a 0.5 MW PowerStore flywheel at Playa Santiago, Alajeró, to provide automatic frequency and voltage support. The original installation entered service in December 2013, completed its experimental program, and was definitively disconnected in November 2018. A 2020 Canary Islands authorization allowed relocation to the El Palmar diesel plant; Endesa later describes a La Gomera flywheel operating as an auxiliary grid-quality asset, but a commissioning notice that conclusively links the relocated unit was not located.
la-gomera.status: Original Playa Santiago demonstration retired; probable relocation/continued auxiliary use remains incompletely documented. The official 2020 authorization records the 2018 definitive disconnection, approves construction of a 0.5 MW flywheel installation at El Palmar, and cites measured improvement in frequency response and grid quality during the pilot. Endesa's current storage page says a flywheel operates on La Gomera, creating evidence of later operation but without a commissioning date or serial-number trail. Separately, the Tenerife-La Gomera submarine interconnection entered service on April 23, 2026; Spanish regulation now treats the two islands as one non-peninsular electrical system, so La Gomera is no longer an electrically isolated island grid.

equipment.detail: Low-speed flywheel rated 0.5 MW and 18 MW-seconds in the project technical paper, used for rapid active-power absorption/injection and automatic frequency and voltage support.
Two IGBT-based inverters connect the flywheel to the grid in the technical design. Specific converter product numbers were not located.

The 2026 link includes a double-circuit submarine cable and the Chío (Tenerife) and El Palmar (La Gomera) substations, changing the operating boundary from a standalone island to an interconnected two-island system.

technicalDetails.detail: Flywheel

The flywheel automatically absorbs short wind-power surges and supplies short lulls, emulating primary frequency regulation and supporting voltage. Its seconds-scale energy is suited to stabilization, not long-duration island backup.

In its 2018 notice to the Canary government, the operator reported a notable improvement in system frequency response and grid quality, then declared the STORE experimental phase complete and definitively disconnected the Playa Santiago unit.

The 2020 resolution authorizes construction at El Palmar and Endesa's later storage page says a La Gomera flywheel is operating as an auxiliary service. However, no located public commissioning record explicitly closes the chain from the disconnected Playa Santiago unit to an operating relocated asset, so current physical status is not fully verified.

A July 30, 2023 fire affected seven of nine generator sets at El Palmar and caused an island-wide outage; the Canary government later said full restoration took more than 60 hours and imposed a €12.1 million sanction on Unelco. The sources do not attribute the event to the flywheel, and a 5 kWh stabilizer could not provide long-duration replacement generation.

The Tenerife-La Gomera interconnection entered service April 23, 2026. A June 2026 ministerial order formally redefined the islands as one electrical system, ending La Gomera's status as a standalone island grid even if local diesel and flywheel assets continue operating.

No definitive relocated-flywheel commissioning certificate, current availability, maintenance history, measured response-event data, annual avoided curtailment, or quantified fuel savings was located. Current island energy mix after the April 2026 interconnection also was not yet available.

la-palma.summary: La Palma's documented STORE project is a fast-response ultracapacitor installation at Endesa's Los Guinchos diesel power station, not a generation microgrid for the whole island. The 4 MW, 20 MW-s system is connected to the isolated island grid at 20 kV and injects power for roughly five to six seconds after a generator trip, buying time for generator governors and reducing under-frequency load shedding. Ingeteam supplied the conversion, control, and integration package under Endesa's STORE research program.
la-palma.status: Installed and demonstrated around 2013–2014. The Canary Islands government published Endesa subsidiary Unión Eléctrica de Canarias Generación's application for definitive authorization of the 4 MW installation in 2019, and Endesa continues to describe the Los Guinchos ultracapacitor as an island-storage reference. No recent public source reports 2025–2026 availability, cycling, or maintenance condition, so present operational performance is not independently confirmed.
la-palma.historicalLocation: La Palma, Santa Cruz de Tenerife, Canary Islands, Spain

technicalDetails.detail: After the loss of a generating unit, the controller commands near-instantaneous active-power injection to slow the frequency decline while the surviving diesel generators' governors increase output.
The short power burst was designed to reduce or avoid under-frequency load shedding on La Palma's isolated electric system.
The converters can provide continuous reactive-power support up to 2 MVAr, adding voltage-control value beyond the seconds-long active-power discharge.

The ultracapacitor is storage and produces no net electricity. Its 4 MW figure should not be added to La Palma's generation capacity or treated as renewable generation; it stabilizes a diesel-dominated isolated grid that also includes renewable resources.

A 2019 official notice identified a 4 MW ultracapacitor installation at Central Diésel Los Guinchos and opened public review for definitive authorization, indicating a transition from research installation toward permanent-plant status.
Current corporate pages continue to cite the installation, but they do not disclose recent operating hours, outages, state of health, or whether the full original bank remains in service.

lac-des-mille-lacs-first-nation.summary: Lac des Mille Lacs First Nation commissioned an off-grid solar-battery-diesel system for its community complex and roundhouse on July 28, 2017. JAZZ Solar's current project portfolio reports 97 kW of PV and 250 kWh of battery storage, with solar also diverted to domestic-hot-water preheating. The Nation reported in early 2018 that the system had nearly eliminated diesel use since launch, except for brief operation after four to six sunless days; that is strong early performance evidence, but not an audited present-day energy mix.
lac-des-mille-lacs-first-nation.status: Commissioned in 2017 and still presented in JAZZ Solar's current portfolio as an off-grid project and long-term partnership. The latest detailed first-party operating account located is from February 2018. No recent public record provides battery state of health, annual solar fraction, diesel runtime, or confirms whether the original 2017 storage bank remains unchanged in 2026.

technicalDetails.detail: The documented system serves the First Nation's community complex and roundhouse. Available sources do not establish that every residence is electrically connected to this microgrid.
The Nation reported that the complex remained off diesel from late July 2017 into early 2018 except for a couple of short periods following four to six consecutive days without adequate sun.
The case study reports approximately three months of exclusively solar-battery operation after commissioning before seasonal solar conditions required backup generation.
PV first supplies facility loads and charges storage; surplus energy can be diverted to water heating, while diesel remains the firm backup for prolonged low-insolation events.

Early community testimony supports a very high solar share and near-elimination of diesel for the first months, but no audited annual percentage or recent fuel log is public. That 2017–2018 result should not be presented as a verified 2026 energy mix.

IESO's 2023 Indigenous Energy Support Program lists funding for development of a micro-utility operating system by Lac des Mille Lacs First Nation. It indicates continued energy-capacity building but does not specify replacement or expansion of the original PV-battery equipment.
