les-anglais-haiti-microgrid.summary: EarthSpark's Les Anglais microgrid is an operating solar-battery-diesel utility that grew from a 14-customer clean-energy pilot in 2012 into a town-scale smart grid. The rebuilt 2019 configuration centers on 93.3 kWp of PV, a 100 kW power-electronic hub, 205.5 kWh of Samsung lithium-ion storage, and a 30 kW Cummins diesel set. EarthSpark now reports more than 500 connected homes and businesses; the most detailed operating factsheet reports 471 connections, 37 streetlights, and average use of 250–325 kWh/day in July 2021.
les-anglais-haiti-microgrid.status: Operating. The original 2015 grid was heavily damaged by Hurricane Matthew in October 2016, including destruction of the distribution network and about 40% of its PV modules, while its core power electronics and battery survived. The project was relaunched in 2018 and received a new battery and power-electronics configuration in June 2019 for 24/7 service. EarthSpark's current site lists Les Anglais as operating, but no newer audited reliability, fuel-share, or battery-health dataset was found.

technicalDetails.detail: The SparkMeter microgrid metering system enables utilities to implement pre-payment as well as real-time monitoring and control on microgrids and central grids alike.
First prepay microgrid in Haiti
100 KW Power Management Hub
400 KW battery storage
300 solar PV panels on a ground-mount array.
72 solar PV panels on the roofs of the market structures.
EKo Pwòp began with 14 customers in 2012 and reached 54 by October 2013; the town-scale system opened in June 2015 with approximately 430 customers.
Hurricane Matthew destroyed the distribution grid and roughly 40% of the PV array in October 2016, but the protected battery and core power electronics survived.
The service relaunched in 2018. New power electronics and the current Samsung battery arrangement entered operation in June 2019, enabling 24-hour service and street lighting.

Solar supplies daytime loads and charges the battery; stored energy extends service after sunset, while the 30 kW diesel generator provides emergency and low-renewable backup. No current audited annual solar/diesel percentage is public.

SparkMeter supports prepaid service, time-varying prices, remotely adjustable customer power limits, usage analytics, and loss/theft detection, allowing the utility to manage scarcity without flat rolling blackouts.

EarthSpark's current overview says grid electricity can cost customers up to 80% less than the kerosene, candles, phone charging, and other prior energy services it displaces; this is a comparative claim, not a published current tariff schedule.
The detailed 90% availability figure and headline greater-than-98% reliability claim likely represent different reporting windows or metrics. The underlying factsheet does not reconcile them.

long-beach-microgrid-feasibility-study.summary: The City of Long Beach community microgrid was a 2016 NY Prize Stage 1 feasibility design, not an operating system. The concept linked civic, police, fire, water, wastewater, transit, housing, and shelter facilities with natural-gas generation, 300 kW of PV, 100 kW of battery power, and existing diesel backup. It was technically feasible in the study but economically dependent on resilience value and incentives; Long Beach is absent from NYSERDA's official Stage 2 award list, and no construction or commissioning evidence was found.
long-beach-microgrid-feasibility-study.status: Stage 1 feasibility study completed; unbuilt. Long Beach appears in NYSERDA's Stage 1 roster but not among the eleven Stage 2 awards. Classification as not advanced is an inference from the official award records plus the absence of subsequent construction or operating documentation.

technicalDetails.detail: The proposed microgrid would include combined heat and power, fuel cell, solar, and energy storage, combined with demand-management technology.
The proposed microgrid would include combined heat and power, fuel cell, solar, and energy storage.

The design covered City Hall, police and fire functions, wastewater and water treatment, Channel Park Homes/Michael Valente housing, the Ice Arena shelter, Long Beach LIRR station, and selected feeder loads.
A community-scale 15 kV-class network would coordinate multiple utility feeders and facility nodes, island from the PSEG Long Island system, and prioritize critical loads during outages.
The fuel cell and CHP were modeled as high-utilization resources, PV and batteries as variable/fast assets, and diesel or dual-fuel generators as resilience and peaking resources.
Battery systems and standby generators were intended to establish local voltage and frequency, restart other DER, and bridge transitions into islanded service.
The design was primarily natural-gas generation with smaller solar and battery components, plus diesel backup. All annual-output values are planning-model results, not measured generation.
The study found the project technically feasible but financially dependent on incentives, third-party structures, and the assumed frequency/severity of grid outages.
The project is present on the official Stage 1 list and absent from the Stage 2 list, supporting classification as an unbuilt feasibility concept.

long-island-community-microgrid-project-.summary: The Long Island Community Microgrid Project (LICMP) was Clean Coalition's NY Prize Stage 1 concept around PSEG Long Island's East Hampton GT substation: 15 MWdc of distributed solar, a centralized 5 MW/25 MWh battery, smaller facility batteries, and existing diesel peakers would serve 3,343 accounts and priority community facilities. The modeled solar fleet could supply roughly half of the study area's annual electricity. LICMP did not advance to an NY Prize Stage 2 award and was not built in this form.
long-island-community-microgrid-project-.status: Feasibility study completed in 2016; unbuilt. LICMP appears on NYSERDA's Stage 1 award roster but not the official Stage 2 list. A separate 76 kW solar/60 kW battery project at East Hampton Town Hall entered service in January 2023; it is not a reduced or commissioned phase of the 15 MW/25 MWh LICMP and must not be merged into this record.
long-island-community-microgrid-project-.historicalLocation: East Hampton Airport, Daniels Hole Road, Wainscott, NY, United States

technicalDetails.detail: Technology would include up to 15 MW of local solar, a 25-MWh energy storage system, and other distributed energy resources (DER).
The Long Island Community Microgrid Project will provision about 50% of its grid-area electric power requirements from local solar.
The result will be an optimized local energy system combining up to 15 megawatts (MW) of solar power with a 25-megawatt-hour energy storage system.
Technology would include up to 15 MW of local solar, a 25-MWh energy storage system, and other distributed energy resources (DER).
Through this effort, we uncovered over 30 potential sites for solar PV installations, with a collective capacity of than 32 megawatts (MW).
The proposed microgrid centered on East Hampton GT substation Bank 4 and two distribution feeders, combining a large centralized battery with distributed PV and critical-facility resources.
Priority sites included two Suffolk County Water Authority plants and the Springs Fire District; the architecture allowed expansion to airport and other emergency-service facilities.
The design called for feeder- or transmission-outage islanding, black start, tiered load priorities, and continued autonomous operation if communications with higher-level systems failed.
MC2 would use load, PV, weather, battery, and cost forecasts to schedule resources, manage state of charge, coordinate protection, and minimize use of diesel generators.

The feasibility model's approximately 50% annual solar share was prospective. Grid power would supply most of the balance in normal operation, while storage shifts solar and existing diesel provides emergency firm capacity.

Clean Coalition's analysis emphasized transmission deferral, resilience, and jobs, while the NY Prize standardized methodology tested different assumptions. All figures are planning estimates because the project was not constructed.
LICMP is listed among Stage 1 winners but not among NYSERDA's eleven Stage 2 awards; the unbuilt classification is therefore an evidence-based inference from official program records.
The 2023 Town Hall system was developed separately by NYPA and Inclusive Prosperity Capital. Its 76 kW/60 kW scale and municipal-building scope are not evidence that the 15 MW LICMP was commissioned.

longmeadow-headquarters.summary: ABB commissioned a grid-connected industrial microgrid at its 96,000 m² Longmeadow headquarters and manufacturing campus near Johannesburg in June 2016. The verified configuration is approximately 750–760 kWp of rooftop solar, an ABB PowerStore 1 MVA/380 kWh lithium-ion system, ABB Microgrid Plus control, and diesel backup. Official South African reporting in 2023 said solar supplied about 30% of the campus's roughly 2,400 MWh annual consumption; legacy claims of a wind turbine and flywheel do not match ABB's project documentation.
longmeadow-headquarters.status: Commissioned and publicly described as operating through at least 2023. ABB and successor Hitachi Energy continued to use Longmeadow as an operational microgrid reference after commissioning. Exact 2025–2026 battery state of health, diesel runtime, and recent reliability metrics are not public, so continued operation of every original component cannot be independently verified.

technicalDetails.detail: Stunning photo of 2800 solar panels being installed as part of ABB Longmeadow's MicroGrid project tx @drone_africa pic.twitter.com/TapslFluef
The system can operate grid-connected or islanded, with PowerStore establishing voltage and frequency and supporting transitions when the utility supply fails.
Microgrid Plus prioritizes available PV, uses battery power for rapid balancing and transition support, and starts diesel generation when longer-duration firm supply is required.
PowerStore provides grid-forming capability, frequency and voltage regulation, renewable smoothing, and power-quality support in addition to short-duration backup.
The 96,000 m² site includes ABB South Africa's headquarters, manufacturing, and logistics operations and employed approximately 1,000 people at commissioning.

The 2023 update's approximately 30% solar share is the strongest recent official energy-mix evidence. Grid electricity supplies much of the balance in normal operation, with diesel reserved for outages and resilience.
Public sources provide nameplate, architectural, and annual solar-share information but no recent battery degradation, diesel-fuel consumption, outage count, or islanding-success dataset.
Hitachi Energy's later references reflect the transfer of ABB's Power Grids business; they do not mean the Longmeadow campus changed from ABB ownership to Hitachi Energy ownership.

los-alamos-microgrid.summary: Los Alamos's microgrid was the NEDO Japan–U.S. Collaborative Smart Grid Demonstration on Los Alamos County's municipal distribution system, not an internal Los Alamos National Laboratory microgrid. The project paired a nominal 1 MW landfill PV plant with 1.8 MW/8.3 MWh of hybrid storage—NGK's 1 MW/6 MWh sodium-sulfur battery and Hitachi's 0.8 MW/2.3 MWh lead-acid system—under Toshiba's μEMS. Full demonstration operation began in September 2012 and the bilateral program concluded in 2014–2015.
los-alamos-microgrid.status: Historical demonstration, now partially retired. County procurement documents issued in October 2024 state that the 1 MW/6 MWh sodium-sulfur battery had been unmaintained, unheated, and uncharged for at least 36 months and was at end of useful life. The County approved a roughly US$945,240 removal contract in December 2024 with a May 31, 2025 target; public completion evidence was not located. The present status of the separate lead-acid bank and full original μEMS configuration is unclear, so the 2012 hybrid system should not be labeled currently intact or fully operational.

The μEMS assigned slower, sustained deviations to the 6 MWh sodium-sulfur bank and faster PV fluctuations to the 0.8 MW lead-acid system, while optimizing charge state and day-ahead dispatch.
Construction and staged testing began under the FY2009–2014 bilateral program; full operation was announced in September 2012 and the demonstration concluded around 2014–2015.

County reliability documentation says island operation required a complete feeder shutdown and reconfiguration before and after the islanded period, and that the feature had never been tested at the time of that plan. It should not be described as proven seamless transfer.
NEDO tested conditions in which PV supplied up to about 75% of energy at the demonstration site. This was a test condition, not a verified annual share for Los Alamos County's entire electric system.
The 2024 County RFP reports that the NAS cells had been unmaintained, unheated, and uncharged or self-discharged for at least 36 months, making the bank unavailable since at least roughly 2021.

County Council approved Renewance's removal contract in December 2024 with a May 2025 target. A public completion notice was not found, so removal should be described as contracted rather than definitively completed.
The host was Los Alamos County's municipal utility. LANL and Sandia participated in research, but the project is not evidence of a LANL-campus microgrid.
Recent large solar-storage developments in the region are separate projects. They must not be treated as upgrades that keep this original 1 MW NEDO demonstration operational.

mannheim-wallstadt-microgrid.summary: Mannheim-Wallstadt was a European field-test microgrid in an ecological residential estate of roughly 580 households and 1,200 inhabitants. MVV Energie developed it under the EU FP6 More Microgrids program to test demand response, medium- and low-voltage islanding, distributed PV and CHP, storage, and customer interfaces. Major trials ran from 2006 through 2009; public sources do not confirm that the exact research configuration remains in operation in 2026.
mannheim-wallstadt-microgrid.status: Historical research demonstration completed around 2009. The residential district remains part of Mannheim's electricity system and its lessons fed later smart-grid work, including Model City Mannheim, but no current primary documentation confirms continued operation of the original islanding controller, flywheel, Sunny Backup battery, fuel cell, or participant equipment as one microgrid. It should not simply be labeled currently operational.

technicalDetails.detail: The 'Washing with the Sun' customer trial ran in summer 2006, Energy Butler home trials followed in 2008, and final islanding field tests were carried out in 2009.
More than 20 households and the daycare center shifted washing and other flexible loads away from evening or cloudy periods toward times of local solar production.
The Kinderhaus low-voltage section, containing two PV systems, Sunny Backup storage, and controllable loads, was separated and resynchronized during field trials.
Published demonstration summaries report a brief frequency rise of about 2 Hz when loads were shed during islanding; control was described as imperfect but acceptable before reconnection.

Documented DER capacity was much smaller than the district's 80–230 kW load, so the public grid supplied most normal-operation energy. Island tests covered a limited subsection rather than continuously powering all 580 households.
The 1.2 kW flywheel and the approximately 10 kWh Sunny Backup battery are separate assets or phases. They should not be combined into one storage device or described as a 1.2 kWh battery.

Lessons from Wallstadt contributed to later Mannheim smart-grid activities such as Model City Mannheim, but those successor deployments are distinct projects and do not prove the original hardware remains active.
No 2025–2026 primary source was found that inventories the original DER, storage, controls, and islanding equipment or reports current microgrid operation.

mara-island.summary: This record is the stand-alone microgrid on Marado (also rendered Mara Island), South Korea's southernmost inhabited island near Jeju; it is not the neighboring Gapado system. A 150 kW photovoltaic plant using 2,010 GE 75 W modules was commissioned in December 2005 for Nam Jeju-gun and operated in parallel with the island diesel plant. Later Korean utility research describes two 75 kW PV blocks, two 600 kWh battery systems, a short-duration 50 kW EDLC device, supervisory controls, and diesel generation. A 2019 engineering study, however, says Marado's PV and battery equipment was not actually operating because surplus-power and operational problems had not been resolved. That paper's proposed 1 MVA medium-voltage DC connection to Gapado was a simulation/design proposal, not evidence of a built inter-island link.
mara-island.status: Historically commissioned, but the last detailed field-status source found reports the PV and 1.2 MWh battery plant inactive and represents Marado as diesel-supplied. No authoritative later source through July 2026 was found confirming that the renewable plant was restored, that the batteries were replaced, or that the proposed Gapado-Marado MVDC cable was built. Treat the original installation as real, its current renewable operating status as unverified, and the MVDC link as proposed only.

equipment.detail: 2,010 modules totaling approximately 150 kW.
Coordinates diesel minimum loading and battery dispatch using droop control; commercial EMS model not disclosed in the reviewed material.
2,010 open-ground modules supplied for the 150 kW plant.
EMS and gateway architecture interfacing static transfer switches, intelligent electronic devices, power-conversion systems, PV, batteries, diesel generators, and loads.
EDLC rated 50 kW for 5 seconds (250 kJ), used for rapid transient support in the documented architecture.

A three-phase four-leg inverter compensator was developed and tested using Marado operational data; it is research equipment and should not be assumed to have become the permanent production configuration.

technicalDetails.detail: The project is on Marado/Mara Island. Gapado appears in a later proposed interconnection study but is a different island microgrid.
The PV plant was commissioned in December 2005. KEPRI subsequently documented a fuller PV-battery-diesel EMS configuration, and the 2019 paper evaluated a possible MVDC interconnection.

Although the PV and battery assets had been installed, the 2019 authors state that they were not operating because the surplus-power problem and other operational issues had not been resolved. Installed capacity therefore must not be reported as demonstrated current renewable output.

The documented microgrid is an isolated diesel system augmented by AC-connected PV, battery power-conversion equipment, fast transient storage, switching/protection, and supervisory control. The proposed MVDC link would have connected it to Gapado, but the paper reports simulations rather than commissioning.
No current operator report, recent generation mix, fuel savings, battery chemistry, replacement history, or post-2019 operating update was found through July 2026.

marble-bar-and-nullagine-microgrid.summary: This is an umbrella record for two separate Horizon Power town systems in Western Australia's Pilbara: Marble Bar and Nullagine, about 88 km apart. The original combined portfolio comprised about 505-508 kW of tracked solar, 2.24 MW of diesel generation, and one 500 kW flywheel at each town under ABB/Powercorp controls. Marble Bar was integrated in May 2010 and Nullagine in October 2010. Contemporary case-study data reported roughly 30% annual solar contribution at each site, much higher instantaneous/daylight penetration, substantial diesel displacement, and improved interruption performance. It overlaps intentionally with the separate `marble-bar` record; that component record should be used for Marble Bar-only updates.
marble-bar-and-nullagine-microgrid.status: Both towns remain active Horizon Power service areas and their solar stations remain described by the utility. The original architecture is no longer uniform: Marble Bar commissioned a central BESS in 2021, and a current control-engineering case study says its original ABB flywheel reached end-of-life and the storage/control system is being replaced or upgraded. No equivalent recent technical update was found for Nullagine, so its current flywheel condition, solar share, and storage availability are unverified. Do not describe the pair as one electrically interconnected microgrid.

Original supervisory and generator/renewable integration platform for the two diesel-solar-flywheel systems.
Later central BESS at Marble Bar only; supplier, inverter, cell chemistry, and nameplate ratings were not disclosed in the utility announcement.

technicalDetails.detail: PV/diesel Microgrid with PowerStore grid-stabilizing technology and Microgrid Plus System by ABB
Flywheel Storage

This portfolio record aggregates two independent isolated grids for comparative reporting. The towns are not connected by a shared microgrid feeder. The separate `marble-bar` record is a component-level duplicate, not an additional third project.

Tracked PV displaced diesel while the flywheel and Microgrid Plus controls supplied rapid balancing/grid support, allowing very high instantaneous solar penetration without treating the flywheel as bulk-duration storage.

ARENA's case study reports Marble Bar outage duration falling from 38 minutes in 2009-10 to 8 minutes in 2010-11 and Nullagine from 110 minutes to zero over the same comparison period. The result is historical and not a current reliability guarantee.

A later independent case study reported parasitic nighttime losses from the flywheel and said Horizon Power would likely choose a battery if repeating the design. Marble Bar subsequently did commission a BESS.
No recent public source was found giving a combined present-day generation mix, Nullagine storage condition, current diesel consumption, or the Marble Bar BESS's rated power and energy.

marble-bar.summary: Marble Bar is the Marble Bar component of the two-town Horizon Power solar-diesel project also represented by the `marble-bar-and-nullagine-microgrid` umbrella record. Its original system integrated 302 kW of tracked solar, four 320 kW diesel generators, a 500 kW ABB/Powercorp flywheel, and Microgrid Plus controls in May 2010. Historical case studies reported about 30% annual solar energy, 65% daytime solar, up to 92% instantaneous solar penetration, and a reduction in annual diesel use of roughly 405,000 litres. The system has since entered a modernization phase: a central BESS was commissioned in 2021, and a current engineering case study identifies the original flywheel and PLC/control assets as end-of-life or unsupported.
marble-bar.status: Operating Horizon Power town system with ongoing storage and controls modernization. The 2021 BESS is commissioned and the central solar plant remains active, producing more than 1 GWh per year according to Horizon Power. The original PowerStore flywheel should not be presented as current: it is explicitly described as end-of-life in the replacement project. Public sources reviewed do not disclose the replacement BESS rating, chemistry, exact commissioning configuration, or current renewable fraction.

technicalDetails.detail: PV, diesel, storage
This is not separate from the Marble Bar portion of `marble-bar-and-nullagine-microgrid`; the umbrella record additionally includes Nullagine.
The integrated solar-diesel-flywheel system was commissioned in May 2010. ARENA reports an original project cost of about A$16 million.
Annual outage duration fell from 38 minutes in 2009-10 to 8 minutes in 2010-11 in the historical case-study comparison.

The original flywheel supplied short-duration grid support while PV displaced diesel. The current project replaces obsolete generator/control/storage assets and adds conventional battery storage, but public documentation does not reveal the finalized one-line design or BESS ratings.
No recent audited fuel saving, present annual solar percentage, BESS dispatch data, or complete post-upgrade equipment schedule was found through July 2026.

marisol-village.summary: Marisol is a remote community on the Alto Huayabamba River in Pachiza District, San Martín, Peru. The record spans two distinct installations. In November 2011, SMART Hydro Power installed a 5 kW floating hydrokinetic turbine for a village then described as 28 families/approximately 240 residents, supplemented by 24 very small household solar systems. Floods, rising water, and debris subsequently damaged or disabled that first configuration. A redesigned hybrid system reported operating from November 2014 combined a river turbine, more than 1.2 kW of PV, a 48 V battery bank, backup generation, and a SMART energy-management system for roughly 60 households. Older catalog figures of 2 kW PV and 20 kWh storage were not corroborated by the supplier's current project page and are retained only as legacy values.
marisol-village.status: The 2014 hybrid rebuild is documented as commissioned and is still presented as a completed reference project by SMART Hydro Power, but no independent recent operating report, turbine replacement history, battery health, load data, or current service count was found through July 2026. The 2011 floating-turbine installation should not be described as continuously operating; project literature explicitly records its failure/inactivity before the later redesign.

technicalDetails.detail: The 2011 floating-turbine project and the hybrid system reported running from November 2014 are successive phases, not two simultaneously verified plants.
Project case material says unusually high/rising river water and debris damaged or disabled the original floating installation, requiring redesign and reinstallation.
The rebuilt system combines continuous river energy where available with PV, a low-voltage battery bank, backup generation, centralized EMS, and a village distribution network.

Project profiles describe household electricity, community services including dental equipment, and round-the-clock availability after the rebuild, but do not publish interval generation, uptime, or independently audited reliability data.

Current hydro rating, actual PV capacity above the stated minimum, storage energy, diesel use, renewable fraction, tariff governance, and post-commissioning maintenance/replacement history remain unverified.

marsabit.summary: Marsabit is Kenya Power's isolated town power system at Marsabit, Kenya; it is not the 310 MW Lake Turkana Wind Power plant elsewhere in Marsabit County. The documented hybrid configuration added two 275 kW wind turbines to diesel generation and an ABB 500 kW PowerStore flywheel with Microgrid Plus controls. Kenya's 2022 public register lists the Marsabit off-grid undertaking at 1.666 MW licensed capacity using wind and liquid-fuel generation, while an ABB presentation described 2 x 800 kW diesels plus 550 kW wind; these are different regulatory/equipment figures and are not merged. Recent evidence shows the town remained off-grid, suffered serious outages attributed to aged generators, and was still procuring repairs for two ABC medium-speed diesel engines in 2026.
marsabit.status: Operating but capacity- and reliability-constrained off-grid utility system. Kenya Power was procuring repair services and spare parts for two Marsabit Power Station diesel engines in 2026, confirming that thermal generation remained operationally important. A national-grid transmission connection has been discussed/planned, but no source reviewed establishes that Marsabit was interconnected by July 2026. Current wind availability, flywheel condition, renewable fraction, and fuel consumption were not publicly verified.

technicalDetails.detail: Wind, diesel, storage
This record covers the Marsabit town off-grid station. Lake Turkana Wind Power is a separate, grid-scale project and must not be counted as this microgrid's generation.

The flywheel and Microgrid Plus controller smooth wind variability and coordinate diesels so that the isolated grid can maintain frequency and voltage without using the flywheel as long-duration energy supply.
In 2023 residents protested persistent outages; Kenya Power attributed constraints to old/unserviceable generators and moved an additional 500 kVA generator from Moyale as an interim measure.
The 2026 tender for major repairs, spare parts, testing, and recommissioning of two ABC engines is direct evidence that diesel remains central to the station and that overhaul work is ongoing.
No current wind production, flywheel availability, diesel efficiency, customer energy mix, outage trend after the repairs, or confirmed grid-interconnection date was found through July 2026.

mawson-station-microgrid.summary: Mawson is the Australian Antarctic Division's year-round Antarctic research station microgrid. Two cold-climate Enercon E-30 wind turbines, each rated 300 kW on 30 m towers, entered service in 2003 and were integrated with the station diesel powerhouse by Powercorp controls. The system uses surplus wind in three 90 kW electric boilers for station heat, providing a controllable thermal sink rather than electrochemical battery storage. The first operating year reportedly cut fuel consumption about 27%, with wind averaging 44% of station electrical demand and supplying roughly 60% under typical winds. One turbine suffered a critical failure in 2017 and remains described by the division as unavailable; the surviving 300 kW unit was repaired and returned to full operation after a blade-motor fault in 2023.
mawson-station-microgrid.status: Operating wind-diesel station system with one of the original two 300 kW turbines available at the last authoritative equipment updates found. Diesel generator sets remain the backup and can run in parallel with wind. Original 600 kW wind nameplate should not be reported as current available wind capacity. No post-2023 public performance series or 2026 fuel/renewable fraction was found, and there is no evidence that the failed second turbine or the never-erected spare third turbine has entered service.

equipment.detail: Two specially adapted variable-speed 300 kW turbines designed for Antarctic cold and high winds; only one is documented available after 2017.
Switchboards, engine controls, and supervisory software dynamically coordinate wind output, diesel generation, station load, and electric boilers.
Three 90 kW controllable boilers use surplus wind electricity for station heat and provide rapid balancing headroom.
Multiple powerhouse generator sets provide backup and can operate in parallel with wind; current manufacturer, models, and ratings were not published in the sources reviewed.

technicalDetails.detail: The wind project was formally opened on 3 March 2003 and integrated into the existing station powerhouse.

Published descriptions sometimes call the electric-boiler arrangement energy storage, but it is a flexible thermal load that converts surplus electricity to useful heat. No electrochemical BESS is documented.
A third turbine was delivered as part of the early project but was not erected because its foundation was not ready. Installed wind capacity was two turbines, not three.
One turbine experienced a critical failure in 2017. In 2023 the other turbine had a blade-motor fault that AAD staff and Enercon support repaired, returning that unit to full operation.
AAD has documented power interruptions when wind falls faster than the boiler controls can shed load, illustrating the balancing challenge of a small isolated wind-diesel system.
No recent annual fuel consumption, measured renewable fraction after loss of the second turbine, current diesel schedule, or second-turbine replacement plan was found through July 2026.

maxwell-afb.summary: Maxwell Air Force Base in Alabama has multiple distinct microgrid/energy phases that should not be combined as one commissioned plant. The legacy CERTS research demonstration connected two existing 600 kW diesel generators in one building with a new 100 kW generator in another, creating a 1.3 MW two-building test system whose autonomous droop controls were successfully demonstrated without a central dispatch controller. Separately, a modern US$107.9 million energy-savings performance contract includes 4 MW of combined heat and power and broader efficiency/resilience work. A later DOE AFFECT award for the Gunter Annex is ongoing through July 2027 and now identifies 4.6 MWdc solar, a 5 MW/20 MWh BESS, and a microgrid controller; DOE's earlier selection description used approximately 5.5 MW of PV, indicating scope evolution.
maxwell-afb.status: Multi-phase site. The CERTS configuration is a completed historical R&D demonstration, but the sources reviewed do not confirm that its exact three-generator topology remains in production service. The 4 MW CHP/ESPC is an executed modern infrastructure project, while the Gunter Annex solar-storage microgrid is an awarded, ongoing project rather than a commissioned asset; its grant period runs to 31 July 2027. No source found demonstrates current operation of the planned PV/BESS or permits adding those proposed capacities to the legacy 1.3 MW demonstration.

equipment.detail: Two existing 600 kW units and one new 100 kW unit used in the CERTS two-building demonstration; manufacturers and models were not disclosed in the research overview.
Autonomous peer-to-peer/droop control allowed sources to share load and transition without reliance on a central command-and-control system.
Modern 4 MW CHP installation; prime mover, fuel, heat-recovery configuration, and manufacturer/model were not identified on the DOE recognition page.
Awarded 4.6 MWdc Gunter Annex array; module, inverter, and EPC suppliers were not disclosed in the sources reviewed.

Controller for Gunter Annex solar, BESS, facility loads, and grid-island operation; supplier and model were not disclosed.

technicalDetails.detail: The historical 1.3 MW CERTS demonstration, the modern 4 MW CHP ESPC, and the awarded Gunter Annex PV-BESS microgrid are separate scopes and dates. Their capacities must not be summed as if one simultaneously commissioned microgrid.
The CERTS work successfully validated autonomous source control and a sectionalized two-building microgrid using three generators without central dispatch.
The Gunter Annex award couples PV, a four-hour BESS, and a controller so the battery can serve the annex during loss of grid power while also shifting energy by time of use in normal operation.

DOE's selection-stage description used approximately 5.5 MW PV; the later award record uses 4.6 MWdc. The later award value is more specific, but neither is evidence of installed capacity before commissioning.
No commissioning date, equipment vendor schedule, actual CHP performance, operational PV/BESS data, or confirmation that the old CERTS topology remains in service was found through July 2026.

mendare-village-karnatanka-microgrid.summary: This project is the Mendare village DC pico-grid in the Male Mahadeshwara Hills near Mysore, Karnataka, India; the slug's `Karnatanka` is a misspelling of Karnataka. SELCO project material documents 600 Wp of solar (two 300 W modules), four 12 V 200 Ah batteries in series as a 48 V/200 Ah bank, a Phocos 48 V charge controller, overhead 48 V DC distribution, and basic LED/mobile-phone service for 22 Soliga-community houses. The underlying equipment schedule therefore contradicts legacy catalog headlines of 1 kW solar and 800 Ah storage: series-connected batteries increase voltage, not ampere-hour capacity. Field research in 2016-17 described Mendare as an operating 0.6 kW lighting pico-grid serving about 20 customers, with evening-only service and limited productive use.
mendare-village-karnatanka-microgrid.status: Historically installed and independently observed operating during 2016-17 fieldwork. A project catalog continues to label it operational, but no recent operator log, battery-replacement record, remote-monitoring data, customer count, or 2026 field verification was found. Current operating status is therefore unverified; retain the project as a small lighting-focused DC pico-grid rather than a 1 kW AC microgrid.

technicalDetails.detail: The Mendare Village Microgrid is a solar microgrid in India by SELCO foundation in Karnataka.
The communities do not have grid power, but a number of households have installed solar-home-systems.
The Model – Platform Microgrid
The model being piloted is therefore:
Establish which services could be introduced later, building on-top of the existing infrastructure and operational model established.
Following this model at Mendare:
4x 200Ah 12V, series connection, Primetech Charge controller: 48V Phocos CR

Mendare is a Soliga tribal settlement in the Male Mahadeshwara Hills of Karnataka. It should not be conflated with other SELCO `Community Mini-Grid: Karnataka` projects that use different 1.2 kW/96 V configurations.

The 600 Wp PV value is supported both by the component schedule and field study. Four 200 Ah batteries in series remain a 200 Ah string; 800 Ah is not the bank capacity unless strings are parallel, which the project diagram does not show.
A centralized PV/battery enclosure feeds a low-voltage DC overhead network. Household fuses and highly constrained end-use packages limit demand and avoid inverter conversion losses.
The 2016-17 field study found the pico-grid operating and useful for lighting, but its small capacity and evening schedule provided little productive-use energy or broader livelihood transformation.

Commissioning date, current operating status, battery replacement interval, measured solar yield, outage history, collected tariffs, and remote-logger data were not established by recent authoritative sources.

mesa-del-sol-microgrid.summary: This record covers NEDO's Albuquerque Business District Smart Grid Demonstration at Mesa del Sol's Aperture Center, not PNM's separate Prosperity solar-storage facility about 2 km away. The roughly 400 kW commercial-building microgrid combines 50 kW of PV, a 240 kW gas-engine generator, 80 kW of phosphoric-acid fuel cells, and a lead-acid battery rated about 90 kW discharge/160 kWh, plus unusually extensive chilled- and hot-water storage. The Japan-U.S. demonstration ran from 2012 through March 2014; ownership of the equipment then transferred to the University of New Mexico, which has continued to use it as a research testbed.
mesa-del-sol-microgrid.status: Commissioned in May 2012; the formal NEDO demonstration ended in March 2014 and the equipment transferred to UNM in April 2014. A 2020 UNM-Siemens agreement identified it as an operating research testbed, and a UNM dataset contains 13 months of measurements through April 2023. No later public asset-condition or availability report was found, so operation is evidenced through 2023 but 2026 health of each generator, cell stack, and battery is unverified.

specifications.label: Demonstrated power quality
specifications.value: After two years of refinement, BEMS1 held point-of-common-coupling power to about 2 kW standard deviation. During island tests voltage remained within 480 V ±10%, while frequency remained within 60 Hz ±0.3 Hz more than 98% of the time.

technicalDetails.detail: 900 kW Lead Acid Batteries Capacity
160 kWh Lead Acid Battery Storage
Thermal Storage

PNM's Prosperity installation, roughly 2 km from Aperture Center, was coordinated by BEMS2 but was a separate utility asset. NEDO text cites 500 kW PV and 500 kW/500 kWh storage for that facility; it is not part of Mesa del Sol's on-site nameplate.
Coordinating the gas engine and fuel cell with storage reduced the modeled storage capacity required for PV smoothing by about 14%; the study estimated up to 17.7% with low communications delay.
NEDO reports grid/island switching and reconnection without instantaneous interruption in the tested configuration.
The 2023 UNM dataset contains 18 measured features for power, voltage, frequency, and temperature over 13 months, providing stronger operating evidence than undated project-directory listings.

No audited annual generation by source was found. The 50 kW PV array is small relative to the approximately 400 kW building load, while grid power, gas generation, and fuel cells provide the balance; battery and thermal storage shift energy rather than generate it.

microgrid-technology-and-demonstration-c.summary: This is Oncor's System Operating Services Facility (SOSF) microgrid and its Technology Demonstration and Education Center in Lancaster, Texas. It is a working utility testbed—not merely an exhibit—built as four independently islandable but mutually connectable zones. The 2015 installation combines approximately 106 kW of PV, five fuel-fired generators totaling about 670 kW, and two battery systems totaling 225 kW/425 kWh, with S&C switching/protection and Schneider control software.
microgrid-technology-and-demonstration-c.status: Operational in March 2015 after a nine-month design/build effort. North Central Texas Council of Governments still described it as a successful operating project in 2022, and S&C continues to present it as a proven microgrid reference. No recent Oncor publication disclosed annual availability, generation mix, fuel consumption, battery state of health, or major replacements, so continued showcase/reference status is clearer than 2026 component condition.

Propane MicroTurbine (65 kW)
Photovoltaic (PV) Solar Arrays (200 kW)

S&C's engineering case describes grid-forming BESS energizing the island first, followed by PV and the propane microturbine; a diesel is then synchronized separately because of harmonic/control constraints.
Continuous monitoring detects faults, isolates affected sections, preserves priority loads, and permits healthy zones to reconnect in a different topology.
The TDEC interface presents live SCADA and operating data from the actual facility microgrids, rather than a simulation-only display.

The facility is normally grid connected and exists to test mixed DER behavior. No audited annual solar, diesel, or propane contribution was published; nameplate values must not be converted into an energy percentage.

milford-microgrid.summary: Milford's downtown municipal microgrid serves five public/community facilities in Connecticut. The as-built public record is a 400 kW natural-gas CHP system plus 125 kW battery storage, with Schneider EcoStruxure controls and islanding infrastructure. The frequently repeated 120 kW solar figure belongs to the 2014 award-stage design: Schneider's 2018 design-build announcement explicitly called the project solar-ready, and the state's final project tracker does not list installed PV.
milford-microgrid.status: Completed and granted permission to operate in September 2021. Connecticut DEEP classifies it as operational, the city's 2023 hazard plan marks the project completed, and 2023 city procurement records document ongoing Schneider monitoring and maintenance. No public record found through July 2026 establishes installed PV, battery energy capacity/chemistry/vendor, annual CHP output, outage performance, or measured savings.

miramar-microgrid.summary: MCAS Miramar's installation-wide microgrid is a 12 kV system serving more than 100 flight-line and support buildings—about 60% of base load—not every load on the installation. Its current documented portfolio is 3.2 MW landfill gas, 6.45 MW of new Tier 4 diesel/natural-gas generation, roughly 1.3–1.9 MW solar depending source/date, a separate 2 MW emergency diesel, and a 1.5 MW/3.3 MWh grid-forming lithium-ion BESS commissioned in November 2024. A separate earlier 230 kW PV/250 kW zinc-bromine flow-battery project at the Public Works building must not be merged into the base-wide BESS.
miramar-microgrid.status: The base-wide microgrid was completed in 2020 and publicly demonstrated in March 2021. It has performed more than 17 base-wide outage tests by 2023 and supported SDG&E during regional heat events. The new 1.5 MW/3.3 MWh BESS completed commissioning with a 5.5-hour black-start/island test on November 16, 2024 and then accumulated 12 continuous operating months through 2025. A May 2026 CEC final report confirms the integrated system operational and documents measured savings.

specifications.value: 2 MW emergency diesel generator with paralleling switchgear, labeled the B7777 generator in the 2025 DOE configuration.

specifications.label: Renewable share
specifications.value: Latest CEC analysis says landfill gas plus solar supplies approximately 52% of microgrid energy needs; EPA describes approximately 50%. This is the best documented operating mix and is not a claim of zero-carbon generation because landfill gas accounting and diesel/gas backup remain material.

specifications.label: Measured BESS economics
specifications.value: CEC reports US$258,268 demand-charge savings for calendar 2025, more than US$21,000/month. The report gives 917 kW average monthly demand reduction in one section and 962 kW elsewhere; capital cost was US$3,249,933, simple payback about 13 years, and projected 20-year NPV US$322,451.

equipment.detail: Three paralleled grid-forming packages, each 500 kW/1,100 kWh, for 1.5 MW/3.3 MWh total.
Integrates PCS/DER controls, AGC, unit commitment/economic dispatch, renewable and load forecasts, tariffs, islanding, black start, and reconnection.
More than 230 advanced meters feed the Energy and Water Operations Center and control platform.
480 V and 12 kV switchgear within a 12 kV base microgrid.
Separate Public Works demonstration used a 250 kW zinc-bromine flow battery with titanium electrodes and 230 kW PV; storage energy rating was not established in the DOE page.

technicalDetails.detail: Battery ramp rate is approximately 1 MW/s and reaches full 1.5 MW in about 1.5 seconds, versus roughly 182 kW/s for diesel (about 10 seconds) and 7 kW/s for natural gas (about 3 minutes).
Grid-connected P/Q and power-factor modes; island generator-following P/Q and Hz-Watts; grid-forming voltage/frequency and UPS-like modes.

Fuel-quality interruptions can abruptly remove 1.6–3.2 MW of landfill-gas output. The BESS supplies ride-through and now permits both landfill-gas generators to operate while islanded, whereas only one was allowed previously.

The first full Energy Resilience Readiness Exercise included a day-long utility outage; SDG&E requested dispatch during 2020–2022 heat events, including output roughly equivalent to 3,000 homes during the August 17 event reported by USMC.

The DOE Public Works 230 kW PV/250 kW flow-battery pilot is a discrete building-scale research system. It should not be added to the later 1.5 MW/3.3 MWh base-wide BESS without a source proving both remain simultaneously active and inside the same accounting boundary.
DOE's 2025 slides separately depict future targets of 4.8 MW landfill gas, 3 MW PV, and 3 MW/6 MWh storage; those are not commissioned nameplate values in this record.

mole-st-nicholas-microgrid.summary: Sigora's Môle-Saint-Nicolas system in northwest Haiti began as a stand-alone solar-diesel micro-utility and later became part of one interconnected network spanning Môle, Jean Rabel, Bombardopolis, and the Presqu'île/Savanne Môle area. The best site-specific 2018 record lists 208 kW solar, 200 kW diesel, 1,055 customer connections, and 24 km of lines at Môle. Broader pilot figures—1.25 MW diesel, about 0.2 MW solar, and roughly 4,500 customers—cover the multi-municipality network and must not be assigned to the original Môle plant alone.
mole-st-nicholas-microgrid.status: Operating as part of Sigora Haiti's northwest network. A 2025 Haiti electrification review says the expanded system serves roughly 4,300 customers, while Sigora's current site claims 24/7 service to more than 30,000 people across four municipalities and a pico-grid. Public sources do not verify a commissioned wind plant or disclose current battery capacity, annual solar/diesel generation, availability, generator health, or audited 2026 customer counts.

Different customer/person and date scopes explain the figures.

specifications.value: 24/7 AC service at 120/240/480 V using prepaid smart metering; Sigora reports 25-year contracts for the network.

IDB's project page separately lists an US$8 million syndicated package and US$1.5 million financed amount, approved in 2019 and signed December 17, 2020; these financing fields should not be summed without deal documents.

equipment.detail: Prepaid mobile-account meter with anti-theft features, demand ceilings, tiered restoration delay, secure RF mesh/cloud communications, and single-, split-, or three-phase configurations.
208 kW documented at Môle in 2018; module and inverter manufacturers/models were not publicly disclosed.
Two units totaling 200 kW at Môle in 2018; make, individual ratings, and models were not disclosed.

Storage is discussed in expansion and secondary reporting, but no authoritative commissioned kW/kWh, chemistry, vendor, or model was found for Môle. It is therefore not included in installed nameplate.

technicalDetails.detail: The original Môle plant and the later interconnected northwest network are related but different accounting scopes. Network diesel capacity cannot be attributed to the Môle site.

IDB documents wind turbines as a proposed expansion component/environmental topic, not as installed inventory. No reliable source confirms the legacy 100 kW wind claim was commissioned; Sigora's generic statement that its hybrid grids can incorporate wind is not project evidence.
Sigora reported in 2017 an average 1.167 outages and about 31 minutes of downtime per week. This is operator-reported historical performance, not a current independent availability audit.

Secondary reporting says service was restored within 55 hours after Hurricane Matthew and within 10 hours after Irma for customers then served across Môle and Jean Rabel; panels were temporarily removed for Irma protection.
Môle's documented solar and diesel nameplates were roughly equal in 2018, but annual kWh by source was not published. Capacity parity is not an energy-mix percentage.

molokai-secure-renewable-microgrid-proje.summary: The Molokaʻi Secure Renewable Microgrid Project is HNEI's fast-response utility BESS demonstration on the isolated Maui Electric island grid, not a generic label for every Molokaʻi renewable project. Its core asset is a 2 MW Altairnano lithium-titanate battery with a Parker-Hannifin PCS, variously reported as 330/333 kWh usable or 397 kWh nominal. It was built to supply sub-second reserve and frequency/fault response as rooftop PV displaced roughly 2 MW of the island's approximately 5 MW daytime generation.
molokai-secure-renewable-microgrid-proje.status: Commissioned in February 2016 and transferred to Maui Electric. HNEI's updated 2022 project page says the Molokaʻi BESS was operating, and later HNEI research continued to use it with a programmable load bank. No asset-specific public source after 2022 was found confirming state of health, cell replacement, availability, or continued dispatch in July 2026; classify it as operational through the latest direct confirmation, with current condition unverified.

specifications.label: Storage
specifications.value: 2MW/333KWh

specifications.label: Power rating
specifications.value: 2 MW charge/discharge capability for fast reserve, fault response, and frequency support.

specifications.label: Energy rating
specifications.value: HNEI's 2020 annual report gives 397 kWh, while an ONR/HNEI technical deck gives 330 kWh (commonly repeated as 333 kWh). These likely reflect nominal versus usable/revised rating; preserve both rather than selecting one silently.

specifications.label: Duration
specifications.value: Approximately 10–12 minutes at full 2 MW, depending on whether 330 or 397 kWh is used; this is a power-quality/reserve battery, not multi-hour energy shifting.

specifications.label: Grid context
specifications.value: Approximately 5–5.5 MW island system; around 2 MW of daytime conventional generation had been displaced by roughly 400 rooftop-PV systems when the project was documented.

specifications.label: Commissioning
specifications.value: February 2016.

specifications.label: Control latency result
specifications.value: The initial utility communications path required about 350 ms and was modeled as destabilizing for the intended response. Direct PCS measurements reduced typical end-to-end response to about 58 ms, near the modeled approximately 50 ms requirement.

equipment.detail: Technical deck describes 416 series cell groups in a 1S7P module arrangement; system marketed in Altairnano's large-format BESS family.
2 MW-class bidirectional inverter/PCS; exact product model was not disclosed in the sources reviewed.
Fast-response control and communications architecture for contingency reserve and frequency response.
750 kW programmable resistive load bank installed in 2019 to coordinate with the 2 MW BESS, absorb excess PV, and support frequency/down-reserve experiments.

technicalDetails.detail: Altairnano 2MW/333KWh lithium-ion titinate battery
Approximately 400 hundred rooftop PV systems on Moloka‘i totaling 2.4 MW are enough to provide
Diesel Generators

The battery supplies rapid reserve and transient response after generation trips or high-PV disturbances; its short duration means its key value is stability, not replacing diesel energy over hours. By 2019, HNEI reported about 2.3 MW rooftop PV against daytime demand as low as 3.3 MW and paired the BESS with a 750 kW controllable load bank.

Modeling showed that using the original slow utility data path could worsen instability. Direct measurement/control at the PCS enabled the necessary fast action; live contingency tests were deliberately bounded for grid safety.

Do not merge this 2 MW fast-response BESS with the later Molokaʻi New Energy Partners proposal (4.88 MW PV plus 3 MW/15 MWh storage) or other community-solar projects; they are separate projects and phases.

The BESS produces no net energy. HNEI documents high rooftop-PV penetration and continued diesel generation, but no project-specific annual Molokaʻi solar/diesel percentage was found. Maui County-wide RPS values are not a substitute for island data.

morotai-island-minigrid.summary: The Morotai/Daruba hybrid minigrid commissioned in April 2012 combined 600 kWp solar, 2.7 MWh of battery storage, and seven diesel generators with Optimal Power Solutions' Macro Hybrid System. Its lifecycle matters: automatic control was already problematic in 2016, Indonesia's electricity directorate says Daruba PLTS Unit 1 stopped operating in March 2019, and a 2022 report described only two-thirds of the system functional and manual operation. In June 2026 PLN announced preparation of a new 600 kWp Juanga hybrid PV project; that planned replacement/addition is not yet commissioned capacity.
morotai-island-minigrid.status: Original 2012 solar asset is not confirmed operational. The strongest official status record says it ceased operation in March 2019; later reporting describes partial/manual operation, while a 2023 planning document still lists 600 kW and 350 kW hybrid PV assets as available infrastructure without proving output. As of June 14, 2026, PLN was preparing a new 600 kWp plant at Juanga for hybrid integration. Classify the legacy project as impaired/out of service with an uncommissioned renewal project in development.
morotai-island-minigrid.historicalLocation: Morotai Island, Morotai Island Regency, North Maluku, Indonesia

1,350 kVA total inverter capacity; 2026 academic description of the legacy plant mentions 14 on-grid inverters and 1,348 modules, but exact makes/models remain undisclosed.
2.7 MWh storage in the original configuration; chemistry/vendor/model not found and condition after the 2019 outage is unknown.
Seven-unit original fleet; individual make/model/rating not disclosed. By 2015 only five were operating.

technicalDetails.detail: Inverter Technology OPS PIM and GEC Inverter Capacity 3 x 250 kVA GEC
Battery – lead acid
Diesel Engines 7 nos, total 1.75 MW Solar PV Capacity 600 kWp

A 2016 presidential inspection found the automatic system malfunctioning, forcing manual operation, with only one technician supporting the plant. The ministry emphasized remote-site spares, regular checks, and additional trained staff.
The electricity directorate's 2019 performance report explicitly says PLTS Daruba Unit 1 had not operated since March 2019. This is stronger status evidence than later capacity inventories.

A 2022 Antara report caption says only two-thirds of the 2012 system functioned and it was manually coordinated with diesel, while a 2023 Bappenas inventory lists two hybrid PV plants (350 and 600 kW) as existing infrastructure. Neither demonstrates full restored output; the conflict is retained.

No recent island generation-by-source data was found. Historical PV output was far below nameplate and diesel remained the balancing source; the 2026 600 kWp plant is planned, so it cannot be counted in operating renewable mix.

mustique.summary: Mustique's private island utility has evolved through several distinct solar-diesel-storage phases. The 2015 DHYBRID Solar Valley project added 1 MWp PV to roughly 3 MVA average diesel generation. A later phase reached 1.5 MW PV and integrated a 500 kW/1 MWh Tesla battery. The current DHYBRID case describes an island-wide 7 MWp PV, 5.2 MW/10 MWh BESS, and 10,680 kVA diesel system under its Universal Power Platform; the 2024 EPC account also reports an 18-hour all-solar trial in May 2023.
mustique.status: Operating and substantially expanded. Mustique's own September 2022 update said work was underway to raise solar's share from 16% toward 70%. DHYBRID's current project pages show completed BESS Phase I (1.3 MW/2.257 MWh) and Phase II (5.2 MW/10 MWh) configurations with 7 MWp PV, while Solar Watt Systems reported the full system operating in 2024 and additional Lagoon solar construction. Exact 2026 annual renewable share, BESS chemistry/vendor for the final phase, diesel run hours, and independently audited fuel savings were not found.

Solar Watt reported about 70% fuel-dependency reduction after reaching 7 MW and roughly 30% from a recent 3 MW addition; these are operator/contractor claims whose accounting periods and demand conditions are not fully disclosed.

equipment.detail: Coordinates PV and BESS charging/discharging and starts/stops diesel generators; evolved from the 2015 Fuel Reduction System with SCADA, remote monitoring, and ring communications.
500 kW/1 MWh battery system integrated with the 1.5 MW PV phase; exact Tesla product designation was not stated.

5.2 MW/10 MWh final Phase II battery installation; DHYBRID's public page does not identify battery manufacturer, model, or chemistry, so it should not be assumed to be the same Tesla equipment as the intermediate phase.
7 MWp island-wide current total; public sources reviewed do not identify module or inverter manufacturers/models.
10,680 kVA plant retained for firm capacity/backup; make, model, unit count, and fuel-storage autonomy not disclosed.

technicalDetails.detail: The Universal Power Platform prioritizes solar, schedules battery charge/discharge, and automatically starts/stops diesel units. The original 2015 system added permanent remote monitoring, SCADA, and ring communications to address island-grid fluctuations.
Solar Watt reports the island ran exclusively on PV/battery energy for 18 hours in a May 2023 trial. This is a demonstrated interval, not evidence of year-round 100% renewable supply.

The 2015 supplier projected/claimed about 490,000 liters diesel and 1.2 million kg CO2 saved annually with 3.5-year payback. The later 1.5 MW PV/1 MWh battery page claims 762,000 liters and 1.78 million kg CO2 annually. These are phase-specific supplier figures, not additive savings.
The 1 MW, 1.5 MW, BESS Phase I, and 7 MW/Phase II descriptions are sequential configurations. Do not add their PV or battery ratings together.
Solar Watt's February 2024 article also said a further 1.1 MW Lagoon project was under construction. It is excluded from the 7 MW installed total absent a later completion source.

necker-island-microgrid.summary: Necker Island's private-island microgrid was built in at least two documented stages, rather than as the archived 700 kW-solar/300 kW-wind configuration. Modeselekt reports a 2015 first phase with 350 kW of solar PV, 900 kW of diesel generation, desalination and integrated controls, followed by a 2019 phase that added 900 kW of wind, 1.2 MWh of battery storage, a second desalination plant and controllable thermal/water loads. An earlier 2014 NRG/Virgin agreement and 2015-2019 planning documents describe changing targets, not the final as-built equipment list.
necker-island-microgrid.status: Operational according to the latest project-integrator account located. Modeselekt says the combined system remains in operation, can run on 100% renewable energy on many days, and has reduced annual diesel use by an average of 90%. Those are supplier-reported results without a disclosed baseline, audit, annual MWh series or fuel-volume dataset. The first phase also survived Hurricane Irma in 2017 with its controls/data intact and resumed operation when personnel could safely restart generation and desalination. No independent 2024-2026 availability, battery-health or turbine-performance report was found.

specifications.label: Phase 2, commissioned 2019
specifications.value: 900 kW combined wind capacity, 1.2 MWh battery storage, a second desalination plant described as a 'water battery,' and controllable pool-heat-pump/thermal loads

specifications.label: Historical planning figures versus as-built record
specifications.value: RMI's earlier snapshot listed roughly 300 kW solar installed and planned 900 kW wind plus 500 kWh storage; the 2019 BVI R-NETS report repeated 300 kW/900 kW/500 kWh planning values. The archived database instead listed 700 kW solar and 300 kW wind. None matches Modeselekt's later phase-by-phase as-built account, so the archived capacities should be replaced rather than added to it.

specifications.label: Reported operating outcome
specifications.value: Supplier-reported average annual diesel reduction of 90%, with 100% renewable operation on many days; no audited annual renewable fraction or fuel baseline was published

equipment.detail: Solar PV, diesel generators, wind turbines and battery storage form the electrical supply. Reviewed public sources do not identify PV module/inverter models, diesel-generator models, turbine manufacturer/models, battery power rating, chemistry, manufacturer or usable energy.
Two desalination plants and pool heat pumps are dispatchable loads. Phase two treats stored fresh water as a 'water battery,' shifting desalination to periods of abundant renewable production.

A staged island control and network architecture integrates generation, storage and controllable loads. The public case studies do not name controller hardware or publish dispatch logic, telemetry intervals or cybersecurity details.

technicalDetails.detail: NRG's agreement targeted at least 75% renewable energy and described solar, wind, storage, diesel backup, efficiency and automated controls, subject to British Virgin Islands approval. It should be treated as a development announcement, not evidence that its unspecified design was commissioned.

Modeselekt reports that Irma severed external communications in 2017 but the internal microgrid network continued collecting data and functioning; generation, desalination and solar were restarted when the island was safe to access. The account does not provide outage hours, damaged-equipment inventory or an independent resilience audit.

RMI's older case study attributed a 15-20% diesel reduction to the initial roughly 300 kW solar installation and projected a future 75% renewable system. That historical result predates the larger wind/storage phase and is not a current-system performance figure.

No public annual generation by source, peak/average load, fuel litres, curtailment, storage state of health, component availability, operating-cost savings or verified renewable fraction through July 2026 was located.

neelakantarayanagaddi-village-microgrid.summary: SELCO Foundation's Neelakantarayanagaddi project is a very small centralized 48 VDC solar microgrid built for 41 houses plus a school and three temples in a river-island village in Karnataka's Yadgir area. The documented design uses 1.2 kWp of EmmVee PV, Primetech batteries, a Phocos 48 V controller, overhead DC distribution, 3 W LED lights, phone charging, per-house fuses and both local and remote timing/monitoring. The archived '1,600 Ah' storage label is electrically misleading, and the source deck itself contains an unresolved energy-versus-Ah conflict.
neelakantarayanagaddi-village-microgrid.status: The system was documented as installed and operating in SELCO's 2014-2016 program material, but no reliable site-specific 2024-2026 operating report was found. A 2016 report said residents crossed to the mainland to charge mobile phones while monsoon flooding isolated the village. That raises a service-availability question but does not establish whether the microgrid had failed, lacked sufficient charging capacity, or was unavailable to those residents. Present battery condition, replacements, collections, expansion and grid arrival remain unknown; the record should not continue to label the project operational without qualification.

specifications.label: Battery source conflict
specifications.value: The case material lists eight 12 V, 200 Ah Primetech batteries and elsewhere a 48 V, 400 Ah bank, which would equal 19.2 kWh nominal; the same deck also labels storage as 9.6 kWh. The archived record's 1,600 Ah is merely the sum of eight battery Ah ratings and is not a valid bank rating. The reviewed sources do not reconcile whether the 9.6 kWh figure reflects an earlier four-battery configuration, usable energy, or a documentation error.

Armstrong Energy Foundation reportedly provided INR 600,000 and the local business associate INR 100,000, with collections intended for maintenance and future expansion.

equipment.detail: Four 300 W, 24 V modules, described as series-connected and installed on a raised metal mounting structure for protection.
Eight 12 V, 200 Ah batteries are listed. The public material does not identify battery model, chemistry, date of replacement, allowable depth of discharge or present state of health.
DC charge regulation at the central plant; no AC inverter is needed for the documented 48 VDC load architecture.
48 VDC 3 W LED lamps and 48 VDC phone chargers. The nonstandard voltage improved distribution efficiency for tiny loads but the program deck flags replacement-device availability as a challenge.

technicalDetails.detail: Neelakantarayanagaddi Village Microgrid is a solar microgrid in India by SELCO foundation in Karnataka.
41 houses and 3 temples PV and batterySchool The village Bridge across river Subsistence farming Dam Nearest road access
The Model – Platform Microgrid
The model being piloted is therefore: 1.
Establish which services could be introduced later, building on-top of the existing infrastructure and operational model established.
Following this model at Mendare: 1.
8x 200Ah, 12V, series connection, Primetech Charge controller: 48V Phocos CR
PV panels: 4x 300W, 24V, series connection, EmmVee
The centralized plant supplies 48 VDC through overhead wiring. Service lines enter a junction box in each house and individual fuses limit household power draw.

An entrepreneur manually controlled service timing, while a centralized data logger allowed remote monitoring, data collection and remote changes to system timing. Public material does not identify modem/network type, controller make, alarm response or historical telemetry.

SELCO emphasized simple, robust designs, locally held spares and a strong community partner. Its own lessons note that specialized 48 V loads can be difficult to replace and that ongoing support is essential.

The village is periodically cut off when the river rises. A 2016 news report described residents going to the mainland for phone charging during isolation; this is a contemporaneous service signal but not a diagnostic record of the microgrid.

No recent generation, load, battery replacement, uptime, collections, customer-growth or utility-grid interconnection data was found. Commissioning date is also not unambiguously stated in the reviewed material.

new-york-affordable-housing-microgrid.summary: The generic archived name refers to the Marcus Garvey Apartments/Village distributed-energy system at 353 Chester Street in Brooklyn. NYSERDA's current DER registry identifies 479.7 kW of solar PV, two 210 kW Bloom natural-gas fuel cells and a 300 kW/1,200 kWh lithium-ion battery. The assets are coordinated for demand-charge management and Consolidated Edison's Brooklyn-Queens Demand Management program, while selected community/management spaces can be supplied during outages. Rounded case studies often say 400 kW solar and 400 kW fuel cell; those are nominal marketing figures, not additional assets.
new-york-affordable-housing-microgrid.status: Operational asset with public evidence extending beyond its 2017 commissioning, though recent device-level operating data is limited. NYSERDA's registry records the three technologies and their commercial dates; a state climate case study retrieved in 2024 continues to present it as an operating resilience project. NYSERDA monitoring windows ended in March 2020 for fuel cells and March 2021 for solar, so neither those dates nor the current registry prove continuous output after them. No 2024-2026 battery state-of-health, availability, outage-islanding result or annual energy balance was found.

equipment.detail: Two 210 kW natural-gas units. NYSERDA records them as non-exporting resources.

Three outdoor lithium-ion battery blocks are shown in the commissioning-era operations dashboard. The archived record names LG Chem, but the current NYSERDA registry and reviewed case studies do not provide a cell/rack model or independently verify that manufacturer.

Coordinates battery charging/discharging and site DER for demand-charge and grid-program value. Public sources do not disclose controller hardware, optimization source code or communications/cybersecurity architecture.
Rooftop PV is distributed around the property. Reviewed sources do not identify module, inverter, racking or rapid-shutdown models.

technicalDetails.detail: 400 KW Fuel Cell from Bloom Energy
400 KW Rooftop Solar

The battery generally charges during lower-cost/off-peak periods and discharges during higher-demand periods. It also participates in Con Edison's Brooklyn-Queens Demand Management program; NYSERDA lists demand-charge reduction as the other principal value stream.

During a grid outage the project is designed to support a security office, management office and community room, providing heat, light, phone charging and medicine refrigeration for up to about 12 hours. That duration applies to selected critical loads, not all 625 apartments.

NYSERDA described the 2017 system as New York City's first permitted outdoor lithium-ion installation and a pioneering low-income multifamily microgrid. Historical-first claims do not themselves establish current performance.
NYSERDA's case study shows material reduction of the property's evening peak between roughly 8 p.m. and midnight on a peak summer day in 2018, but does not provide a full-year interval dataset.
No current battery capacity test, cycle count, fuel-cell availability/fuel use, annual PV output, outage-islanding event report, tenant bill dataset or 2024-2026 operating-cost statement was located.

niobrara-energy-park-microgrid.summary: Niobrara Energy Park near Carr in northern Weld County, Colorado, was a shovel-ready industrial/data-center development concept, not an operating microgrid. Harrison Resource Corporation and related entities obtained land-use and gas-pipeline approvals and marketed roughly 640-662 acres for natural-gas generation, cogeneration, solar, wind, geothermal, storage, research and data centers. Capacity concepts changed substantially between sources; no reviewed evidence shows that the archived 50 MW solar, 30 MW wind and 650 MW gas/diesel configuration was procured or built.
niobrara-energy-park-microgrid.status: Original proposal not built and effectively superseded. Contemporary releases continued to market the site to a buyer or joint-venture developer years after approval, and NCMC Inc. purchased the full 662-acre property from Niobrara Energy Park LLC in August 2019 for $4.9 million as a real-estate investment. No evidence was found that Harrison commissioned generation before the sale or that NCMC subsequently built the proposed microgrid through July 2026. Current disposition of individual parcels and any non-energy development requires county-title research beyond the public project record.

Large data centers, research facilities and other industrial uses were the intended anchor loads. None of the reviewed sources proves that a data center was constructed on the original project schedule.

technicalDetails.detail: Weld County approved the concept in March 2011; CH2M/IDC was engaged later that year; the gas certificate followed in 2013. Marketing still sought a buyer or joint-venture developer in 2015, and the land was sold in 2019.

This Colorado proposal should not be conflated with later/current developments using the Niobrara Energy Park name in Cheyenne/Laramie County, Wyoming. The archived record's County Road 29/Carr location identifies the Weld County project.

Because no operating plant was established, renewable share, fuel use, generation, outages, emissions savings and microgrid-islanding performance are not applicable. Approval, zoning and 'shovel-ready' language must not be converted into operational status.

No final power-plant design, equipment orders, financing close, construction notice, interconnection completion, pipeline in-service notice or commissioning record was located. The current use of all former park parcels is also not established here.

nj-transitgrid.summary: NJ TRANSITGRID is an umbrella resilience program, not one operational microgrid. Its two independent components were (1) the approximately 104 MW natural-gas Microgrid Central Facility/Traction Power System in Kearny and (2) Distributed Generation Solutions at seven stations, garages and a ferry terminal. NJ TRANSIT formally canceled the central facility on January 26, 2024 and redirected $503 million. The smaller seven-site program survived and still had design, construction-management, construction and commissioning contract actions before the board in July 2025.
nj-transitgrid.status: Split outcome. The Microgrid Central Facility and associated traction-power project are canceled, after progressing through environmental review and early design but before construction of the generation plant. The Distributed Generation Solutions component remains active: NJ TRANSIT's July 2025 board minutes authorized expanded final-design, construction and commissioning work, but no reviewed source establishes that all seven installations had reached commercial operation by July 2026. The archived database's 'operational' classification is therefore wrong for the umbrella program.

specifications.label: Distributed-generation sites
specifications.value: Seven facilities: Greenville, Wayne and Meadowlands bus garages; Newark Penn, Newark Broad Street and Secaucus Junction rail stations; and Port Imperial ferry terminal. The 2019 program announcement specifically included a Wayne solar canopy and described local fuel-cell, solar and CHP technologies more generally.

specifications.value: AECOM construction assistance up to $1.556 million; Burns construction management up to $5.286 million; Dynamic US final design/construction/commissioning up to $47.585 million, plus a 5% contingency. Contract ceilings are not plant capacity or completed-project cost.

specifications.value: $503 million of federal resilience funding was redirected to other NJ TRANSIT projects after industry responses were judged financially infeasible and regional-grid improvements reduced the facility's relative need

equipment.detail: Five natural-gas generator sets, 600 kW PV and flywheel storage appeared in early design documents. Manufacturer, model, unit ratings and final storage energy were not established as procured equipment before cancellation.

FTA/NJ TRANSIT materials identify a mix of fuel cells, solar PV, combined heat and power and backup/local generation across seven sites. Reviewed public sources do not provide a final site-by-site as-built capacity, model, fuel, storage or commissioning list.

The program envisioned SCADA, dedicated fiber and secure coordination among a central plant, distributed sites and rail operations. These documents describe design objectives and cybersecurity recommendations, not a verified commissioned control network.

technicalDetails.detail: NJ TRANSIT will purchase electric, non-revenue vehicles to maximize energy storage.

FTA defined the Traction Power System and Distributed Generation Solutions as independent projects in 2016. Canceling the central facility did not cancel the seven-site program, and evidence of continued distributed-generation contracting should not be used to revive the central plant.
The Final Environmental Impact Statement and Record of Decision were completed in April 2020. Environmental approval allowed the project to proceed but did not establish construction or operation.

Sandia documented open questions at the 20% design stage over network boundaries, communications, firewalls/data-diode approaches, remote access and ownership of cybersecurity controls. Its follow-on guide proposed a defense-in-depth architecture; the recommendations are not evidence of final installation.

The archived page's statement about purchasing electric non-revenue vehicles to maximize energy storage appears to be one program measure, not a specification for the traction microgrid and not proof that vehicle batteries supplied grid services.

No verified final capacity/equipment schedule, site-by-site completion dates, islanding tests, generation, fuel use, storage performance or avoided-outage data for the seven-site program was located through July 2026.
