nouadhibou-iron-ore-mine-microgrid.summary: The archived name is geographically misleading: SNIM's iron-ore mines are inland around Zouerate and F'derick, while this 4.4 MW wind/16 MW diesel hybrid serves SNIM's coastal port, processing, industrial and community electricity system at Nouadhibou/Cansado. Sixteen 275 kW Vergnet GEV MP-C turbines operate on the 5.5 kV diesel grid under Vergnet's Hybrid Wizard control strategy. SNIM's 2024 report still counts the wind plant in service, and a 2024 repair/training mission provides recent maintenance evidence.
nouadhibou-iron-ore-mine-microgrid.status: Operational with documented 2024 maintenance. SNIM's 2024 annual report lists 4.4 MW of wind at Nouadhibou within its 19.4 MW clean-generation portfolio, alongside the 16 MW Nouadhibou thermal plant. Vergnet completed a repair and technician-training mission in July 2024. A recent African Development Bank account says wind supplies nearly 30% of the plant's electricity needs; no site-specific annual MWh, availability, curtailment or diesel-savings series was published, so the exact current renewable fraction and performance cannot be independently audited.

specifications.label: Wind plant
specifications.value: 4.4 MW: sixteen 275 kW turbines. Vergnet's 2024 repair announcement rounds the plant to 4.5 MW; SNIM's annual report and the exact unit arithmetic support 4.4 MW as the canonical rating.

specifications.value: 16 MW from four diesel generator sets feeding a 5.5 kV isolated industrial grid

specifications.label: Wind resource
specifications.value: 8.78 m/s mean wind speed at the 55 m turbine hub height in the project study

specifications.label: Reported current contribution
specifications.value: African Development Bank reports the Nouadhibou wind plant supplies nearly 30% of plant electricity needs; the account does not publish the measurement period, denominator or annual MWh.

specifications.label: SNIM-wide context, not site production
specifications.value: SNIM reported 19.4 MW total clean capacity in 2024 (15 MW solar at Zouerate plus 4.4 MW wind at Nouadhibou) and 415,823 MWh total company-system generation, including 343,226 MWh for industrial facilities and 62,113 MWh for communities. Those MWh figures combine multiple power plants and cannot be assigned to the Nouadhibou hybrid alone.

equipment.detail: Sixteen 275 kW, two-bladed turbines with 32 m rotor diameter and 55 m hub height. The design was selected for a warm, dry, near-shore and highly corrosive environment and supports lowering/servicing suited to remote sites.

Four generator sets totaling 16 MW provide dispatchable generation and grid-forming support. Reviewed public sources do not identify engine/alternator manufacturers, models, individual ratings or fuel-efficiency curves.

Supervisory control measures load and generation, limits wind to preserve diesel minimum loading/spinning reserve and manages connections and shortfall response. Public material does not identify the installed software version or current hardware platform.

technicalDetails.detail: Wind Turbine: 16 Vergnet GEV MP-C, 275kW, 32m rotor diameter, 55m hub height
Total power: 4.4 MW
Vergnet GEV MP Wind Turbines Deliver Energy and Cost Savings for SNIM Iron Mine
The facility is powered by a 16 MW diesel plant equipped with 4 diesel gensets, feeding a 5.5 kV grid.
Specific wind turbine technology was also required to address the near-shore location, very dry and warm climate, and highly corrosive environment.
A Hybrid Approach – Investing in Wind Energy and Vergnet’s Hybrid Wizard® Solution

Sources variously cite 2012 construction/start and 2013 commissioning. The project was operational by 2013; the one-year wording difference should be retained rather than forced into a single unsupported date.

The control study modeled wind profile, load cycles and diesel characteristics to maximize renewable penetration while maintaining frequency/voltage, generator reserve and minimum diesel loading. No battery storage is documented in the operating architecture.

Vergnet reported completing repair work and training a five-to-six-person SNIM technical team in operation and maintenance in July 2024. The announcement does not disclose how many turbines were unavailable, downtime duration, replacement parts or post-repair availability.

SNIM mines ore inland and transports it by rail to the Nouadhibou coast. The wind-diesel plant is therefore better classified as a port/industrial microgrid within an integrated mining system, not an onsite iron-ore mine microgrid.

No site-specific annual wind MWh, capacity factor, curtailment, diesel litres, generator heat rate, turbine availability, outages, repair scope or measured emissions savings through July 2026 was located.

nova-nickel-mine-microgrid.summary: Nova is IGO's remote nickel-copper-cobalt operation in Western Australia's Fraser Range. Its power system evolved from a 19 MW diesel station to a battery-free solar-diesel hybrid in 2019 and then to an engine-off hybrid with approximately 16 MW total solar, a 12 MW/12 MWh BESS and two synchronous condensers. The archived 6.7 MW solar/20 MW diesel figures reflect the original contract/design scale, while Zenith's current as-built account reports 5.5 MW initial solar and 19 MW diesel before the 10.2 MW relocatable-solar expansion.
nova-nickel-mine-microgrid.status: Operational, with the renewable expansion commissioned and engine-off operation demonstrated. IGO's FY2023 report says the new solar farm and battery were commissioned and achieved Nova's first period on 100% renewable power; later company material lists the full 16 MW-solar/12 MW-battery system as installed. The power system also experienced a major diesel-engine-room fire in December 2022 that shut the mine for 18 days before 14.9 MW of temporary generation restored operations. IGO now expects the Nova mine to reach end of life in late 2026, making the expansion's relocatable design important; no public post-closure destination or current battery-health data was found.

This is separate from, and should not be merged into, the 12 MW/12 MWh engine-off BESS.

specifications.label: Initial-solar operating result
specifications.value: Zenith reports an average 11 GWh/year in the first three years, approximately 3 million litres/year of avoided diesel and 8,100 tCO2/year avoided. These are operator-reported figures and predate the full engine-off expansion.

equipment.detail: Five 3 MW medium-speed sets and three 1.7 MW high-speed sets are listed by Zenith. Their simple arithmetic exceeds the published 19 MW plant total, likely reflecting gross/nominal versus station rating, but the source does not reconcile it. Engine and alternator manufacturers/models are not disclosed.
Single-axis-tracking PV integrated directly with diesel generation without battery stabilization in 2019. Public sources reviewed do not identify module, inverter or tracker manufacturers/models.
5B reports a 10 MWp, 210-Maverick array deployed in 42 days by a small onsite team and designed to be removed/redeployed after Nova's short remaining mine life.
12 MW/12 MWh battery system. Reviewed public sources do not disclose cell chemistry, pack/PCS manufacturer, model, usable energy, warranty or state of health.
Sodium-sulfur chemistry distributed by BASF Stationary Energy Storage; selected for long duration and tolerance of mine-site temperatures approaching 50°C.

Cloud-prediction and integration controls forecast large cloud events and increase thermal generation before PV falls. Expanded controls coordinate battery, solar, synchronous condensers and diesel-free operation; algorithm and hardware details are not public.

technicalDetails.detail: IGO reports the expansion achieved the mine's first 100% renewable operating period, while Zenith describes the installed system as engine-off enabled when solar and storage conditions permit. Earlier targets of eight or nine consecutive hours were design objectives; no public annual hours-at-engine-off dataset was found.

A December 3 fire extensively damaged a 10 MW diesel engine room and suspended all mine operations. IGO said the fire did not damage the control room, switch room, solar PV, BESS or distribution network. IGO and Zenith deployed 14.9 MW of temporary generation and resumed operations within 18 days.

The original power station began in 2015, mine commercial production began in July 2017, the first hybrid solar phase was commissioned in 2019, and the battery/solar expansion reached commissioning and initial engine-off operation in FY2023 with further completion reporting in FY2024.

IGO announced in March 2025 that Nova is expected to reach end of life in late 2026. The 5B array was expressly chosen to reduce stranded-asset risk by permitting removal and redeployment; public material does not state plans for the diesel station, BESS, synchronous condensers or original solar after closure.

No current annual energy mix, engine-off hours, BESS cycling/state of health, PV curtailment, post-fire permanent-repair scope, diesel litres after expansion, plant availability or closure/redeployment plan was located.

nuie-island.summary: The slug and archived title misspell Niue, and the archived 480 kW 'solar' value is a load figure, not generation capacity. Niue's national island grid has accumulated several distinct solar/storage phases: an early Optimal Power Solutions/Mitsubishi/Toshiba fast-response battery; a 2019 Vector PowerSmart project that brought total PV to about 1.1 MW and added a 0.80 MW/3.15 MWh Tesla battery and central EMS; and a much larger 2.79 MWp PV/8.19 MWh BESS project at Hikufenoga/Tamakautoa that was still approaching testing and commissioning in the latest government update.
nuie-island.status: Existing island grid operating, major expansion not yet proven commissioned. The 2019 hybrid demonstrated diesel-off operation and initially supplied 28.6% of energy from solar. A November 2023 lightning strike affected the BESS and solar infrastructure, and Niue soft-launched a relocated/new power station in November 2024 while working to restore and reintegrate storage and solar. A July 2025 UNDP summit account reported 38% renewable penetration. The latest Government of Niue construction update says the NZD 20.5 million Hikufenoga project was nearing completion, with testing/commissioning ahead and transfer to Niue Power Corporation anticipated in mid-2026. No completion/acceptance notice was found by July 20, so the expansion should remain construction/commissioning rather than operational.

specifications.label: Legacy data correction
specifications.value: Niue had about 342-343 kWp of installed PV around 2014 against approximately 480 kW electricity demand; only a small share of potential solar reached the grid because of instability/curtailment. The archived record transformed that 480 kW demand into solar capacity and listed 200 kWh storage, neither of which matches the original context or OPS's current 300 kWh project description.

specifications.label: 2019 solar portfolio
specifications.value: 600 kW of new PV increased island-wide solar capacity to approximately 1.1 MW

specifications.label: 2019 battery
specifications.value: 0.80 MW / 3.15 MWh Tesla Powerpack 2 BESS at the power station, integrated with old and new solar and diesel generation

specifications.label: 2019 initial performance
specifications.value: Over a five-month reporting period from May 2019, solar supplied 28.6% of energy and avoided more than 130,000 litres of diesel. Diesel generators were off 5-7 hours/day on average and up to 10 hours; Vector's six-month corporate account reported 320,000 kWh from the new 600 kW array and up to 12 diesel-off hours on sunny days.

specifications.label: Current expansion
specifications.value: 2.79 MWp PV and 8.19 MWh BESS at Hikufenoga/Tamakautoa, plus electricity-network safety and resilience upgrades; more than 4,000 panels and about 1,000 concrete foundations were reported in the latest progress update

specifications.label: Current-project forecast
specifications.value: Government forecast of 816,000 litres of diesel and 2,202 tCO2e avoided in year one; the 2025 UNDP summit account separately cited up to 753,000 litres/year and more than 52,000 tCO2e across a 25-year asset life. Both are forecasts, with differing assumptions that are not reconciled in the public material.

specifications.label: Renewable-energy target and reported progress
specifications.value: 80% renewable electricity target; a 2025 UNDP account reported 38% penetration before the new 2.79 MWp project was commissioned

equipment.detail: 0.80 MW/3.15 MWh lithium-ion system. The public project material does not state module count, usable energy, warranty or present state of health.
Bespoke EMS balances legacy/new solar, diesel sets and battery charge state; it starts/stops diesel, sends solar directly to load or storage, and reduces curtailment while maintaining grid stability.

OPS currently describes 300 kWh of lithium-titanate storage integrated for Mitsubishi, delivering roughly 2C-3C transient response to cloud ramps and a rock-crusher load. A regional source also mentions a 120 kW battery system. The archived 200 kWh value and 500 kVA GSC label are not corroborated by the current OPS page.
2.79 MWp of PV and 8.19 MWh of battery storage are under construction. The reviewed government sources do not name panel, inverter, battery-cell, PCS or energy-management-system manufacturers/models.

The island historically used four 500 kVA diesel generators. The new station soft-launched in November 2024 includes two new generators, while both old and new stations remained available during transition; new generator manufacturers and ratings were not published.

technicalDetails.detail: OPS GSC Inverter –
200 kWh Lithium Ion

The EMS unified pre-existing solar arrays, 600 kW of new solar, the Tesla BESS and diesel generators on a live island network. Storage absorbed solar variability, shifted energy and allowed diesel engines to stop during favorable periods.

A November 2023 lightning strike affected solar and BESS infrastructure. By the November 2024 new-power-station launch, government reported grid-control repair, cabling, fuel-tank and lightning-protection work and preliminary commissioning involving ComAp, Arnolds Switchboards, Generator Services and Tesla; reintegration was then expected in December. No final acceptance/test result was located.

The latest government update says over 4,000 panels were installed, roughly 85% of concrete works were complete and major BESS foundations were in place, with testing/commissioning and planned outages still ahead. Although its page gives a July 2026 completion date and says mid-2026 handover, that schedule is not evidence of completion.

Government pages label content for the same Hikufenoga/Tamakautoa, 2.79 MWp/8.19 MWh activity under different phase headings ('Phase 1' and 'Phase 3'). The capacities should be counted once, and the project should be identified by site and scope rather than page heading alone.

This is Niue's national isolated electricity system serving government, households, commercial loads and industry, not a standalone community microgrid at one building. Diesel remains necessary while renewable/storage assets are repaired and expanded.

No current hourly/annual energy mix, diesel generation, outage indices, tariff/savings result, battery capacity test, lightning-damage inventory, 2026 acceptance certificate or as-built vendor schedule for the new project was found.

nullagine.summary: Nullagine is the smaller, electrically separate town system in Horizon Power's two-town Marble Bar and Nullagine solar-diesel program; it is about 88 km from Marble Bar and is not connected to it. The original Nullagine plant combined 203 kW of tracked photovoltaic generation, three 320 kW diesel units, a 500 kW/16.5 MW-s flywheel and ABB Microgrid Plus controls. It was integrated in October 2010. Contemporary reporting put solar at roughly 30-40% of annual energy, while daylight or instantaneous shares were much higher. The legacy catalog's 300 kW solar and 1.28 MW diesel values are Marble Bar's ratings, not Nullagine's, and the catalog's 18,000 kW flywheel value is a unit/transcription error.
nullagine.status: Nullagine remains an active Horizon Power service area, but the utility's 2024-25 annual report lists it among non-performing systems affected by generation outages and network issues; town-specific SAIDI/SAIFI is not published there. No current source found establishes whether the 2010 flywheel is still available or gives a recent solar share. A separate long-duration storage trial began in 2023. ARENA initially announced a Redflow 100 kW/400 kWh zinc-bromine battery for Nullagine, but Horizon Power later selected and procured EnerVenue nickel-hydrogen technology. The trial runs to 2028; neither commissioning nor the replacement battery's power/energy rating was publicly confirmed through July 2026. Treat the Redflow rating as a superseded plan, not installed equipment.

equipment.detail: 900 single-axis tracked panels totaling 203 kW in the original 2010 installation.
500 kW, 16.5 MW-s flywheel used for fast balancing so solar could displace diesel at high instantaneous penetration. Current availability was not established.
Coordinated tracked solar, diesel units and PowerStore flywheel in the original system.
Nickel-hydrogen technology selected and procured for the current trial. Model, power, energy, inverter and commissioning date were not disclosed in the sources reviewed.

technicalDetails.detail: PV, diesel, storage

Nullagine and Marble Bar were delivered as one program but are separate isolated town power systems approximately 88 km apart. Capacities must be assigned by town rather than treated as one connected microgrid.

Nullagine's renewable system was integrated in October 2010. The two-town Marble Bar/Nullagine program cost about A$14 million and received a shared A$4.9 million Australian Government grant; those portfolio values cannot be allocated wholly to Nullagine.

The original case study reports average interruption duration improving from 110 minutes in 2009-10 to zero in 2010-11. ABB's presentation estimates roughly 400,000 litres of annual diesel displacement and 1,100 tonnes of CO2 reduction, and describes about 60% daytime renewable penetration. These are historical vendor/case-study figures, not current audited results.

The 2010 flywheel was short-duration balancing equipment. The later ARENA/Horizon project is a distinct long-duration trial intended to test storage exceeding eight hours and DERMS coordination. A 2024 Redflow plan was replaced by EnerVenue nickel-hydrogen technology, so neither project phase should be conflated with the other.

Horizon Power's 2024-25 annual report includes Nullagine among its non-performing systems and says generation outages and network problems—including weather and wildlife impacts across the listed systems—affected reliability. It does not isolate a Nullagine-specific cause or publish town-level metrics in that passage.

No public source found through July 2026 gives current Nullagine generation by fuel, present diesel fleet condition, flywheel availability, solar output, or commissioning/performance results for the EnerVenue battery.

nusa-penida.summary: Nusa Penida is PLN's isolated 'Three Nusa' electricity system serving Nusa Penida, Nusa Lembongan and Nusa Ceningan, Bali. Its present renewable core is the Suana hybrid plant: 4.2 MWp DC/3.5 MW AC of solar and a 3 MW battery integrated with diesel generation. Battery energy is reported inconsistently: PLN, the energy ministry and Hitachi's service description give 1.8-1.84 MWh through 2025, while a Hitachi equipment table and a May 2026 PLN Indonesia Power field account say 3 MWh. The plant entered service for the 2022 G20 period, and PLN formally inaugurated an automated Smart Microgrid in November 2025. This is distinct from an older, poorly performing wind-solar-diesel demonstration begun in 2006-09.
nusa-penida.status: The Suana solar-battery-diesel system is operational, and PLN reports the Smart Microgrid operating since November 2025 with automatic real-time dispatch across the three islands. Diesel remains dominant: a May 2026 field account puts installed diesel capacity at about 21 MW against a 16 MW peak load. Through August 2024 the solar plant had generated 3.7 GWh, 6.57% of system generation, at an 18% realized capacity factor. PLN's 2025-34 plan identifies an additional 4.5 MW solar-plus-storage project for 2026-27 and 10 MW wind-plus-storage for 2027-28; these are plans, not commissioned capacity.

Energy is 1.84 MWh in Hitachi's service agreement and narrative project description, using nine battery racks and three battery inverters; PLN and the ministry similarly report 1.8-1.84 MWh. A Hitachi equipment table and May 2026 PLN Indonesia Power field account instead say 3 MWh. The public record does not explain whether this is nameplate-versus-usable energy, an upgrade or a reporting error; no reviewed source supports the recovered catalog's 3.3 MWh figure.

specifications.label: Current customer and system scale
specifications.value: PLN reported more than 23,000 customers, 60.91 MVA of installed customer capacity and 59.82 GWh of energy transactions through August 2025 across the Three Nusa system. These are system/service metrics, not generation capacity or renewable output.

specifications.label: Historical wind fleet
specifications.value: A 2014 study describes nine turbines totaling 735 kW; the recovered catalog reports 720 kW. The dated conflict is retained, and neither value is counted as current because the 2025 Smart Microgrid description omits wind.

specifications.label: Proposed expansion
specifications.value: RUPTL 2025-34 planning cited in May 2026 identifies 4.5 MW of additional solar plus BESS for 2026-27 and 10 MW of wind plus BESS for 2027-28. Storage ratings and procurement/commissioning were not established.

equipment.detail: PLN and ministry sources identify BESS capacity but do not disclose the cell, inverter, EMS, or controller manufacturers/models.

Hitachi identifies a modular CATL lithium-iron-phosphate battery. Its service agreement and narrative rate the installed system 3 MW/1.84 MWh with nine racks and three battery inverters; another Hitachi table and a May 2026 field account say 3 MWh, retained as an unresolved source inconsistency.
Suana ground-mounted array rated 4.2 MWp DC/3.5 MW AC on roughly 4.5 hectares. Module and inverter manufacturers were not established by the reviewed primary sources.
Hitachi's control layer monitors system anomalies and dispatches the battery, while PLN's later smart-microgrid layer automatically balances solar, storage and diesel across the Three Nusa network.
Nine small wind turbines and two small PV plants were part of the 2006-09 demonstration. That older configuration had no battery and is not evidence of current wind availability.

technicalDetails.detail: Wind, PV, diesel

The Smart Microgrid is not limited to one plant or one island: it coordinates the isolated electricity system serving Nusa Penida, Nusa Lembongan and Nusa Ceningan. Suana is the principal solar/BESS site and diesel generation remains the firm supply.

Small wind and solar resources were added from 2006 through 2009. The modern Suana plant was commissioned ahead of the November 2022 G20 summit; Hitachi documented a two-year service program, and PLN formally inaugurated the automated Smart Microgrid on 13 November 2025.

The 2014 evaluation found the older wind-solar-diesel system contributed only about 3.23% of generation versus 6.28% expected, citing harsh climate and operating difficulties. These findings apply to the pre-BESS demonstration, not the 2022 Suana plant.

The 3 MW battery provides PV smoothing and dispatch support while the smart controls continuously coordinate solar, stored energy and diesel output. Whether its energy rating is 1.84 or 3 MWh, the cited values describe roughly 0.6-1 hour at full power, so it is a short-duration balancing resource rather than long-duration storage.
Hitachi's documented service agreement included inspections and testing every six months, remote troubleshooting and knowledge transfer to operations personnel over two years.

PLN projected the Suana solar plant would avoid about 4,190 tonnes CO2e per year. This is a utility estimate, while the ministry's later 3.7 GWh/6.57% figures are observed production through August 2024.

nushima-island-dc-microgrid.summary: This was a fiscal-2012-to-2014 Japanese Ministry of the Environment demonstration on Nushima Island in Minamiawaji, Hyogo Prefecture. It is not Naoshima in Kagawa, as the recovered catalog's historical location says, and it is unrelated to the Niijima smart-grid project. The experiment linked three small 360 V DC microgrids, 8 kW of photovoltaic generation, 1 kW of wind and a 46 kWh stationary lithium-ion battery, supplemented by the island's AC utility supply. It also tested mobile batteries, ship-to-grid power from a plug-in hybrid boat, direct-DC appliances, visualized dynamic pricing and demand response.
nushima-island-dc-microgrid.status: The funded technology demonstration concluded after fiscal 2014. The reviewed sources document the prototype, conversion performance and behavioral trials, but do not establish that it became an island-wide production microgrid or remained in service after the project. No authoritative operating, replacement or decommissioning update through July 2026 was found. The historic system was grid-assisted and experimental, not a self-sufficient renewable power system for all of Nushima.

equipment.detail: Controls battery charge/discharge from total power balance and state of charge across a 360 VDC bus; no commercial controller model is identified.
High-frequency-link DC/DC converters, sinusoidal PWM DC/AC inverter, AC/DC grid interface, and converters for stationary/mobile batteries and ship charging.
46 kWh lithium-ion storage connected to the 360 V DC architecture. Manufacturer, cell chemistry subtype and model were not identified in the reviewed publications.
High-frequency DC/DC converters supplied low-voltage DC loads, while a DC/AC inverter supported conventional 100 V rms loads and a bidirectional converter interfaced with the utility network.
Two forms of transportable battery and a 96 V mobile architecture were tested to move stored energy among the separated DC microgrid sites.
A plug-in hybrid boat was used as a mobile energy resource in the S2G experiment. The project source does not establish a commercial vessel or battery model.
Prototype loads included equipment designed to accept the 360 V DC bus directly, avoiding an additional conversion stage.

technicalDetails.detail: The site is Nushima Island (沼島), administered by Minamiawaji in Hyogo Prefecture. Naoshima in Kagawa is a different island; the recovered catalog's Naoshima location is erroneous.

Three separated DC microgrid sites combined local renewables, stationary and mobile storage, an AC-grid interface, direct-DC and conventional AC loads, plus supervisory information exchange. Energy could be moved physically by battery or boat rather than through a continuous island-wide DC cable.

Laboratory/field measurements reported more than 90% conversion efficiency when charging the stationary battery and whole-system conversion efficiency around 80%, with some configurations above 70%. These are component/prototype measurements, not an annual microgrid efficiency or renewable share.

The Hyogo project account reports that combining visualized variable pricing with the distributed-energy trials reduced electricity consumption by about 30%. The source does not provide a long-term normalized baseline or claim that generation alone caused the reduction.

The Ministry of the Environment demonstration ran during fiscal years 2012-2014. A 2015 symposium presentation documented the concluded experiment and its mobile-battery, ship-to-grid and dynamic-pricing elements.
No current operator page, later commercial deployment, annual generation record, permanent islanding test or post-demonstration asset disposition was found through July 2026.

nyu-microgrid.summary: The NYU Microgrid is New York University's Washington Square campus combined-heat-and-power system, not NYU Langone Health's separate 11 MW Energy Building or NYU Tandon research microgrids. The plant entered full operation in January 2011 with two dual-fuel Solar Taurus 60 gas turbines, heat-recovery steam generators and a 2.4 MW steam turbine, for 13.4 MW peak electrical output. It distributes electricity to 26 university buildings and thermal service to 44. The recovered catalog's 5.5 MW capacity is one gas turbine's nominal rating, not the whole CHP plant.
nyu-microgrid.status: NYU's own 2021 campus account describes the cogeneration plant as serving its Washington Square network, and no authoritative source found through July 2026 reports retirement. It remains best classified as an operational grid-connected CHP microgrid capable of separating from Con Edison, with natural gas as its normal fuel and liquid-fuel capability for backup. NYU has pursued campus decarbonization/net-zero planning, but no reviewed source establishes replacement of the plant, addition of BESS/PV to this microgrid, or a current annual availability/emissions result.

equipment.detail: Two nominal 5.5 MW dual-fuel turbine-generator packages; natural gas is the normal CHP fuel and liquid-fuel capability provides resilience.
Two HRSGs recover combustion-turbine exhaust heat and include duct firing to increase steam output.
Redundant ControlLogix PLC architecture with FactoryTalk supervisory software, control panels, instrumentation and SCADA integration delivered by Thermo Systems.
2.4 MW turbine-generator powered by recovered steam; manufacturer/model was not established in the reviewed sources.
Approximately 2,400-2,500 tons of cooling capacity integrated with the campus thermal network; manufacturer/model was not established.

technicalDetails.detail: This record covers NYU's Washington Square campus CHP/microgrid. NYU Langone Health operates a separate hospital energy facility, and NYU Tandon conducts separate microgrid research; their capacities and equipment must not be added here.

The plant uses two combustion turbines with separate HRSGs, a common recovered-steam system, a 2.4 MW steam turbine, district thermal distribution, automated controls and a utility intertie with protective relaying. It can operate as a grid-connected CHP plant or electrically isolate the campus network.

During the October 2012 regional blackout, the system separated from the grid and maintained electricity to the connected campus core, including essential services. This is demonstrated islanding performance, not merely a design claim.

The commissioning account reports approximately 80% overall CHP efficiency. NYU/New York sources estimated about 23% lower carbon emissions than the prior oil-fired plant; the NYSERDA report also records NYU's estimate of 57,500 tons/year and 38% reduction under its study assumptions. These dated estimates use different baselines and should not be presented as one current audited result.
The New York State 2100 Commission estimated annual utility-cost savings of roughly US$5-8 million. This is a post-Sandy policy-report estimate rather than a current NYU financial disclosure.

No authoritative post-2021 source found through July 2026 gives present heat/electric output, fuel consumption, islanding-test frequency, emissions, outage statistics or major equipment replacement. A state-supported net-zero study shows decarbonization planning but does not establish retirement or conversion of this plant.

oneida-nation-of-the-thames.summary: This record covers the solar-and-storage work of Oneida Nation of the Thames in Ontario, Canada. It is not the Oneida Indian Nation in New York and has no connection to the 250 MW/1,000 MWh Oneida Energy Storage project in Haldimand County. Federal and Ontario governments funded a community-housing solar microgrid in 2016. The clearest project-specific later account is JAZZ Solar's 2021 installation of a 650 kW net-metered solar system with battery storage spanning the Oneida Nation School, Community Centre, Health and Education Centre and Seniors Citizens Home. Oneida subsequently formed Onyota’a:ka Kahsatst^sla’ Ltd., also branded Solar Energy: OK Ltd., to own and operate community solar assets and train an Oneida energy team.
oneida-nation-of-the-thames.status: Oneida's current environment page describes existing solar systems under monitoring, safety and troubleshooting training, supporting continued community operation, while JAZZ documents the 2021 650 kW campus project. JAZZ catalogs a Family Healing Lodge solar job under 2024, but Oneida's owner page says the program is working on its installation; completion and capacity are therefore unverified. Public sources do not disclose the Oneida battery's power, energy, chemistry, manufacturer, commissioning tests or recent performance. A 2023 federal release describes Taykwa Tagamou Nation and Oneida collectively as having built 'Community Solar (1 MW) and Storage (38 kWh) projects'; those ratings cannot safely be assigned to Oneida alone.

Oneida's Energy Team is being trained in system monitoring, performance analysis, safety and troubleshooting. The owner page does not name a commercial EMS, SCADA or monitoring-platform product.

technicalDetails.detail: Awarded $814,568 from Ontario and $814,568 from the federal government to develop solar-powered microgrid

The community is Oneida Nation of the Thames in Ontario. Do not combine this project with Oneida Indian Nation projects in New York or the unrelated Oneida Energy Storage utility-scale battery in Haldimand County, Ontario.

Public funding in 2016 described a housing solar microgrid without a rating. By 2020 Oneida had established a dedicated operating subsidiary, and the vendor's 2021 project account describes a 650 kW net-metered solar-plus-battery system across major community facilities. The sources do not explicitly prove whether this is the exact final configuration of the 2016 housing scope or an evolved/additional phase.

The documented 2021 asset is a multi-building, net-metered photovoltaic system paired with battery storage. Public evidence does not establish islanding capability, critical-load transfer, black start, feeder topology or the battery-control strategy, so 'microgrid' should not be expanded into unsupported technical claims.

Natural Resources Canada announced more than C$1 million collectively for Taykwa Tagamou Nation and Oneida training, aimed at distributed-energy skills, grid modernization and preparation to consider community energy storage. The funding total is shared and cannot be allocated wholly to Oneida.

No current generation history, renewable share, avoided fuel/emissions, storage dispatch data, outage performance, interconnection agreement, battery health or independent commissioning record was found through July 2026.

otis-air-national-guard-base.summary: The Otis project at the 102nd Intelligence Wing on Joint Base Cape Cod, Massachusetts, was designed to combine a 1.5 MW wind turbine, a 1.6 MW diesel generator and a 1.6 MW/1.2 MWh lead-acid UltraBattery under Raytheon's Intelligent Power and Energy Management controller. It was unveiled in August 2018 and widely described as the first U.S. Department of Defense wind-powered microgrid. The December 2020 ESTCP final report materially qualifies that claim: the battery successfully entered ISO New England frequency-regulation service, but the final wind-turbine feeder connection was not completed, the existing diesel could not be used for extended islanding without a policy waiver, and the full hybrid microgrid's five-day islanding objective was not demonstrated.
otis-air-national-guard-base.status: The last authoritative project-closeout evidence classifies the BESS as operational in grid-tied ISO New England regulation, with successful testing, while the complete wind-diesel-battery islanding configuration remained incomplete. The final report says the wind feeder and fiber were built but the last transformer/turbine connection was outstanding; the diesel was unavailable to the demonstration and was being replaced/right-sized. No later primary source found through July 2026 confirms those integration gaps were closed. Therefore this should not be labeled an operating full hybrid microgrid solely from the 2018 ribbon-cutting coverage.

technicalDetails.detail: The intended architecture placed grid-forming battery storage at the center of an islandable feeder with wind, standby diesel, automated switching, fiber communications and Raytheon controls. About three miles of electric cable, 165 utility poles and four miles of fiber were installed, but physical infrastructure completion did not equal a completed system-level islanding demonstration.

The storage system successfully completed grid-tied characterization and participated in ISO New England frequency regulation. The final report estimates gross regulation revenue around US$24.80/hour, roughly US$217,000/year for continuous availability, and a projected net value of about US$178,000 per MW-year; these are modeled/period-specific economics, not a current revenue disclosure.

The project did not conduct the planned 120-hour islanding test. High-wind operation was tested only in hardware-in-the-loop simulations, the diesel could not be made available under the applicable Air Force instruction without a waiver, and the wind turbine was not finally tied to the new feeder.

One inverter module failed near final reporting and was scheduled for warranty replacement in January 2021. No later source reviewed confirms that replacement or reports current battery state of health.

The August 2018 unveiling and contemporaneous ISO New England account described an operational microgrid and market opportunity. The later final report is controlling evidence for outcome: only the BESS/grid-services path was fully demonstrated, while wind/diesel islanding integration remained unfinished.

over-yonder-cay-minigrid.summary: Over Yonder Cay is a private-island resort microgrid in the Exumas, Bahamas. The currently described system combines 720 kW DC of photovoltaics, three Northern Power Systems NPS 100-21 wind turbines totaling 300 kW, a 6 MWh battery bank, two parallel 300 kVA bidirectional multiport inverters and three 250 kW Caterpillar diesel generators. The resort reports 96% renewable energy, using predictive controls to dispatch generation, storage and flexible loads such as desalination and thermal uses. The plant evolved materially from its initial 2010-13 configuration, so the recovered catalog's 375 kW solar capacity describes an earlier phase, not the current owner-listed array.
over-yonder-cay-minigrid.status: The resort and its renewable system remain actively presented by the owner, which reports 96% renewable supply and rare diesel operation. Initial commissioning occurred in April 2010, the first two wind turbines followed in October 2010, and a third was added in 2011; the solar and storage plant was later expanded to 720 kW DC and 6 MWh. Current performance is owner-reported rather than independently audited, and no public annual generation, fuel-use, battery-health or outage dataset through July 2026 was found. Battery chemistry, manufacturer and replacement history remain undisclosed.

Three 250 kW backup generators in the current owner listing.

Provides on-island weather measurements for operating awareness and predictive control.

A compact rack-mounted intelligent controller and SCADA system forecast weather, demand and battery state, dispatch generators/flexible loads, archive data and support simulation. No current commercial product name was disclosed.

technicalDetails.detail: Solar DC power is coupled through a 750 kW MPPT stage toward the central battery/inverter system; wind and diesel feed the island electrical network, while two bidirectional multiport inverters regulate power exchange with storage. Automated controls coordinate firm, variable and deferrable resources.

When renewable production exceeds immediate electrical needs, controls route energy to desalination/water production, pool or water heating and other schedulable loads, reducing curtailment and diesel starts.

The owner's technical page literally calls the island network a '13.2 kVA distribution loop' connected through 480 VAC transformers. Because kVA is not a voltage rating and no corroborating one-line diagram was found, the likely intended 13.2 kV interpretation is not silently substituted.

Optimal Power Solutions commissioned the initial system in April 2010. The first two wind turbines were installed in October 2010, a third was added in 2011, and the wind fleet passed 1 million kWh of cumulative generation by mid-2013. Solar and storage ratings in later owner material show subsequent expansion.

Northern Power reports that the turbines survived Hurricane Irene in 2011. The owner's six-megawatt-hour battery is described as roughly two days of autonomy, but public sources do not give the load basis, critical-load fraction or tested black-start sequence behind that claim.

The original vendor described an approximately US$6 million project with a projected five-to-six-year payback. No public source reviewed provides realized payback, current operating cost, annual fuel savings, battery degradation or independently verified 96% renewable performance.

pearl-harbour.summary: This slug originated as SPIDERS Phase I at Joint Base Pearl Harbor-Hickam (JBPHH), Hawaii: a 2011-13 Department of Defense/Department of Energy cyber-secure microgrid demonstration integrating about 150 kW of solar, up to 2.4 MW of diesel generation, an uninterruptible-power system and automated controls. It must not be conflated with later projects on the same joint base. SPIDERS demonstrated black start, high instantaneous PV penetration and diesel savings, but a 2018 Navy-microgrid retrospective reported that the system had become idle after operator turnover and an expired password. Separately, the Hawaii Air National Guard's PEARL microgrid completed testing in March 2024 with a 1 MW/600 kWh battery and 1 MW diesel generator, and further expansion was contracted in 2025. Ameresco's 99 MW Puʻuloa microgrid and 6 MW Puʻuloa Solar-plus-storage project are additional development-stage projects, not SPIDERS upgrades or commissioned capacity.
pearl-harbour.status: SPIDERS Phase I was a completed demonstration, but its current operability is unverified; the last substantive outcome source found says it was idle in 2018. PEARL is the current demonstrated microgrid at the 154th Wing: HTDC reports successful testing in March 2024 and awarded Burns & McDonnell a US$1.67 million 2025 contract for continued development and added renewables. Puʻuloa remains separate and in development. The 99 MW front-of-meter microgrid received a 2024 DOE GRIP selection, while Hawaii's current project listing describes the 6 MW solar/30 MWh storage project as under Public Utilities Commission review with 2027 commercial operation. Capacities from these phases must not be summed as one Pearl Harbor system.

specifications.value: 1 MW/600 kWh in HTDC's current project description. Earlier design material reported 500 kWh, so 600 kWh is treated as the completed/tested configuration rather than combined with the older value.

specifications.label: PEARL diesel and load
specifications.value: 1 MW Tier 2 diesel generator serving an anticipated peak microgrid load of about 1 MW, including critical and non-critical facilities.

specifications.label: Puʻuloa front-of-meter microgrid proposal
specifications.value: 99 MW planned microgrid/energy asset with firm and flexible generation, controllers and electrical infrastructure. The 2024 DOE selection is development support, not evidence of commissioning.

specifications.label: Puʻuloa Solar proposal
specifications.value: 6 MW photovoltaic generation with a 6 MW/30 MWh BESS in the current Hawaii project listing. The Navy's 2024 environmental assessment analyzed 6 MW/24 MWh, approximately 10,950 modules and 39 BESS blocks; the difference reflects scope evolution and is retained explicitly.

equipment.detail: Remote HMI, black-start sequencing, solar integration, and secure generation control were demonstrated; the reviewed federal overview does not name the controller model.
UPS supported designated uninterruptible loads; manufacturer and energy capacity not stated.

Cyber-secure controller, automated switchgear/protection and human-machine interface integrated existing solar, diesel, utility supply and uninterruptible loads. Burns & McDonnell, IPERC, Parsons and IPKeys were project partners, but the reviewed sources do not assign a definitive commercial controller model.

UPS bridged the critical uninterruptible load during transitions/black start. Manufacturer, model, energy capacity and verified 1,250 kW rating were not established by the reviewed federal performance material.

1 MW/600 kWh system used for excess-solar capture, generator-start support and seamless grid/island transitions. Public HTDC material reviewed does not name its battery manufacturer, chemistry or model.
1 MW Tier 2 generator paired with battery storage, automated switchgear and microgrid controls; manufacturer/model was not disclosed in the reviewed owner page.

Proposed 6 MW storage power with 30 MWh current planned energy; Navy environmental analysis described 39 BESS blocks at a then-current 24 MWh scope. Equipment vendors are not established in the reviewed sources.

technicalDetails.detail: SPIDERS Phase I, PEARL, the 99 MW Puʻuloa microgrid and Puʻuloa Solar are separate projects at or associated with JBPHH, with different hosts, dates, architectures and status. Their generation and storage ratings are not one aggregate installation.

Federal closeout material reports successful grid disconnection, black start and reconnection; photovoltaic penetration reached about 90% at times, the microgrid exported roughly 1 MW to the grid in testing, and coordinated dispatch reduced diesel use by nearly 30% in the test conditions. These are demonstration results, not annual current performance.

A 2018 industry retrospective on Navy microgrids reported that operator turnover and an expired password left the roughly US$4 million SPIDERS asset sitting idle. No later primary source found through July 2026 confirms recommissioning, so its present status remains unverified rather than assumed operating.

PEARL uses battery storage, a diesel generator, automated switchgear and controls to serve critical and non-critical 154th Wing loads, support black start and transition between utility-connected and island modes. HTDC reports completed construction and successful testing in March 2024.

In April 2025 HTDC awarded Burns & McDonnell a US$1.67 million contract for continued microgrid development, expansion and additional renewable integration. This supports ongoing enhancement, not a second 1 MW system to add to the first.

Ameresco announced DOE GRIP selection in October 2024 for a 99 MW front-of-meter microgrid with smart-grid technologies, controllers and electrical infrastructure. Separately, the current Hawaii energy project listing shows the 6 MW solar/30 MWh storage project under PUC review with 2027 commercial operation. Neither is counted as operating through July 2026.

penetanguishene-microgrid.summary: The Penetanguishene project is a utility distribution-feeder microgrid built beside the former PowerStream Robert Street Municipal Substation, not a town-wide renewable mini-grid. Its physical DER is a 750 kW/500 kWh Samsung lithium-ion battery—only about 40 minutes at rated output—plus five automatic circuit switches, a step-voltage regulator, communications, and KEPCO's MiDAS controller. The project can island one feeder serving roughly 400–440 customers, but no public source establishes installed on-site solar, wind, or fuel-fired generation inside this microgrid boundary.
penetanguishene-microgrid.status: PowerStream and KEPCO commissioned the system on June 29, 2016. Canada's Smart Renewables and Electrification Pathways registry still labels it operational, with a February 2021 project update and a page modification in June 2023. PowerStream became part of Alectra Utilities in 2017. No asset-specific 2024–2026 operating report, retirement notice, battery augmentation, or replacement has been found, so the best supportable current status is operational as last publicly confirmed—not independently verified continuously operating in 2026.

specifications.label: Storage
specifications.value: 1 MW

specifications.label: Battery power and energy
specifications.value: 750 kW/500 kWh lithium-ion, equivalent to 0.67 hours (40 minutes) at full rated discharge. Older descriptions calling it a '1 MW' system are not supported by the commissioned nameplate.

specifications.label: Served boundary
specifications.value: A single distribution feeder serving about 400 customers in PowerStream's launch release and about 440 households in KEPCO reporting. A separate 7.2 MW figure describes the feeder/system scale, not installed DER capacity.

specifications.label: Backup-duration claim
specifications.value: PowerStream said the system could supply several hours of backup to approximately 400 customers. Because 500 kWh cannot sustain 750 kW for several hours, that statement necessarily assumes load shedding and/or a substantially lower critical-load level; the public release does not quantify it.

specifications.label: Energy mix
specifications.value: Normally Ontario grid electricity; while islanded, energy previously stored from that grid. The controls were designed to accommodate future distributed generation, but no validated source shows commissioned PV, wind, CHP, or diesel generation within the project.

specifications.label: Reported funding split
specifications.value: A 2017 technical presentation reports KEPCO contributing C$2.7 million and PowerStream C$1.8 million. These are partner-reported contributions, not a verified final all-in capital cost.

equipment.detail: 500 kWh stationary lithium-ion battery coupled to a 750 kW power-conversion system; exact cell, rack, and inverter model numbers were not disclosed.

equipment.model: MiDAS (Microgrid Distributed Energy Resource Automation System)
equipment.detail: Coordinates automatic grid/island transitions, battery dispatch, protection/switching, voltage management, and remote operation.
Five automatic circuit switches, a step-voltage regulator, automation, communications, and SCADA/control-centre interfaces.

technicalDetails.detail: MiDAS was demonstrated in grid-connected and autonomous island modes and is intended to optimize operation without continuous operator intervention while retaining control-centre and KEPCO remote visibility.

The installation tested feeder-level resilience, voltage support, peak/energy management, distribution automation, and a commercializable Korean-Canadian microgrid control package—not primarily renewable-energy production.

A later industry case study says the served area experienced no power interruptions after implementation, but it does not provide the observation window, event count, or independent utility reliability indices. It should be treated as a case-study claim rather than audited SAIDI/SAIFI evidence.

The federal registry is the latest asset-specific public status found. Alectra's current innovation pages do not provide battery condition, availability, dispatch, replacement, or outage-performance data for Penetanguishene; absence from a marketing list is not evidence of retirement.

philadelphia-navy-yard-microgrid.summary: The Philadelphia Navy Yard microgrid is PIDC's privately managed, unregulated campus distribution system on the redeveloped civilian Navy Yard—not a U.S. Navy base microgrid. The current public portfolio includes an 8 MW four-engine natural-gas peaking plant, a 6.4 MW/14.8 MWh Citizens Energy lithium-ion battery, roughly 400 kW of community solar (more than 1,200 panels producing about 930 MWh/year), and a tenant-owned 600 kW Bloom fuel-cell installation, alongside grid imports. Building 7R/GridSTAR is a distinct nested research microgrid and should not be silently added to every campus-wide total.
philadelphia-navy-yard-microgrid.status: The campus grid is operating and currently advertised at 44 MW. Dominion Energy began a 10-year utility operations-and-maintenance contract in November 2021. The final 6.4 MW/14.8 MWh BESS was dedicated in August 2021 and participates in peak management and PJM frequency regulation. PIDC reported a US$31 million grid expansion/upgrade program in its 2024 annual review. Public sources do not provide a recent islanding test, outage-coverage duration, annual fuel/energy mix, or availability factor, so resilience capability should not be overstated as demonstrated indefinite island operation.

specifications.value: NA

specifications.label: Campus grid rating
specifications.value: Current Navy Yard materials state 44 MW. A 2021 engineering page calls it 52 MW, likely reflecting a different configuration/rating date; both are preserved rather than presented as simultaneous generation capacity.

specifications.label: Campus demand and service scale
specifications.value: In 2021 the grid served 105 accounts in about 120 facilities with a 33 MW peak and an expected future load near 50 MW.

specifications.label: Battery
specifications.value: 6.4 MW/14.8 MWh lithium-ion, about 2.31 hours at full output, comprising 2,240 batteries in three 53-foot containers. Earlier project material used 6.2 MW; 6.4 MW is the final dedicated rating.

specifications.label: Natural-gas generation

specifications.label: Solar
specifications.value: The original community-solar array is reported as 400 kW; the current Navy Yard page reports more than 1,200 panels and approximately 930,000 kWh/year. Occasional rounded '1 MW solar' program slides should not replace the specific array rating without a source documenting an expansion.

specifications.label: Fuel cell
specifications.value: 600 kW Bloom Energy fuel-cell installation owned/hosted by Urban Outfitters and net-metered through the private grid; fuel consumption and annual output are not public.

specifications.label: Energy mix gap
specifications.value: The normal mix includes PECO/PJM imports, natural-gas peaking generation, solar, the tenant fuel cell, and battery charging/discharging. No current public source allocates annual MWh or emissions by source, and the BESS is not net generation.

equipment.detail: 6.4 MW/14.8 MWh lithium-ion system in three 53-foot containers with 2,240 battery units. Citizens Energy is owner/operator; public final sources do not establish a cell or PCS model.
Four continuous-duty reciprocating engine-generator sets, 8 MW aggregate, on a 13.2 kV interconnection. Exact engine models were not found in public owner/EPC pages.
600 kW tenant-owned installation at Urban Outfitters.
Approximately 400 kW rooftop array under a power-purchase arrangement; more than 1,200 modules and approximately 930 MWh/year on the current site page.

technicalDetails.detail: Peak shaving/demand management for the private grid and frequency-regulation participation in PJM; Citizens Energy owns and dispatches the asset under its project arrangement.

PIDC operates an unregulated/private campus network rather than a conventional regulated utility franchise. The gas plant connects at 13.2 kV and the project evolved incrementally, which is why it has been described as an 'unintentional microgrid.'

Penn State's Building 7R/GridSTAR demonstrator is a CHP-enabled renewable submicrogrid inside the Navy Yard. GE/Alstom controller and micro-PMU projects were R&D/test activities; their goals should not be read as proof that every advertised control function is deployed across today's entire 44 MW network.

PIDC's 2024 annual review reports a US$31 million expansion and upgrade of the energy grid to accommodate campus growth, confirming active infrastructure investment but not adding a new disclosed DER nameplate.

No recent public source reports a full-campus black-start/islanding test, critical-load MW, island duration, fuel inventory, battery round-trip efficiency, annual savings, or outage reliability indices.

port-jefferson-microgrid-feasibility-stu.summary: Port Jefferson was a 2015–2016 NY Prize Stage 1 feasibility concept for a large village resilience network, not a built microgrid. The design would have combined 3.2 MW of existing hospital/water/wastewater standby diesel, 5.2 MW of new natural-gas CCHP/reciprocating generation, 298 kW total solar, 550 kW/2.2 MWh of four-hour batteries, hospital absorption chillers/thermal storage, and four isolation switches over PSEG Long Island distribution infrastructure. It is distinct from National Grid's Port Jefferson power station and later power-station repowering/storage discussions.
port-jefferson-microgrid-feasibility-stu.status: The Stage 1 feasibility report was completed August 31, 2016. The project does not appear among NYSERDA's awarded Stage 2 detailed-design projects, and no procurement, construction, commissioning, or operating evidence was found through July 2026. The Village's current Power Plant Working Group continues to explore future uses for the separate National Grid generation site; that does not establish that the NY Prize microgrid was built. Status should therefore remain feasibility completed / not advanced to documented implementation.

specifications.label: Storage
specifications.value: NA

specifications.label: Proposed served load
specifications.value: 44,206 MWh/year; 8,916 kW noncoincident peak and 7,788 kW modeled coincident July peak. The boundary included roughly 1,300 residences, 250 small businesses, two hospitals, water/wastewater, schools, Village facilities, fire/DPW, ferry, and downtown loads.

specifications.label: Existing standby diesel in concept
specifications.value: 3.2 MW total: Mather Hospital 2 x 500 kW; St. Charles Hospital 900 + 300 + 250 + 250 + 100 kW; Suffolk County Water Authority 100 kW; wastewater plant 250 kW. These were existing emergency units, not a new central plant.

specifications.label: Proposed new firm generation
specifications.value: 5.2 MW natural gas: 1.6 MW CCHP at Mather, 1.6 MW CCHP at St. Charles, and a 2 MW gas reciprocating generator at St. Charles.

specifications.label: Proposed solar
specifications.value: 298 kW total: existing 50 kW at Mather and 48 kW at the Spear property, plus a proposed 200 kW array at the middle/high-school campus.

specifications.label: Proposed electrochemical storage
specifications.value: 550 kW/2.2 MWh total: 250 kW/1 MWh at the wastewater plant and 300 kW/1.2 MWh at the water authority, both four-hour concepts. No vendor, chemistry, or procurement was selected in Stage 1.

specifications.label: Thermal systems
specifications.value: Two proposed 313 kW absorption chillers at the hospitals. The report's tables cite Mather thermal storage at 469 kW/4.69 MWh, while narrative material references 1.16 MW of cooling storage; this internal scope/rating inconsistency was never resolved by a final design.

specifications.label: Stage 1 economics
specifications.value: Estimated capital cost US$19.405 million. With assumed US$7 million NY Prize and US$4.5 million PSEG-LI CHP support, the report modeled normal-operation NPV US$8.72 million, benefit-cost ratio 1.1, and IRR 9.8%; private financing was judged difficult without incentives. These are study assumptions, not realized costs/returns.

equipment.detail: Multiple hospital, water, and wastewater standby units totaling 3.2 MW; manufacturer/model and emissions-upgrade condition were not inventoried in the public Stage 1 report.
Two 1.6 MW hospital CCHP plants and one 2 MW reciprocating gas generator; technology class was defined but vendors/models were not selected.
Two four-hour systems totaling 550 kW/2.2 MWh; no chemistry or supplier was committed.
Four new isolation switches using existing PSEG-LI distribution topology to create the island boundary.

technicalDetails.detail: The proposed microgrid would incorporate a mix of existing and new combined heat and power, solar, and energy storage.
The proposed microgrid would incorporate a mix of existing and new combined heat and power, solar, and energy storage

DER-CAM was used for normal-operation economic dispatch and sizing, with separate outage/islanding engineering. Results depend on assumed tariffs, gas availability, incentives, load profiles, and equipment costs from the 2015–2016 study period.

The report proposed a microgrid energy-services company (MESCO) with long-term microgrid energy-service agreements among municipal, institutional, utility, and generation parties; none is documented as executed.

The concept assumed firm natural-gas service/reinforcement by November 2019 and retained diesel. Resilience benefits therefore depended on gas-system availability and fuel arrangements that were assumptions, not completed infrastructure.

Port Jefferson is absent from the official NY Prize Stage 2 award list. That, combined with the lack of later construction records, is strong evidence the Stage 1 concept did not progress under NY Prize, though it is not a formal cancellation notice.

Do not merge this village critical-load concept with National Grid's Port Jefferson power station, its repowering proposals, or any separate battery proposals at that generation site. GE's 'Town of Port Jefferson' wording is an alias/mislabel for the Village-sponsored study.

portsmouth-naval-base-microgrid-testing.summary: This project is at Portsmouth Naval Shipyard in Kittery, Maine—not Norfolk Naval Shipyard in Portsmouth, Virginia. Its documented architecture has two generations: an ESTCP-era microgrid that integrated an existing 10.4 MW natural-gas CHP plant, emergency diesel generation, GE controls/Fast Load Shed, and a 500 kW/580 kWh Saft battery; and a US$58 million 2020 energy-savings expansion that added a 7.5 MW CHP plant, expanded controls/distribution redundancy, and announced a larger battery configuration. Public sources conflict on whether the expanded battery is 1 MW/2 MWh or a 1.5 MW/2.5 MWh engineering scope, so only the original 500/580 installation is treated as unambiguously commissioned at exact rating.
portsmouth-naval-base-microgrid-testing.status: The original microgrid completed two live base-wide outage simulations and successfully maintained critical loads. Ameresco's current portfolio page continues to list shipyard CHP, emergency generation, microgrid controls, Fast Load Shed, and a 1 MW/2 MWh BESS. A February 2026 Town of Kittery climate-planning packet also describes the shipyard's natural-gas cogeneration, microgrid Fast Load Shed, and power-plant BESS as available capabilities. The broader expansion is therefore operational/currently referenced, although a public commissioning report reconciling all battery and generation nameplates has not been found.
portsmouth-naval-base-microgrid-testing.historicalLocation: Portsmouth Naval Shipyard, Kittery, ME, United States

specifications.label: Storage
specifications.value: 500 kW/580kWh Battery Energy Storage System (BESS)

specifications.label: Original CHP plant
specifications.value: 10.4 MW from two 5.2 MW Solar Taurus 60 natural-gas turbines, with two 70,000 lb/hour heat-recovery steam generators and two 70,000 lb/hour package boilers; some summaries round the plant to 10 MW.

specifications.label: Emergency diesel
specifications.value: Ameresco's detailed project sheet identifies 2 x 2 MW emergency diesel generators (4 MW total). The ESTCP page describes 2 x 1.5 MW, likely an earlier boundary/design figure; the discrepancy is preserved.

specifications.label: Original battery
specifications.value: 500 kW/580 kWh Saft lithium-ion, about 1.16 hours at rated power, providing immediate bridge/ramp support while CHP or emergency units respond.

specifications.label: Expansion CHP
specifications.value: The 2020 ESPC announced a new 7.5 MW CHP plant in addition to existing generation. Public announcements sometimes call the pre-existing site CHP fleet '14 MW,' which does not reconcile cleanly with the detailed 10.4 MW two-turbine plant and may use a broader site boundary.

specifications.label: Expanded battery conflict
specifications.value: The 2020 award and Ameresco's current page use 1 MW/2 MWh; Waldron describes 1.5 MW/2.5 MWh. These may be design versus final scope, but no public as-built acceptance document was found. They must not be summed with each other; whether the 500/580 unit was replaced or incorporated is also not public.

specifications.label: Energy mix
specifications.value: Normal supply is utility electricity plus natural-gas CHP and recovered heat/steam; diesel is emergency generation and the battery is storage, not a fuel source. No installed renewable generator or current annual MWh/fuel split is documented in the cited sources.

specifications.label: Expansion economics
specifications.value: US$58 million ESPC award, with approximately US$175 million in guaranteed/expected savings over a 22-year performance period. These are contract projections rather than independently audited realized savings to date.

equipment.detail: Two 5.2 MW units in the original 10.4 MW CHP plant, each paired with heat recovery.
500 kW/580 kWh lithium-ion system; exact Saft product model is not identified in the cited public project material.
Supervisory microgrid controller integrated with Ameresco's Fast Load Shed system for islanding, critical-load prioritization, generation dispatch, and reconnection.
Two 70,000 lb/hour heat-recovery steam generators plus two 70,000 lb/hour package boilers in the documented original plant.

technicalDetails.detail: Ameresco announced May 3rd, 2016 that its Federal Solutions Group successfully demonstrated GE’s microgrid control system at Portsmouth Naval Shipyard in Kittery, Maine.
The project shows how the DOD is increasingly looking at energy storage to reduce dependence on diesel generators.
500 kW / 125 kWh BESS
10MW Combined Heat and Power (CHP) plant

Two live tests simulated a base-wide utility outage. The controls shed nonessential loads and maintained mission-critical loads with on-site generation and storage, validating transition and island operation rather than merely a desktop study.

Automated load prioritization balances the island when grid supply is lost or generation changes, avoiding the need to keep all emergency diesel units preemptively running and enabling smoother CHP-supported islanding.
The battery supplies instantaneous transition/bridging power and frequency/ramp support while slower combustion units start or adjust; ESTCP also evaluated grid-connected ancillary-service value.

A 2026 Kittery municipal climate packet refers to the shipyard's cogeneration, Microgrid Fast Load Shed, and power-plant BESS as current resilience capabilities. It is corroborative public evidence, not a Navy performance test or detailed asset register.

All figures here concern Portsmouth Naval Shipyard in Kittery, Maine. The original ESTCP demonstration and 2020 ESPC expansion are related but distinct phases, and public battery ratings should not be added without an as-built single-line diagram.

princeton-university-microgrid.summary: Princeton's campus microgrid is a long-running district-energy and electric system anchored by a 15 MW GE LM1600 natural-gas CHP turbine, a 2.6-million-gallon chilled-water thermal store, boilers/chillers, and campus distribution. Its solar fleet expanded from 5.3 MW in the Hurricane Sandy era to 16.5 MW AC across ten arrays in 2022. Princeton is now layering geo-exchange, electric heat pumps, hot/cold thermal storage, the TIGER plant, and the West Plant onto—not yet publicly documented as fully replacing—the legacy CHP architecture.
princeton-university-microgrid.status: Operational and undergoing a multi-year energy transition. The microgrid famously islanded for roughly 1.5 days during Hurricane Sandy after an approximately 20-minute interruption/restart. Current university reporting says the 16.5 MW AC solar portfolio is connected to the microgrid, produced about 20,800 MWh in FY2025, and supplies about 19% of current campus electricity. TIGER and the West Plant are operating in parallel as of 2025. Public sources do not say that the 15 MW CHP has been retired, nor do they publish a current all-source MWh mix, newer islanding test, or battery BESS—thermal storage remains the major disclosed storage technology.
princeton-university-microgrid.historicalLocation: Princeton University, Princeton, NJ, United States

specifications.value: 15 MW

specifications.value: General Electric LM1600, natural-gas or diesel capable

specifications.label: Legacy CHP
specifications.value: 15 MW GE LM1600 natural-gas combustion turbine in combined heat and power service. Princeton's plant page says CHP can achieve roughly 80% overall fuel utilization compared with separate heat and power production.

specifications.label: Legacy chilled-water storage
specifications.value: 2.6 million gallons, charged when electricity/cooling costs are lower and discharged during peaks to reduce chiller and electric demand.

specifications.label: Solar fleet
specifications.value: 16.5 MW AC across ten arrays following the 2022 expansion, compared with 5.3 MW and 16,500 panels in 2014. Current university materials say it supplies about 19% of electricity use.

specifications.label: Measured solar output
specifications.value: University reporting shows 7,162 MWh in FY2021 and approximately 20,800 MWh in FY2025 after the expansion.

specifications.label: Historical campus electrical load
specifications.value: 2014 university reporting cited roughly 16 MW average, 10 MW at night, and 27 MW on a hot day. These are useful operating-context figures but are not current 2025/2026 peaks after campus expansion/electrification.

specifications.label: New thermal storage
specifications.value: TIGER includes separate hot- and chilled-water tanks. Engineering reporting describes approximately 2.25 million gallons each; a campus newspaper reports 2.2 million gallons each. University project pages do not publish definitive tank nameplates.

specifications.label: Energy mix gap
specifications.value: Solar is currently reported at about 19% of electricity; the remainder is a changing combination of CHP, grid purchases, and growing electrified geo-exchange/heat-pump loads. The older claim that CHP met 75–85% of demand is Sandy-era and should not be reused as today's mix.

equipment.detail: Dual-fuel aeroderivative gas turbine drives the 15 MW generator; exhaust heat produces campus steam.
Real-time model monitors electricity and fuel prices plus campus electric, steam, and chilled-water demand to guide operators; vendor and software release are not disclosed.
2.6 million gallons of chilled water shift chiller load to lower-cost hours; newer TIGER and CUB facilities add separate hot- and cold-water storage tanks.
Gas-turbine exhaust heats water to make steam for heat, hot water, sterilization, and steam-driven cooling; equipment model is not stated.
15 MW natural-gas turbine-generator with heat recovery serving the campus steam/district-energy plant.
2.6-million-gallon chilled-water tank; energy-equivalent MWh depends on operating temperature differential and is not published on the current plant page.
Ten arrays totaling 16.5 MW AC, connected to the campus microgrid; module/inverter models and DC nameplate were not found on the public project page.

Electric heat pumps/chillers, hot- and chilled-water thermal storage, two transformers, controls, and a natural-gas backup generator. University pages do not disclose the backup generator's MW or model.

Engineering reporting cites 583 bores approximately 850 feet deep and a buildout to four 1,750-ton heat-pump chillers (about 7,000 tons total), with 19,680 ton-hours of cooling storage; treat these as design/engineering figures rather than a university as-built asset register.

technicalDetails.detail: Gas cogeneration plant also provides heating and cooling for roughly 180 buildings.
The heart of the Princeton microgrid is the cogeneration plant (center), which was constructed in 1996.
Operators import or export power based on real-time economics and can disconnect the campus to run as an electrical island during utility outages.
After a roughly 20-minute interruption and turbine restart, the campus islanded at about 13 MW until reconnection shortly before midnight on October 31, 2012.
The gas turbine produces electricity while exhaust heat supplies steam; the district-energy plant also dispatches steam- and electric-driven chillers.
The upgraded microgrid is adding solar, geo-exchange, heat pumps, natural-gas backup generation, and paired hot/cold thermal-storage tanks at TIGER and CUB.

After the regional grid failed, the campus experienced about a 20-minute interruption while the plant restarted, then served about 13 MW in island mode for roughly 1.5 days before reconnecting before midnight October 31, 2012. This was a successful island operation, not seamless UPS service.

The legacy controller optimizes CHP, grid purchases, chillers, boilers, and chilled-water storage against hourly electricity/fuel costs and plant constraints; resilience/islanding is layered on a normally economic campus utility operation.

Princeton is converting building heating from steam to lower-temperature hot water and using electric heat pumps, geo-exchange bores, and large hot/cold stores. TIGER and the West Plant operating in parallel provide geographic and equipment redundancy while construction continues.
No current source cited here documents a campus electrochemical BESS. The 2.6-million-gallon legacy tank and new TIGER tanks store thermal energy and should not be reported as battery MW/MWh.

Princeton publishes solar output and decarbonization progress but not a current CHP capacity factor, total campus MWh by source, islanding test history after Sandy, outage reliability indices, or an exact retirement date for the LM1600.

pulau-ubin-microgrid.summary: Pulau Ubin's microgrid is a small Singapore island system serving more than 30 homes and businesses, originally launched with rooftop solar, battery storage, and recycled-cooking-oil biodiesel generation. Its modern EDPR phase installed 328 kWp solar and a 1 MWh experimental vanadium redox-flow battery in 2023. Crucially, Singapore's Ministry of Trade and Industry disclosed in March 2026 that the actual 2025 electricity mix was only about 10% solar and 90% diesel—not the up-to-90% solar ambition publicized for the enhancement.
pulau-ubin-microgrid.status: Operating but experiencing reliability and storage-performance problems. MTI reported approximately 300 MWh annual consumption and 13 supply trips in 2025. EDPR is upgrading the system, with an upgraded battery expected to be commissioned later in 2026; as of the research cutoff that upgrade was not yet complete. The system therefore remains diesel-dominant and the existing flow battery should be described as experimental, not proof of a near-renewable autonomous grid.

equipment.detail: Integrates and coordinates supply and demand and houses energy storage; EMA does not disclose the controller, battery, inverter, generator, or PV module models.
328 kWp installed in 2023, including a solar green-roof concept intended to lower module temperature; exact module/inverter makers and DC/AC ratio are not public.
1 MWh vanadium redox-flow battery with a smart-pump concept; vendor, model, power rating, electrolyte volume, and current usable capacity are not disclosed.

Current backup/firm generation supplies about 90% of annual energy. Original 2013 material described generators running on biodiesel recycled from cooking oil, but the 2026 government reply says diesel and does not verify continued biodiesel use or generator model/capacity.
Underground customer cables, a central control system, storage, and remote/automated management; current controller vendor and detailed protection architecture are not public.

technicalDetails.detail: Solar PV, Biodiesel,

The 2022 design expected vegetation to reduce solar-module temperatures by 3–8°C and improve efficiency by 1.5–4%. These are design expectations; measured site results were not published in the sources reviewed.

The vanadium system uses an actively controlled smart pump, projected to improve battery-system efficiency by up to 3%. MTI now describes the battery as experimental and says an upgrade is needed, so the projected gain should not be treated as demonstrated.

The 2013 original DLRE/OKH solar-biodiesel system and the 2023 EDPR Sunseap 328 kWp/1 MWh enhancement are successive phases. The 285 kWp figure was planned; 328 kWp was installed. The later 2026 battery upgrade is future as of the cutoff.

The most consequential update is the gap between the enhancement's up-to-90% solar aspiration and the government's measured 2025 mix of 10% solar. Reliability trips and the pending battery upgrade indicate the project remains an active demonstration/operational learning system.
EDPR was working to commission an upgraded energy-storage system later in 2026. No final capacity, chemistry change, commissioning date, or post-upgrade performance was available by July 20, 2026.

qinous-microgrid-demonstrator.summary: The Qinous demonstrator was a containerized Berlin Schöneweide mini-grid test rig built in 2013–2014, not a renewable-powered customer site. It used a 150 kVA diesel generator, a 100 kW/112 kWh lithium-ion storage system, an added Aquion aqueous/saltwater battery, two 150 kVA converter-based load/PV simulators, and a Bachmann M1 controller. The often-listed '100 kW solar' and '100 kW wind' are adjustable simulated profiles, not installed PV panels or wind turbines; a later project directory's 130 kW combined-storage figure does not reconcile directly with the primary 100 kW/112 kWh baseline plus added Aquion unit.
qinous-microgrid-demonstrator.status: The documented RLI control-development project ended December 31, 2014. Qinous later became a commercial storage/microgrid integrator; Rolls-Royce bought an initial stake in 2018, acquired a 73.1% majority in January 2020, and renamed the company Rolls-Royce Solutions Berlin GmbH in June 2020 as its Microgrid Competence Center. Rolls-Royce separately commissioned a newer Microgrid Validation Center in Friedrichshafen in 2019. No current primary source confirms that the original Schöneweide container test rig, its Samsung battery, or its Aquion battery remains in service in 2026, so the asset status is historical demonstration / present physical disposition unknown.
