specifications.label: Onshore wind
specifications.value: 11 turbines rated 1 MW each, 11 MW total. The original community transition involved mixed local ownership; a comprehensive current turbine-by-turbine ownership/model register was not located.

specifications.label: Offshore wind
specifications.value: 10 turbines rated 2.3 MW each, 23 MW total. The 2025 life-extension decision says the farm can supply annual electricity equivalent to roughly 20,000–25,000 households and may operate through 2037.

specifications.label: Total landmark wind portfolio
specifications.value: 34 MW (11 MW onshore plus 23 MW offshore), not the legacy 21 MW figure. This is installed wind capacity, not island peak demand or guaranteed islanded supply.

specifications.label: District heat
specifications.value: Four local networks use straw and wood-chip boilers, with solar thermal at one network, and historically supplied about 70% of island heating demand from renewable sources. Remaining buildings use individual systems including heat pumps, pellet boilers and solar.

specifications.label: Electric energy balance
specifications.value: The municipality says Samsø produces more electricity than residents and visitors consume. This is an annual/net statement: mainland interconnection balances time-varying production and demand, and no annual 2026 generation-by-source table was found.

specifications.label: Climate targets
specifications.value: 70% greenhouse-gas reduction by 2030 compared with 1990 and climate neutrality by 2050 under the municipal plan; the Academy also frames fossil-fuel phaseout by 2030 as the next transition objective.

equipment.detail: Reviewed community sources describe grid-connected generation and district-heating systems, not a single island-wide controller or EMS model.
Eleven 1 MW-class machines. Public overview sources do not provide one reliable current model list, repowering status or fleet availability figure.
Ten 2.3 MW turbines, 23 MW total. The regulator's life-extension decision governs continued production through 2037; no repowering was announced.

Four community systems centered on locally sourced straw/wood-chip heat, with solar thermal in the northern network; equipment model and thermal-storage inventories are not published in current overview material.
Household/building PV, solar thermal, heat pumps and pellet systems supplement the large shared assets. No utility-scale battery was identified.

technicalDetails.detail: Wind, PV, district heating

Samsø is connected to Denmark's power system and uses that grid to export surpluses and import during deficits. There is no evidence of black-start controls, an island-wide static switch, autonomous frequency control or critical-load islanding.

Local ownership and cooperatives were central: residents and municipal/community entities invested directly in turbines and district heat rather than relying on one utility microgrid developer. Historical reporting attributes about 70% of the roughly EUR 58 million investment to 3,700 residents.

The Energy Academy reports annual renewable overproduction sufficient to offset remaining fossil use and describes a net carbon balance around negative 3.5 tonnes per resident. It is an accounting claim, not evidence that transport, heating and electricity are physically fossil-free at every moment.

Island 2.0 focuses on transport, biogas, heat pumps, replacing aging turbines/oil heating and continuing citizen participation. Specific commissioned capacities and completion dates for those future measures remain project-dependent.

santa-fe-community-college-microgrid.summary: Santa Fe Community College (SFCC) has two nested, date-separated projects. A small educational greenhouse nanogrid was commissioned in 2021 with 12–12.5 kW of tracking PV and a 100 kW/85 kWh lithium battery. The full campus microgrid, launched June 2, 2026 after more than a decade of planning, integrates the college's existing 1.5 MW solar array, a 1 MW/770 kWh campus battery, a 1 MW natural-gas generator, controls and the earlier educational system. Older feasibility descriptions and the greenhouse battery's 170 kWh expansion ceiling should not be mistaken for final campus as-built ratings.
santa-fe-community-college-microgrid.status: The greenhouse educational microgrid was commissioned in July 2021. SFCC formally celebrated launch of the campus-wide microgrid on June 2, 2026 after receiving utility permission to operate. A 2026 procurement seeks Siemens microgrid-control support plus remote monitoring and operations/maintenance, consistent with a newly operational system entering its service phase. No post-launch islanding event, uptime, fuel use, battery throughput or realized savings report was available by July 20, 2026.

specifications.label: Campus battery

specifications.value: 1 MW natural-gas generator. Public sources do not identify the engine manufacturer/model, on-site fuel-storage arrangement or emissions/runtime limits.

specifications.label: Educational greenhouse nanogrid

specifications.label: Energy mix
specifications.value: Normally grid electricity plus campus solar, with battery shifting/support and natural gas for firm islanded operation. The launch material does not quantify current annual solar, gas or grid shares.

specifications.label: Duration language
specifications.value: Burns states that the PV, battery and gas generator can enable indefinite island operation. That is an engineering capability claim contingent on natural-gas availability and operating conditions, not a published endurance test.

equipment.detail: 4,620 fixed 327 W modules, approximately 1.5 MW total, plus a separate 36-panel/11.8–12.5 kW educational tracking array.
1 MW/770 kWh BESS; public project pages reviewed do not disclose manufacturer, model or chemistry.
100 kW/85 kWh lithium-ion BESS, designed with an expansion path to 170 kWh.

Campus controller, monitoring and related control support. Earlier planning materials name Siemens Spectrum Power MGMS, but current procurement does not establish that every 2026 control component retained the exact early-design product/version.
1 MW natural-gas generator; manufacturer and model not publicly identified.

technicalDetails.detail: The nanogrid, by optimizing energy generation, storage, and consumption, will therefore be an excellent complement.

The campus system is designed to disconnect from the utility and sustain college loads using PV, storage and the gas generator, then resynchronize. The smaller greenhouse system initially used manual islanding/resynchronization and functions as a hands-on teaching platform.

The greenhouse installation provided a real-world development/test bed while the broader campus design matured. It is now described as integrated with the campus system, but its 100 kW inverter rating should not be added to the 1 MW campus battery as though both were one homogeneous storage unit.

A New Mexico capital-outlay report states PNM permission to operate had been received before the June 2026 launch, distinguishing actual operation from the many earlier feasibility and funding announcements.

Because campus launch occurred only weeks before the research cutoff, there is no meaningful public operating history yet. Current status is commissioned/early operations, not proven long-duration resilience performance.

santa-rita-jail-microgrid.summary: Alameda County's Santa Rita Jail microgrid is a 12 kV campus resilience system around a roughly 3 MW secure load. Its layered portfolio includes solar PV (1.2 MW in the 2012 as-built record; FuelCell Energy reported 2 MW by 2020), five small wind turbines totaling 11.2 kW, a current 1.4 MW fuel-cell CHP plant, 2 MW/4 MWh lithium-ion storage and about 2.4 MW of existing diesel backup. The present 1.4 MW fuel cell replaced the original 1 MW unit, so both ratings must not be counted simultaneously.
santa-rita-jail-microgrid.status: The expanded CERTS microgrid was completed in March 2012. FuelCell Energy reported successful islanded operation during California's 2020 public-safety power shutoffs, and a 2024 Alameda County document still describes Santa Rita's system as an operating county microgrid. The current 1.4 MW fuel cell is under a 20-year PPA announced in 2015. No 2025–2026 asset-level report discloses battery state of health, renewable output, availability or recent outage tests, so current operation is supported but detailed performance is not.

specifications.value: Grid power in normal operation, PV, natural-gas fuel-cell CHP, battery and emergency diesel, with negligible wind contribution. A current annual generation split was not found.

Rooftop/carport PV plus five small wind turbines; detailed current bill of materials is unavailable.

technicalDetails.detail: Fuel Cell runs on natural gas, biogas, or hydrogen

The battery inverter acts as the island voltage/frequency reference using CERTS droop control, coordinated through a fast static switch and distributed generator controls for automatic separation/resynchronization.

FuelCell Energy says the microgrid operated through 2020 PSPS outages. The source provides no event-by-event load, duration or fuel-use dataset, so it is operational evidence rather than an audited reliability study.
DOE's project page calls the storage sodium-sulfur, while detailed LBNL, OSTI and S&C as-built records call it lithium-ion. The latter consensus is used here and the DOE discrepancy preserved.
S&C projected about US$100,000 in annual savings from peak management and grid services; no current audited realized-savings series was located.

santiago-island.summary: Santiago is Cabo Verde's largest isolated island power system, not one facility-level microgrid. Its documented renewable portfolio includes the 5 MWp Palmarejo Grande solar plant commissioned in 2010, Cabeólica's original 9.35 MW wind farm (11 Vestas V52 turbines), and a December 2025 expansion adding 13.5 MW of wind plus a 6 MW/6 MWh battery. Thermal generation and the island grid remain essential; no current island-wide renewable percentage or full generation mix was found.
santiago-island.status: The solar plant and original wind farm are operational. Cabeólica inaugurated the expanded Santiago wind farm and battery in December 2025, taking site wind capacity to approximately 22.85 MW (marketed as 23 MW). AfDB still labels the broader financing project ongoing with a 2027 completion horizon, apparently reflecting the multi-island/refinancing program rather than contradicting physical inauguration. No 2026 production or battery-throughput report was available.

The 28,086 MWh, 96.1% availability and 11% penetration metrics apply only to the original 9.35 MW wind farm before Phase II.

Physical Phase II inauguration occurred in December 2025, while development-bank reporting keeps the wider financing program open through 2027. Asset status and financing-program status should not be conflated.

sao-vicente-island.summary: São Vicente is an isolated Cabo Verde island grid, not a single controller-bounded microgrid. Its modern renewable portfolio includes Cabeólica's 5.95 MW wind farm, the 6 MWp/5 MWac Norte de Baía solar plant inaugurated in November 2025, and an 8 MW/8 MWh lithium-ion battery at Lazareto inaugurated in January 2026. These newer assets supersede the old database's 27 kW solar figure as a representation of island-scale PV, while thermal generation still supplies the balance.
sao-vicente-island.status: The original wind farm remains operational; the solar plant entered service November 22, 2025 and the Lazareto BESS January 29, 2026. The Government says the combined system raises renewable penetration to about 40%, but no post-commissioning annual generation or independent reliability report was available by July 2026. Current status is operational/early operations, with performance claims still largely projections or official launch metrics.

sapsi-island.summary: Sapsi Island is Sapsi-do in Boryeong, Chungcheongnam-do, South Korea. It was selected in 2015 for the private-sector Energy Independent Island (EII) program with Woojin Industrial Systems' consortium, but the best later academic assessment says the islands failed to secure power-purchase agreements and remained diesel dependent. The proposed Sapsi concept was a 1.6 MW solar-led hybrid targeting 83% renewables alongside an existing 900 kW internal-combustion plant; no credible evidence of construction or commissioning was found through July 2026.
sapsi-island.status: Selected/planned in July 2015, but not demonstrated as built. A 2022 peer-reviewed program review reports that only the Ulleung consortium reached a PPA and that the EII island projects, including Sapsi, achieved little and still relied on diesel. No later PPA, EPC award, interconnection, commissioning, operating data or formal cancellation notice was located. The defensible status is stalled/not implemented as last authoritatively assessed—not operational and not necessarily formally cancelled.

The five-island announcement was a developer selection, not a commissioning statement. Later evidence says the program delivered little and participating islands remained dependent on diesel.

sault-ste-marie.summary: The record combines three distinct ideas that should not be treated as one commissioned microgrid: Sault Ste. Marie's broad 2011 Smart Energy Strategy, a later proposed Utility Distribution Microgrid, and the narrower Sault Smart Grid (SSG) that PUC actually built. The 2011 strategy was not implemented as conceived, and PUC de-scoped the later microgrid business case to SSG. Launched in 2023, SSG is an operational, community-wide distribution-automation and voltage-optimization system serving roughly 33,600 customers; it is not an islandable microgrid and includes no project-owned generation or storage. Its value is measurable grid efficiency, outage mitigation, and a platform for future DER integration. In 2025 it saved an estimated 14.0 GWh, reduced peak demand by 2.732 MW, and prevented 906,201 customer-minutes of interruption, although its annual net benefit was still negative after operating costs. NRCan continued to label its funding file Active, but PUC's later reports establish that the physical system is fully operational.
sault-ste-marie.status: Operational since 2023 as the Sault Smart Grid, a citywide distribution-modernization project rather than an islandable microgrid. The earlier Smart Energy Strategy and Utility Distribution Microgrid concepts were not built in their original forms.
sault-ste-marie.historicalLocation: Sault Ste Marie Innovation, Queen Street East, Sault Ste. Marie, ON, Canada

Fiber and cellular communications link field devices and substations to the control platform

technicalDetails.detail: SSG changes voltage setpoints, detects and isolates faults, restores healthy feeder sections, and consolidates outage and asset data. It cannot intentionally island the city and has no dedicated generation or storage, so describing it as a commissioned microgrid overstates the delivered scope.

The 2011 Smart Energy Strategy lacked a durable implementation/accountability structure. A 2016 Navigant business-case review then examined a Utility Distribution Microgrid, but subsequent PUC reviews de-scoped that proposal into the more economical SSG distribution-modernization program.

The control and communications platform is intended to support future distributed-energy-resource adoption. That enabling capability should not be confused with DER assets already owned or installed by this project.

The 2025 annual report shows C$1.708 million of purchased-power savings but an annual net benefit of negative C$278,015 after operating costs; cumulative net benefit remained slightly positive at C$70,770. The result shows technical savings do not automatically translate to a positive result in every year.

Pandemic disruption, supply-chain constraints, regulatory timing, and scope adjustments delayed delivery. The March 2023 public report described the project as roughly 85% complete at its reporting cutoff; later owner material confirms full operation.

sendai-microgrid.summary: The Sendai Microgrid was NEDO's 2004–2008 Microgrid Power Quality Supply System demonstration at Tohoku Fukushi University, designed and built by NTT Facilities. It is often misidentified as a Tohoku University project. The system combined two 350 kW city-gas engines, 50 kWp of PV, a 200 kW molten-carbonate fuel cell in the original configuration, and a fast power-quality supply backed by a 600 Ah VRLA battery. NTT Facilities continued managing it after the NEDO demonstration. During the March 11, 2011 earthquake, the external grid failure and voltage collapse initially stopped the engines; the battery/PV-backed highest-quality circuits continued until protective shutdown early the next morning. Operators then improvised provisional wiring, restarted the gas engines, and restored selected hospital and care-facility loads before the utility grid returned on March 14. This was a major resilience success, but not uninterrupted service to every load. Fuji Electric donated a 100 kW PAFC after the disaster, explaining why later descriptions differ from the original fuel-cell inventory. No authoritative asset-specific operating update after the early 2010s was located, so current operation and equipment condition are unverified.
sendai-microgrid.status: Historically operational and proven during the 2011 disaster; NTT Facilities continued operation after the 2008 demonstration, but present-day operating status and retained equipment could not be verified from an authoritative post-2013 source.
sendai-microgrid.historicalLocation: Tohoku University, Sendai, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan

technicalDetails.detail: The Sendei Microgrid has different categories of loads based on based on the quality of power they require.
It can supply various classes of power quality within the microgrid, seen below.
The Sendai Microgrid has several generation sources: two gas engines, a phosphoric acid fuel cell (PAFC)1 and a photovoltaic array.
The host was Tohoku Fukushi University in Sendai. Tohoku University is a different institution and should not be attached to this asset record.

On March 11, 2011, loss of the Tohoku grid and a voltage collapse stopped the gas engines and ordinary B3/C supply. The IPS battery plus PV supported DC/A/B1 loads until protective shutdown at about 02:06 on March 12. Operators installed provisional cabling and restarted the engines around noon, restoring selected A/B1/C loads; ventilators on B3 were bypass-fed that afternoon. Grid service returned at 08:16 on March 14.
Sendai's medium-pressure city-gas network remained available because it was supplied by both LNG and a pipeline from Niigata. The distribution route mattered as much as the nominal generator fuel.

The event demonstrated prolonged local supply to life-safety functions, but it also exposed control-power and restart dependencies. Claims that the whole campus ran without interruption omit the overnight battery depletion and manual reconfiguration.

The latest authoritative asset-specific material located describes the early post-disaster period. It does not establish that the original engines, batteries, fuel cells, or multi-quality circuits remain in service in 2026.

sino-danish-microgrid-collaboration-the-.summary: This entry refers to a research platform at Plant B of HT-Shanghai Solar Energy Science & Technology's photovoltaic manufacturing facility, not a generic Shenzhou commercial building. The hardware was installed from September to December 2013 under the Danish RED/HyMG collaboration and then used in the Sino-Danish MeTER_Demo research project from April 2014 through March 2017. A later government case study reports 206 kW total capacity: 136 kW rooftop PV, 20 kW of wind, and a 50 kW energy-storage system, with EMS and advanced metering/communications. An earlier AAU presentation labels the PV array 130 kVA; this likely reflects a design or reporting-basis difference and should be preserved rather than silently normalized. The platform investigated hierarchical control, distributed generation, interoperability, and component/system testing, and it produced a documented research program. MeTER_Demo is formally finished. The case study said the installation was operating and selling electricity to the grid during the project, but no authoritative post-2017 operating update was found, so current asset condition is unknown.
sino-danish-microgrid-collaboration-the-.status: Research and demonstration program completed in March 2017. The installation operated and exported electricity during the project, but continued operation after project close has not been verified.

technicalDetails.detail: HyMG was the 2013 RED predecessor under which the physical platform was installed. MeTER_Demo was the 2014–2017 Sino-Danish research phase. Treating the two names as unrelated assets or using only one date loses the installation-versus-testing chronology.
The program studied distributed generation, hierarchical and multilevel control, advanced metering, standardized communications, interoperability, and both component-level and full-system testing.

The case study described the microgrid as operational during the project and generating revenue by selling electricity to the utility. It did not publish annual generation, export volume, availability, or post-project revenue.
The documented host was Plant B of an operating Shanghai Solar/HT-Shanghai Solar manufacturing facility. The label 'Shenzhou Commercial Building' is not supported by the primary project record.

Aalborg University marks MeTER_Demo finished and lists its research outputs, but no source located confirms whether the hardware was retained, repowered, dismantled, or still interconnected after 2017.

solar-desalination-microgrid.summary: This Baja California Sur record is best described as Yan (Jan) Kunczynski's evolving photovoltaic seawater reverse-osmosis research and commercialization lineage, associated with Sisyan and the La Paz area, rather than a conventional electricity microgrid. The published prototype scale is 1,000–5,000 US gallons per day (about 3.8–18.9 m³/day) of water. No reliable source supports the existing database's 100 kW power rating; it appears to confuse water-production or portfolio figures with electrical capacity. Contemporary reporting describes a progression from battery smoothing to direct solar-only operation, using multiple RO modules that could be dispatched as irradiance changed. Later Baja California Sur research describes a related multimodule PV-RO system with battery-state-of-charge dispatch, showing that configurations differed or continued to evolve. Regional systems attributed to Kunczynski reportedly reached up to 19 m³/day, product TDS below 250 mg/L, specific energy as low as 2.6 kWh/m³, and more than 70,000 solar-powered operating hours, but those portfolio metrics cannot all be assigned to one La Paz unit. Sisyan remains commercially active, while the exact original demonstrator's current location, equipment, and operating status are unverified.
solar-desalination-microgrid.status: Historic research/prototype lineage with later regional deployments and an active commercial successor. The exact original La Paz installation's present operation is unverified; it should not be labeled a confirmed 100 kW operational microgrid.

Multiple relatively small seawater RO modules could be started or stopped to follow available solar power

Sisyan's commercial architecture uses variable-frequency drives accepting DC photovoltaic input and avoids a conventional PV inverter stage; this is a company technology description and not proof of every original prototype component
Sisyan describes recovering pressure from the RO brine and applying it mechanically to the high-pressure pump shaft
Configuration varied by phase/site: the early system reportedly began with batteries and later ran solar-only, while a 2019 related system used a battery bank and state-of-charge-based module control

technicalDetails.detail: Microgrid Energy Managements system monitors battery voltage and adjusts variable frequency drives (VFDs) and modules to match available solar generation.
According to Sisyan LLC , storing desalinated water using available solar energy is energy storage.
Solar Photovoltaics (PV)

The plant couples PV directly to a flexible desalination process and uses product-water storage and modular load control. It is a renewable desalination plant or nanogrid-scale process system, not evidence of a community electricity microgrid with islanded distribution.

Instead of forcing one large RO train to run continuously, the system dispatches smaller RO modules as irradiance and available electrical power change. Water production becomes the controllable load.

The architecture can shift storage from electricity to elevated or tanked fresh water, reducing battery dependence. Located sources also show that some implementations retained batteries, so 'batteryless' should be tied to a specific phase rather than generalized.

Published results mix the original La Paz experiment, later remote-village plants, and Sisyan's commercial design. Ratings and performance are therefore labeled by source scope rather than combined into one synthetic installation.

Sisyan's current website confirms an active technology vendor, not that the original early-2000s plant remains installed or operating. No recent site-specific production or maintenance record was located.

solar-desalination-microgrid-2.summary: Elemental Water Makers commissioned this solar-driven seawater reverse-osmosis plant on Great Camanoe in the British Virgin Islands in July 2015 for a private retreat. Unlike the other solar-desalination record, this is a distinct, site-specific installation. A 12 kWp PV array supports roughly 12,500 L/day (about 3,300 US gal/day) of potable-water production. The system avoids electrochemical batteries by pumping seawater to an elevated buffer tank when solar power is available; gravity then maintains feed pressure, while a mechanical pressure intensifier recovers pressure from the brine to achieve the roughly 50 bar required by RO. The owner describes remote monitoring and a hurricane-rated solar structure. A current vendor case-study page continues to showcase the plant, but its embedded numeric widgets render as zeros and should not be treated as operating data. No recent site-specific availability, production, water-quality, or maintenance report was found, so operation after the dated case material remains unverified.
solar-desalination-microgrid-2.status: Commissioned and operating in July 2015; retained as an Elemental Water Makers case study, but current operation and cumulative production have not been independently or recently verified.

technicalDetails.detail: The Elemental Water Makers desalination microgrid pumps water up a hill during solar hours with 100% solar PV energy and stores the potential energy in a tank.
Solar PV
PV power is used when available to lift seawater. Gravity and recovered brine pressure then perform much of the RO pressurization, substituting hydraulic and water storage for a battery bank.

This is a standalone renewable water-production system rather than a general-purpose electric microgrid serving a community load. Its resilience metric is potable-water availability, not electrical islanding capacity.
The vendor describes energy recovery and mixing/dilution strategies that reduce discharge salinity, but site-specific discharge monitoring results were not published.

The current project webpage's live-performance fields display zero values, apparently because an embedded widget is inactive. The independently stated 12,500 L/day design capacity is the defensible figure; zeros are not evidence that the plant has shut down.
The commissioning and owner case study establish that the plant existed and operated, but no dated post-2021 site report confirms membrane replacements, hurricane damage, uptime, or current output.

southampton-microgrid-feasibility-study.summary: Southampton's NY Prize work ended as a Stage 1 feasibility study; the proposed community microgrid was not built. The August 2016 study modeled a Village of Southampton system serving Southampton Hospital and other municipal, school, water, business, and residential loads. The concept combined existing emergency generators and small PV arrays with a new 1.5 MW hospital CCHP plant, a 2 MW natural-gas reciprocating engine at the Department of Public Works, 300 kW of new PV, and a 200 kW/800 kWh battery. It assumed a third-party microgrid energy-services company would finance and operate new DER while PSEG Long Island retained the distribution system. The base economic case projected a 1.1 benefit-cost ratio; a much stronger case depended on crediting an avoided transmission upgrade. The capital plan also assumed a hypothetical $7 million of NYSERDA support. Southampton was not among the 11 projects selected for NY Prize Stage 2 in 2017, and no evidence of procurement, permitting, financing, or construction was found through July 2026. The town's current webpage merely hosts the old report and is not evidence of an active project.
southampton-microgrid-feasibility-study.status: Feasibility-only; Stage 1 report completed in 2016, not selected for NY Prize Stage 2, and no build was identified.

technicalDetails.detail: The study proposed a third-party microgrid energy-services company to finance, own, and operate new DER. PSEG Long Island would continue owning and operating distribution infrastructure, requiring contractual and operational coordination.
DER-CAM and engineering/economic studies sized and dispatched a conceptual portfolio. Modeled results are not as-built performance, equipment orders, or interconnection approvals.
The strongest benefit case depended on avoiding a transmission investment and on substantial grant funding. Removing the avoided-upgrade credit reduced the benefit-cost ratio to only 1.1.
NYSERDA selected 11 Stage 2 projects in March 2017. The Long Island selections included Huntington, Rockville Centre, and Freeport, but not Southampton.

The town and developer still make the feasibility report available, but no primary evidence was found for later awards, contracts, construction, commissioning, or operations. It should remain a feasibility-study record.

st-helena-island-microgrid.summary: St Helena's island power system remains an operational diesel-wind-solar hybrid operated by Connect Saint Helena, but recent evidence is a deterioration story rather than a completed renewable transition. The installed renewable fleet historically comprised twelve 80 kW WES wind turbines (960 kW) and five named solar installations totaling about 678 kW nominal. The principal active diesel fleet is four Caterpillar sets totaling 5.6 MW, with two older 1 MW RK215 sets also listed historically. A 2020 PASH contract announced 568 kWp of solar, 2.7 MW of wind, and a 3.2 MWh/3.5 MW battery, but COVID-era cost escalation and supply constraints led to termination in November 2021; talks with a reserve bidder also failed in 2022, and nothing was installed. Audit St Helena reports renewable share falling from about 21% in FY2021/22 to 19% in FY2022/23 and 13% in FY2023/24; the regulator reported only 11% in FY2024/25. Wind output fell sharply as the 12 aging turbines—now roughly 12–26 years old—became less available. The island remains diesel-dominant and has suffered generator failures, load shedding, subsidy pressure, and a nine-hour outage in January 2023. Government's revised target is 80% renewable electricity by FY2027/28, but the 2026 audit found planning and delivery weak and did not identify a commissioned replacement project.
st-helena-island-microgrid.status: Operational island grid, currently diesel-dominant with aging wind assets and declining renewable share. The 2020 PASH renewable-plus-storage expansion was cancelled in 2021 and never installed; an 80% renewable target for FY2027/28 remains a plan.

technicalDetails.detail: Wind turbines: WES

Connect signed the renewable PPA in May 2020. COVID-related inflation, material costs, and long lead times undermined the agreed economics, and the agreement was terminated November 15, 2021. Talks with the reserve bidder from February to July 2022 failed because its 2018 offer was no longer viable; procurement then ended.

Generator failures and inadequate available reserve contributed to early-2023 load shedding; a generator failure on January 2, 2023 caused a roughly nine-hour outage. The evidence shows an island grid under asset-health pressure, not simply a renewable percentage shortfall.

By the 2026 audit, the wind fleet was roughly 12–26 years old and fewer of the twelve machines were operable. Diesel units were also about 12 years old. Published nameplate capacity therefore overstates dependable capacity.
Diesel dependence exposed the utility and government to fuel-price pressure. Government support and diesel-related rebates totaled about £2.7 million in FY2023/24, according to the audit.

The current 80% target replaced the unrealized 100% ambition. Audit St Helena found that planning had advanced more than implementation and that no completed replacement build closed the capacity gap by early 2026.

The island is a real hybrid microgrid because diesel, wind, and solar are dispatched within one isolated network. It is not a high-renewables microgrid at present: FY2024/25 renewable contribution was reported at only 11%.

stone-edge-farm-winery-microgrid.summary: Stone Edge Farm's customer-owned Sonoma microgrid is an operational, long-running demonstration and working farm/winery system that has evolved repeatedly since 2013. Current owner material describes a 16-acre property with 25 buildings, seven service entrances, a 480 V three-phase bidirectional loop, a lower-voltage Critical Grid, distributed solar, several battery chemistries, a 65 kW gas microturbine with heat recovery, 28 kW of hydrogen fuel cells, electrolysis and hydrogen storage, pumps, and HVAC under decentralized Heila controls. The detailed owner tour's configuration snapshot lists 160 kW of PV and more than 600 kW of total capability, while a 2018 California Energy Commission case study reports a broader 1.185 MW cumulative installation including 368 kW PV, 324 kW of battery power, and a 400 kW electrolyzer. These are different dates and accounting boundaries, not necessarily an error; individual assets have been added, retired, or repurposed. During the October 2017 wildfire the site islanded for ten days, and by 2018 the owner reported independent normal operation. The current winery site still describes the microgrid as active and evolving. However, no recent audited energy mix, uptime, state-of-health, export, or carbon balance was found, and portions of the technical tour still use future tense. It is solar-led but not 100% renewable because natural gas generation and grid exchange remain part of the architecture.
stone-edge-farm-winery-microgrid.status: Operational and still promoted by the owner as an evolving microgrid. Exact 2026 component inventory and state of health are not fully verified because available technical sources represent different build phases.
stone-edge-farm-winery-microgrid.historicalLocation: Stone Edge Farm, Carriger Road, Sonoma, CA, United States

technicalDetails.detail: During daylight hours, solar PV provides energy for the buildings and charges the batteries.
This project is the first commercial deployment of the two companies’ technologies alongside one another for an energy storage application.

Heila IQ nodes sample local assets at up to 10 Hz and coordinate through supply-and-demand bids that can weight response time, state of charge, carbon impact, and cost. The architecture covers portions of six solar arrays, eleven battery/inverter combinations, four pumps, fuel cells, the microturbine, electrolyzer, and HVAC.

Automatic transfer equipment opens the utility point of common coupling, and grid-forming storage establishes local voltage/frequency. The lower-voltage Critical Grid/UPS tier preserves the most sensitive loads through transitions.

Surplus electricity can produce compressed hydrogen for later use in PEM fuel cells, while the microturbine is described as hydrogen-capable. This is long-duration/fuel storage experimentation; no recent round-trip-efficiency or annual hydrogen-throughput dataset was located.

Microturbine exhaust heat charges hot-water storage and drives absorption cooling, so simple electrical-efficiency comparisons understate the intended CHP value when vineyard/winery thermal loads can use it.

The 2018 CEC report aggregates progressive installations from July 2013 through June 2018 and reports 1.185 MW. The owner's current technical tour uses a smaller PV snapshot and notes some devices as 'being installed.' Asset ratings must retain their date/scope instead of being summed into a false current total.

Solar and stored solar are central, but the farm also imports/exports through PG&E and operates a natural-gas microturbine. Owner statements that the farm's carbon footprint is below zero were not accompanied by a recent independently audited energy or emissions balance.

The winery currently presents the microgrid as active and evolving, and the technical-tour site carries a 2026 copyright. That establishes continuing program presence, but not the in-service state or remaining capacity of every battery chemistry and experimental hydrogen component.

sumba-smart-microgrid.summary: The archived entry collapses two materially different Bila Cenge systems. Indonesia's BPPT first commissioned a 500 kWp thin-film PV smart-grid demonstration in 2012 with two 240 kWh vanadium-redox-flow batteries, two 135 kVA smart generators, and supervisory control on PLN's 20 kV Southwest Sumba network. After the flow battery failed and PV output degraded, an Indonesia-Japan revitalization replaced or reconfigured the plant around 2017 as a 400 kWp mixed-PV, 1,152 kWh lead-acid, 300 kW inverter system designed to inject roughly 200 kW steadily for several hours. The supportable current asset description is the 400 kWp revitalized phase, while the legacy 500 kW value belongs to the earlier configuration.
sumba-smart-microgrid.status: The original 2012 system demonstrably suffered major degradation: a BPPT-authored assessment reported the vanadium-redox battery out of service by about 2016, array degradation of roughly 20-30%, and output capped at 150 kW. A 2020 BPPT paper reports the revitalized 400 kWp/1,152 kWh system operating with energy management and later supporting a controlled EV-charging demonstration. A 2024 BRIN journal article still describes that PV-grid-battery installation, but is a techno-economic study rather than a live availability report. No public post-2024 production, outage, battery-health, or PLN dispatch series was located, so installation is well supported but continuous operation in July 2026 is not independently verified.

specifications.label: Controlled power delivery
specifications.value: The revitalized system was designed to hold approximately 200 kW grid injection for about 7-8 hours in the dry season and about 6 hours in the rainy season. These are reported operating/design durations, not a guaranteed current dispatch profile.

specifications.value: The original plant was tied to PLN's 20 kV network. BPPT reported daytime base load around 1.5 MW and an initial solar contribution approaching 30%; these are historical local-system values.

specifications.label: 2024 economics result
specifications.value: A BRIN techno-economic analysis calculated a 34-year simple payback and benefit-cost ratio of 0.60 under its assumptions, concluding the installation was not financially attractive on that basis. This is a modeled economic result, not an operating-loss statement.

equipment.detail: Developed and commissioned through the BPPT–Kyudenko cooperation; commercial hardware and software model numbers were not stated.
Thin-film amorphous/microcrystalline-silicon modules totaling 500 kWp. Accessible sources do not establish a reliable module maker or model.
Two vanadium-redox-flow batteries of 240 kWh each. Public evidence establishes chemistry, count, and nominal energy but not a trustworthy manufacturer/model; the units were later reported damaged.
Mixed polycrystalline/thin-film array, sixteen 25 kW PV converters, and six 50 kW bidirectional DC/AC inverters. Maker and model numbers were not disclosed in the accessible proceedings.
Two blocks of lead-acid batteries totaling 1,152 kWh nominal. No accessible source identifies the cell manufacturer, model, usable depth of discharge, or replacement history.
Energy-management system, battery control unit, smart-meter control, GSM/SCADA communications, and power-management logic for constant grid output and EV-charging coordination.

technicalDetails.detail: The 500 kWp/480 kWh flow-battery plant and the 400 kWp/1,152 kWh lead-acid plant are successive configurations, not additive assets. A total of 900 kWp and 1,632 kWh would therefore be misleading.
BPPT reported the flow battery unavailable, array performance down roughly 20-30%, and PV output limited to 30% of nameplate (150 kW) to avoid destabilizing the grid after storage failure.

The revitalized plant buffers variable PV through the battery and EMS so the feeder receives a flatter power block. Smart meters and communications provide the controller with grid and plant state rather than treating the array as an uncontrolled generator.

BPPT later linked an EV charging point at PLN Tambolaka to Bila Cenge by point-to-point/radio communications; the EMS temporarily raised plant injection during charging without a reported interruption. This was a demonstration, not proof of a broad public charging network.

No current public dashboard or audited report was found for annual PV yield, curtailment, battery availability, PLN purchases, outages, or operating emissions after 2024. The current status cannot be upgraded from installed/reported to continuously verified operational.

sunderbans.summary: The legacy 120 kW 'Sunderbans' record combines at least two independent West Bengal projects and should not be treated as one Tata Power Solar plant. One was WBREDA's 110 kWp Indrapur/G-Plot mini-grid, implemented by Tata BP Solar and commissioned in 2004. The other was WWF-India/Bushlight's approximately 9.65 kWp Rajatjubili-Annpur system on Satjelia, commissioned around 2011 with a 10 kW hybrid power conditioner and demand-managed distribution. Later WWF work added several separate microgrids elsewhere on Satjelia and Kumirmari. These capacities, operators, communities, and operating histories must remain distinct.
sunderbans.status: The Indrapur 110 kWp system had serious battery problems by 2015 and was reported defunct by 2022 after mainland-grid electricity reached the village in 2018. Rajatjubili had a more resilient community-management design, and one of two systems examined in 2022 was still operating while the other had failed after grid arrival; the reporting does not support assigning that outcome confidently to every legacy asset. WWF's broader Satjelia portfolio was restored quickly after Cyclone Amphan in 2020, but no post-2022 plant-by-plant operating report was found. WBSEDCL reports continuing mainland-grid electrification in Gosaba and Patharpratima through March 2026. The grouped record is therefore historical/mixed status, not a currently verified 120 kW operating microgrid.

specifications.label: Indrapur customers and distribution
specifications.value: About 2 km of local lines serving roughly 120 households and 180 market shops. Early operation delivered an average of about 27 kW for five evening hours; after Cyclone Aila in 2009, service was reduced to about three hours.

specifications.label: Rajatjubili photovoltaic array
specifications.value: 9.65 kWp, often rounded to 10 kW. This is a distinct project and should not be arithmetically combined with Indrapur as though both formed a single 120 kW plant.

specifications.label: Rajatjubili storage and controller
specifications.value: Sixty 2 V flooded-cell batteries, model/type 1200H at C120, controlled through a 10 kW Hybrid Power Conditioner. The case study does not expose enough series/parallel detail to state a reliable nominal kWh total.

specifications.label: Rajatjubili service
specifications.value: Designed for a maximum of about 23 kWh/day and 230 V AC service to 48 houses, seven commercial users, three community buildings, four street lights, and four separately monitored outgoing distribution lines.

specifications.label: Later WWF portfolio
specifications.value: WWF's 2019-20 report gives 84.12 kW combined across six DC grids, one AC grid, and 80 solar street lights on Satjelia and Kumirmari, serving about 700 households/3,000 people. This is a portfolio total, not the rating of Rajatjubili alone.

equipment.detail: Custom 110 kWp PV plant with three 30 kVA inverters and a 240 V/800 Ah tubular lead-acid battery bank. Public field reporting does not identify exact module, inverter, or battery models.

equipment.manufacturer: Optimal Power Synergy India Pvt. Ltd.
equipment.detail: 10 kW power-conditioning and PLC-based control package coordinating the PV array, battery, feeder monitoring, and staged load shedding.
Sixty 2 V flooded lead-acid cells. The accessible case study names the type/rating but not a manufacturer.
Programmable household energy-budget and demand-management devices used with a participatory energy-planning process; precise manufacturer attribution is not stated in the case study.

technicalDetails.detail: Tata Power Solar has solar microgrid projects in Patharpratima, Indrapur (110 kW); and Rajatjubili Village, Gosaba (10 kW).

The archived page's 120 kW total appears to add 110 kWp at Indrapur to a rounded 10 kW at Rajatjubili, despite different islands/communities, sponsors, equipment, and commissioning dates. The two projects should be represented as related regional cases, not a single system.

At the February-March 2015 field visit, PV modules were still producing but battery problems had left the system non-operational. Reporting in 2022 described the plant as defunct after grid electricity arrived in 2018.

The system allocated daily energy budgets, monitored four feeders, and shed lower-priority circuits in stages as battery state required. The architecture aimed to provide managed 24/7 availability without allowing a few users to exhaust the shared battery.

After Cyclone Amphan in May 2020, WWF reported its six approximately 10 kW Satjelia microgrids restored within days while conventional service remained damaged. This is portfolio-level resilience evidence, not proof that every earlier Sundarbans asset survived.

Mainland-grid expansion changed customer willingness to pay and maintenance incentives. The 2022 investigation found one community-managed solar system still useful and another abandoned, while WBSEDCL's current program confirms extensive rural-grid energization across the relevant blocks.

No public 2025/26 battery inventory, energy delivered, tariff collection, outage log, or site-by-site status for Indrapur, Rajatjubili, and the later WWF portfolio was located. A single 'operational' flag would overstate the evidence.

tau-solarcity-tesla-microgrid-american-s.summary: Ta'u's 2016 project is a 1.4 MW solar photovoltaic array paired with 6 MWh of Tesla Powerpack storage on the American Samoa Power Authority's isolated Manu'a grid. SolarCity delivered the solar/EPC scope before its acquisition by Tesla, Tesla supplied 60 Powerpacks, and ASPA owns and operates the utility system. Federal and territorial sources continue to describe the solar-battery plant as supplying all or essentially all of Ta'u's electricity and displacing more than 100,000 gallons of diesel per year, but newer ASPA records also establish that the site remains a solar-diesel hybrid and added three 500 kW Cummins generator sets. '100% solar' should therefore describe the reported normal annual-energy outcome, not the absence of installed diesel backup.
tau-solarcity-tesla-microgrid-american-s.status: The solar-battery system was commissioned in November 2016, and NREL's 2024 baseline plus EIA's current territory profile continue to cite it as supplying 100% of Ta'u electricity. ASPA's fiscal-2024 audit reports three new 500 kW Cummins generators installed and commissioned at the Falesa'o Ta'u Power Plant, while its fiscal-2025 audit calls the leased-site installation a solar-and-diesel hybrid. This does not disprove high renewable-energy operation, but it materially qualifies the original marketing. No public Ta'u-specific 2025/26 hourly generation, fuel burn, battery capacity test, availability, or curtailment dataset was located.

organizations.name: American Samoa Economic Development Authority
organizations.role: Project funding participant identified in commissioning coverage.

organizations.name: American Samoa Power Authority (ASPA)
organizations.role: Territorial utility, project owner/operator, and operator of the Ta'u hybrid power plant and distribution system; later installed three 500 kW diesel generator sets.

organizations.name: Cummins
organizations.role: Manufacturer of the three 500 kW diesel generator sets ASPA reports commissioning in fiscal 2024.

organizations.name: SolarCity
organizations.role: Publicly named participant; role not specified in the recovered record

organizations.name: SolarCity Corporation
organizations.role: Solar developer/EPC that built the array and integrated the project before/around its 2016 acquisition by Tesla.

organizations.name: Tesla
organizations.role: Publicly named participant; role not specified in the recovered record

organizations.name: Tesla, Inc.
organizations.role: Supplier/integrator of 60 Powerpack battery units and associated storage controls; later published performance and avoided-fuel claims.

organizations.name: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency / U.S. Department of the Interior
organizations.role: Federal funding participants identified for the solar-storage project.

specifications.label: Storage
specifications.value: 6 MWH

specifications.label: Solar photovoltaic capacity
specifications.value: 1.4 MW from 5,328 modules. Public sources do not provide a dependable module manufacturer/model, DC/AC ratio, degradation result, or current inverter-limited AC rating.

specifications.label: Battery storage
specifications.value: 6 MWh from 60 Tesla Powerpacks, implying 100 kWh nominal per unit in the commissioned configuration. No public 2026 capacity-retention or usable-energy test was found.

specifications.label: Autonomy and recharge claim
specifications.value: Commissioning material said the battery could sustain the island for about three days without sunlight and recharge fully in roughly seven hours. These are vendor/project claims, not a current guaranteed autonomy rating.

specifications.label: Diesel backup
specifications.value: Three Cummins generator sets rated 500 kW each (1.5 MW aggregate) were installed and commissioned at Falesa'o in fiscal 2024. ASPA's audit does not disclose the exact set model, fuel-consumption curve, or runtime.

specifications.label: Avoided diesel
specifications.value: Tesla, NREL, and EIA use approximately 100,000 to 109,500 gallons per year as the displacement figure. It is a recurring project/agency estimate; no current island fuel ledger was located to audit it.

specifications.label: Renewable-energy share
specifications.value: NREL and EIA describe the system as supplying 100% of Ta'u electricity. Because ASPA simultaneously maintains diesel capacity and describes a hybrid system, this should be read as normal or annual-energy supply rather than a claim that diesel equipment is absent or never runs.

specifications.label: Host community
specifications.value: Contemporary accounts variously cite roughly 600 residents and about 900 people served. The sources use different population/service denominators, so neither value should be treated as an exact current customer count.

equipment.detail: Sixty units totaling 6 MWh, plus Tesla power-conversion/control equipment. Public commissioning sources do not identify a more precise Powerpack hardware revision.
5,328 solar modules totaling 1.4 MW, delivered by SolarCity. Accessible sources do not support a panel or inverter manufacturer/model attribution.
Three 500 kW units commissioned by ASPA in fiscal 2024. Model, voltage, and emissions tier were not specified in the audit.
Solar-storage dispatch and island-grid controls integrate the PV array, Powerpacks, ASPA distribution, and diesel backup. No supportable public controller model or current software version was found.

technicalDetails.detail: 6 megawatt hours of battery storage from 60 Tesla Powerpacks
1.4 megawatts of solar generation capacity from SolarCity and Tesla

PV serves load and charges Powerpacks during solar hours; storage shifts energy into evening and cloudy periods and stabilizes the isolated grid. Diesel generators provide firm backup and resilience, even though published summaries characterize normal energy supply as fully solar.

The installation reduced dependence on seaborne diesel deliveries and the outages or rationing that could follow delayed shipments. This remote-island logistics benefit is separate from the exact, unaudited gallons-per-year claim.

ASPA's 2024 generator procurement and its 2025 description of a 'solar and diesel hybrid system' are authoritative evidence that Ta'u is not physically solar-only. The newer generators may be backup/contingency assets; the audits do not report their annual runtime.
ASPA's fiscal-2025 audit identifies three land leases dating from October 2015 for the solar-and-diesel hybrid system, with terms running through September 30, 2045.
Contemporary local reporting says SolarCity employed and trained approximately 15 local workers to help construct the array, an important implementation detail but not a continuing staffing count.

No public Ta'u-specific series was found for hourly renewable share, annual diesel runtime and fuel, battery state of health, forced outages, replacement modules, or 2025/26 energy served. Current agency summaries substantiate continued relevance, but not component-level performance.

tanjung-batu-laut.summary: The best-supported project is the paired Kampung Tanjung Batu Darat and Tanjung Batu Laut solar-battery-diesel system near Sandakan/Kinabatangan, Sabah—not confidently the same-named Tawau locality in the archived record. It was delivered under Malaysia's rural electrification program around 2010 for approximately 127-128 households. A later engineering table reports 200 kWp PV, approximately 675 kWh of batteries, and two diesel sets rated 270 and 350 kW, while Optimal Power Solutions' announcement gives only aggregate power-conversion figures across three separate Sabah sites. The record must avoid assigning those portfolio totals to Tanjung Batu Laut alone.
tanjung-batu-laut.status: Contemporary 2011-2012 reporting describes the system operating successfully and converting the two villages from six-to-eight-hour household diesel service to continuous electricity. A 2015 risk-management study still treated it as an operating utility PV-battery-diesel case. Current Malaysian records confirm a Kampung Tanjung Batu Laut in Sandakan, but no credible post-2015 site-specific output, maintenance, battery replacement, or outage report was found. Current operation in July 2026 is therefore unverified, and the archived Tawau location should be marked as disputed rather than silently retained.
tanjung-batu-laut.historicalLocation: Tanjung Batu Laut Tawau Sabah Malaysia

organizations.name: Kampung Tanjung Batu Darat and Kampung Tanjung Batu Laut communities
organizations.role: Beneficiary villages, reported as approximately 127-128 connected houses in contemporary/project-study sources.

organizations.name: Ministry of Rural and Regional Development Malaysia (KKLW)
organizations.role: Government sponsor/procuring authority under the Bekalan Elektrik Luar Bandar rural electrification program.

organizations.name: One Alternate Energy Sdn. Bhd.
organizations.role: Lists completion of solar-hybrid system management work for four Kinabatangan villages in September 2010 with SESB as end user; the public milestone does not itemize its exact Batu Laut equipment scope.

organizations.name: Optimal Power Solutions
organizations.role: Publicly named participant; role not specified in the recovered record

organizations.name: Optimal Power Solutions Pty Ltd.
organizations.role: Power-conditioning, control, system-integration, and remote-monitoring supplier across the Sabah project package that included Batu Darat/Laut.

organizations.name: Sabah Electricity Sdn. Bhd. (SESB)
organizations.role: Utility/end user associated with the rural hybrid project and responsible for Sabah electricity service; contemporary sources describe the solar hybrid as supplying the paired villages.

specifications.label: Storage
specifications.value: NA

specifications.label: Photovoltaic capacity
specifications.value: A 2015 engineering table reports 200 kWp for the combined Kampung Tanjung Batu Darat and Laut project. This is the strongest located site-specific rating.

specifications.label: Battery capacity
specifications.value: Approximately 675 kWh in the 2015 project table. Contemporary reporting identifies large lead-acid storage but does not provide maker, model, usable capacity, or replacement history.

specifications.label: Diesel generation
specifications.value: The 2015 table reports one 270 kW and one 350 kW generator (620 kW aggregate). Generator manufacturer/model and whether both ratings are prime or standby were not found.

specifications.label: Connections
specifications.value: Approximately 127-128 houses across the paired villages. An APEC case narrative gives roughly 200 people; the differing household/person figures may reflect partial or approximate counts and should not be forced into a household-size calculation.

specifications.label: Service improvement
specifications.value: Continuous/24-hour supply replaced individual diesel generators that had generally run about six to eight hours per day.

specifications.label: Portfolio-level OPS package
specifications.value: Across three Sabah locations—not Batu Laut alone—OPS announced 430 kW of PV, at least 50% expected fuel reduction, and 710 kW of proprietary inverter capacity: two 250 kVA hybrid power conditioners, two 30 kVA hybrid power conditioners, and four 100 kVA grid-export conditioners.

specifications.label: Location
specifications.value: Official 2024 spatial records list Kampung Tanjung Batu Laut in Sandakan, matching the engineering literature's Sandakan/Kinabatangan project. Malaysia's geographic gazette also lists a separate same-named locality in Tawau, explaining but not validating the archived location.

equipment.detail: OPS proprietary hybrid power conditioners and grid-export conditioners were supplied across the three-site Sabah package. The public announcement does not map a particular inverter count/model to Batu Darat/Laut, so the 710 kW portfolio rating is not assigned to this plant.
Approximately 200 kWp site-specific array. No reliable module manufacturer/model was found.
Large lead-acid battery bank reported at approximately 675 kWh. Chemistry is described in the APEC case narrative, while exact cell and inverter specifications remain undisclosed.
One approximately 270 kW and one approximately 350 kW unit in the engineering table. No supportable maker/model attribution was located.
Computerized solar/battery/diesel dispatch and remote monitoring supplied as part of the integration package.

technicalDetails.detail: PV supplies daytime load and charges the battery; computerized controls start diesel generation when solar and stored energy are insufficient, enabling continuous power without running village diesel sets continuously.

Contemporaneous and engineering sources place the paired Batu Darat/Laut project in Sandakan/Kinabatangan. Because a distinct Kampung Tanjung Batu Laut also exists in Tawau, the legacy 'Tawau' label is ambiguous and should not be treated as confirmed project geolocation.
OPS's 430 kW solar and 710 kW inverter figures cover Batu Darat/Laut, Kuamut, and Kuamut Seberang collectively. Allocating the full package to this record would materially overstate its equipment.

Reporting after commissioning described 24-hour household electricity, better lighting and refrigeration, and reduced reliance on individual gensets. These are qualitative early outcomes rather than audited lifetime performance.

No credible source located after the 2015 engineering study reports site-specific energy production, diesel savings, battery replacement, outages, customer count, or whether the original controls remain in service. Present operation is therefore unresolved.

tanjung-labian.summary: Tanjung Labian is a multi-phase rural solar-battery-diesel system in Sabah's Lahad Datu/Tungku area, not merely the rounded 1.2 MW solar/1.2 MW diesel entry in the archive. The best detailed phase-1 vendor source reports 1,212 kWp PV, 4,320 kWh of batteries, 1,650 kW of combined inverter capacity, and 1,250 kW of diesel. A separate engineering table reports the diesel arrangement as two 500 kW plus one 360 kW set (1.36 MW), an unresolved source conflict. Later extensions added villages and capacity, so phase-1 ratings should not be assumed to describe the entire present service area.
tanjung-labian.status: The initial phase was commissioned around November 2012 and the rural-electrification contractor lists completion in April 2013, with three additional villages completed in June 2014. A 2015 field study found strong renewable penetration but also maintenance and remote-monitoring weaknesses. Sabah's current Energy Roadmap lists Labian among the state's solar-hybrid stations, while TNB/SESB says its portfolio of 35 solar-hybrid stations operates 24/7 and illustrates Tanjung Labian. These sources support continued inclusion in the operating portfolio, but no plant-specific 2025/26 generation, diesel burn, battery health, or availability series was located.

organizations.name: Energy Commission of Sabah (ECoS)
organizations.role: Current state energy regulator/planning body whose Sabah Energy Roadmap lists Labian among off-grid solar-hybrid stations.

organizations.name: Leonics
organizations.role: Publicly named participant; role not specified in the recovered record

organizations.name: Leonics Co., Ltd.
organizations.role: Supplier of inverter, power-conversion, and microgrid-control technology; its technical materials provide the most detailed phase-1 ratings.

organizations.name: Ministry of Rural and Regional Development Malaysia (KKLW)
organizations.role: Government sponsor/procuring authority under the Bekalan Elektrik Luar Bandar rural electrification program.

organizations.name: One Alternate Energy Sdn. Bhd.
organizations.role: Lists completion of the Tanjung Labian BELB solar-hybrid project in April 2013 and an extension to three more villages in June 2014.

organizations.name: Sabah Electricity Sdn. Bhd. (SESB)
organizations.role: Utility/end user and operating organization for the Tanjung Labian solar-hybrid station and its village distribution service.

specifications.label: Storage
specifications.value: 4320 kWh

specifications.label: Phase-1 photovoltaic array
specifications.value: 1,212 kWp. Leonics gives this exact rating; the legacy 1,200 kW is a reasonable rounded value but should not replace the documented number.

specifications.label: Phase-1 battery storage
specifications.value: 4,320 kWh nominal. Neither the accessible vendor deck nor the field study provides a dependable battery-cell manufacturer/model, usable energy, depth of discharge, or current state of health.

specifications.label: Phase-1 inverter capacity
specifications.value: 1,650 kW combined inverter capacity. A commissioning presentation further divides this into approximately 900 kW of bidirectional battery inverters and 750 kW of grid-connected PV inverters.

specifications.label: Phase-1 diesel capacity
specifications.value: Conflicting published values: Leonics reports 1,250 kW total, while a 2015 engineering table identifies two 500 kW plus one 360 kW set, totaling 1,360 kW. The archive's 1,200 kW is rounded and does not resolve the discrepancy.

specifications.label: Phase-1 customers
specifications.value: The engineering table reports 681 connected households. Leonics marketing describes approximately 700 families across seven villages plus a school, clinic, and government facilities; those denominators are close but not identical.

specifications.label: Distribution network
specifications.value: Leonics commissioning material describes approximately 14 km of local distribution serving multiple villages and community facilities.

specifications.label: Reported solar generation
specifications.value: Leonics projected or reported approximately 1.82 GWh/year from the PV plant. The source is a supplier presentation, and no current meter series was found to independently validate the figure.

specifications.label: Extension phases
specifications.value: Public project records show a later three-village extension completed in June 2014. The 2015 study separately tabulates a phase 2 and a Sungai Merah/Tanjung Labian installation, but source-table formatting and overlapping place names make a single safe present-day aggregate impossible.

equipment.detail: Approximately 900 kW of bidirectional battery-inverter capacity in the vendor's phase-1 architecture; accessible material does not provide a model number.
Approximately 750 kW of grid-connected inverter capacity, supplemented by MPPT conversion for part of the array in an AC/DC-coupled architecture.
Phase-1 array totaling 1,212 kWp. Module manufacturer, model, string layout, and current degraded capacity were not found.
Phase-1 bank totaling 4,320 kWh nominal. No reliable public cell chemistry, manufacturer, model, or replacement record was located.
Published phase-1 totals conflict between 1.25 MW and an itemized 1.36 MW (2 x 500 kW plus 1 x 360 kW). No supportable maker/model attribution was found.
Supervisory controls coordinate AC-coupled PV, DC/MPPT-coupled PV, bidirectional battery inverters, village load, and diesel backup.

technicalDetails.detail: The vendor architecture combines AC-coupled PV inverters with battery bidirectional inverters and additional DC/MPPT-coupled PV, allowing storage and diesel generation to form and balance the isolated village grid.

The 2015 study calculated 85.4% renewable penetration over an 11-month period for phase 1, above a 70% design target. The authors caution that conflict-related displacement depressed local load, so the result should not be generalized as normal long-run performance.

Across the Sabah study sites, approximately 85% of users reported satisfaction and significant outages were generally less frequent than once per week. These are multi-site survey results, not a Tanjung Labian-only audited service-level metric.

The field study found no permanently based technical staff at Tanjung Labian; technicians traveled from Lahad Datu, increasing response time. Satellite/web monitoring was unavailable at visited sites and operating-data gaps limited performance management.

ECoS lists Labian as an off-grid solar-hybrid station, and TNB/SESB states that 35 solar-hybrid stations provide 24/7 service while illustrating Tanjung Labian. This is persuasive portfolio-level continuity evidence but not a plant availability report.

No public plant-specific 2025/26 records were found for annual PV output, diesel fuel, battery capacity, inverter availability, outages, customer growth, or replacement capital. Phase ratings should not be represented as a verified current aggregate.

taykwa-tagamou-nation.summary: Taykwa Tagamou Nation's project progressed beyond the archived planning status into a community-wide rollout of grid-connected distributed solar, initially on public buildings and then on residences. The clearest exact pilot is a 50 kW ground-mounted array plus 50 kWh battery at the Community Complex. Developer JAZZ Solar describes a broader 2021 installation as 250 kW solar plus storage and markets the Nation as '100% solarized,' while 2024 community reporting says each participating home has roughly 8-9 kW. Those claims do not form a reconciled as-built inventory and do not mean the community is off-grid or meets 100% of annual load from solar.
taykwa-tagamou-nation.status: Phase 1 on community buildings was reported complete and residential phase 2 underway in 2021; by 2024 community reporting described solar on every home and substantial bill savings. Taykwa Tagamou's own current corporations page describes community-wide solar microgrids managed through its energy enterprise. A February 2026 RFP for load, distribution, and Hydro One connection studies confirms continued grid-connected energy planning and expansion. No consolidated 2025/26 meter, storage, outage, or installed-capacity inventory was found, so the correct status is installed and operating as distributed grid-connected systems, with aggregate performance not independently verified.
taykwa-tagamou-nation.historicalLocation: Taykwa Tagamou Nation, Cochrane, ON, Canada

organizations.name: Government of Canada
organizations.role: Provided C$1.36 million through the Small Communities Fund for the rollout and later more than C$1 million shared with Oneida Nation for distributed-energy training and capacity building.

organizations.name: Hydro One
organizations.role: Utility coordination counterparty for current distribution planning

organizations.name: Hydro One Networks Inc.
organizations.role: Grid/distribution connection counterpart referenced in the Nation's 2026 engineering RFP; it is not identified as owner of the behind-the-meter solar assets.

organizations.name: Independent Electricity System Operator (IESO)
organizations.role: Funded the 50 kW/50 kWh Community Complex pilot and later training, virtual-power-plant feasibility, EV-charging, and energy-as-a-service work through Indigenous energy programs.

organizations.name: JAZZ Solar Solutions Inc.
organizations.role: Developer/EPC and long-term community-energy partner for the solar-and-storage rollout; publishes the 250 kW portfolio claim.

organizations.name: Natural Resources Canada
organizations.role: Program administrator and contribution funder

organizations.name: Ontario Energy Board
organizations.role: Provided approximately C$351,000 through its Innovation Sandbox/Indigenous energy-support work for energy-system research and development.

organizations.name: Takwata Spring Energy Limited Partnership
organizations.role: Taykwa Tagamou-owned community micro-utility/energy enterprise associated with the Nation's solar, efficiency, and broader clean-energy work.

organizations.name: Taykwa Tagamou Nation
organizations.role: Project owner/host and beneficiary; led community energy planning, public-building and residential deployment, and current grid-capacity planning.

specifications.label: Federal contribution
specifications.value: C$1,127,600 capacity-building agreement, 2022–2027

specifications.label: Community Complex pilot
specifications.value: 50 kW ground-mounted solar plus 50 kWh battery storage. This IESO record is the strongest exact project-component rating.

specifications.label: Developer portfolio claim
specifications.value: JAZZ Solar lists a 2021 Taykwa Tagamou installation at 250 kW solar plus battery. Its website does not disclose battery kW/kWh, site breakdown, commissioning test, or whether 250 kW includes the 50 kW IESO pilot.

specifications.label: Residential systems
specifications.value: Community reporting in 2024 says every home received approximately 8-9 kW of rooftop solar. An earlier report described nearly three dozen households in phase 2; no final as-built home count and nameplate rollup was located.

specifications.label: Phase-1 sites
specifications.value: Reported sites included the public-works garage, Community Complex, water-treatment plant, Lands and Resources building, and Healthy Babies building, with solar and storage included in the community-building phase.

specifications.label: Project cost and grants
specifications.value: Contemporary reporting gives an approximately C$2.04 million rollout, including C$1.36 million federal Small Communities Fund support and C$200,000 from IESO. Later capacity-building awards are separate and should not be added as construction cost without project accounting.

specifications.label: Lifetime savings forecast
specifications.value: The project team forecast 16.5 GWh of generation and approximately C$4 million in electricity-bill savings over 30 years. These are projections, not realized lifetime results.

specifications.label: Reported household savings
specifications.value: A 2024 community article reported roughly 40-50% bill reductions and as much as 70% in summer for homes. This is self-reported outcome evidence, not a utility-billing audit.

specifications.label: NRCan 1 MW / 38 kWh statement
specifications.value: A 2023 federal release quotes JAZZ describing 'solar (1 MW) and storage (38 kWh)' deployed through pandemic-era community projects. The wording covers Taykwa Tagamou and Oneida work collectively and conflicts with site-specific figures, so 1 MW is not assigned to Taykwa Tagamou alone.

equipment.detail: 50 kW ground-mounted PV system. Public funding records do not identify module or inverter manufacturers/models.
50 kWh battery system. Chemistry, inverter power, manufacturer, model, usable capacity, and present state of health were not located.
Approximately 8-9 kW per participating home in 2024 community reporting. No verified module/inverter bill of materials or consolidated system count was found.

Distributed arrays and batteries on several Nation facilities. JAZZ is identified as the delivery partner, but accessible sources do not support attributing the underlying hardware manufacturer or model to JAZZ.

technicalDetails.detail: Evidence supports multiple behind-the-meter/grid-connected DER systems spread across buildings and homes. No source establishes an islandable community feeder, black-start capability, or a single central controller; 'microgrid' here is a program label, not proof of whole-community islanding.

JAZZ's '100% solarized community' language means the rollout reached infrastructure buildings and homes. It does not establish 100% annual renewable-energy share, off-grid operation, or zero Hydro One purchases.

IESO funded feasibility, training, and business-model work for residential solar aggregation, EV chargers, and an energy-as-a-service/virtual-power-plant approach. These activities are development work, not proof that a fully dispatchable VPP is commercially operating.

The rollout was structured to reduce household and Nation electricity bills and build Indigenous ownership and technical capacity. Reported household savings are substantial, but a consolidated audited savings dataset was not found.

The Nation's February 2026 RFP seeks load assessment, distribution-capacity review, and Hydro One connection analysis. It is evidence of active expansion/planning, not evidence that existing assets failed.

No authoritative consolidated as-built inventory, battery kW/MWh total, annual production, outage/islanding record, curtailment series, or 2025/26 savings audit was located. Exact pilot values and clearly labeled developer/community claims should remain separate.

tecnalia-microgrid.summary: TECNALIA's microgrid is an active research and test laboratory, not a community power project with a fixed generation mix. The current Smart Grids and Distributed Generation Laboratory is in Derio, Bizkaia, and can be reconfigured around grid emulators, PV and wind generation, grid-forming/following converters, batteries, ultracapacitors, a 250 kW flywheel, controllable loads, and power-hardware-in-the-loop simulation. The archived Azpeitia location refers to TECNALIA's separate thermal laboratory and earlier linked research assets. Likewise, the legacy '160 kW diesel' appears to combine two historical 55 kW diesel generators with a separate 50 kW microturbine and should be retired.
tecnalia-microgrid.status: TECNALIA's official 2026 infrastructure page presents the Derio laboratory as current and available for testing. The 2020 ERIGrid campaign documents grid-forming controls and load-shedding tests, and the 2020-2022 ENERISLA program connected the Derio electrical laboratory with TECNALIA's Azpeitia thermal laboratory and CIRCE facilities for remote multi-energy testing. Because the laboratory is deliberately reconfigurable, a single operational capacity or energy mix is not meaningful. No source indicates closure; current status is active R&D infrastructure.

specifications.value: NA

specifications.label: Grid emulation
specifications.value: Three 50 kW grid emulators, 150 kW aggregate, support programmable grid conditions. A line emulator and flexible 150 kW RLC load allow topology and disturbance testing.
